| **Lightweight (LWT)** | The weight of the completely empty ship, including hull structure (steel), machinery, outfit, and equipment, but excluding all variable loads. | 1 |
| **Deadweight (DWT)** | The total weight of cargo, fuel, lubricating oil, fresh water, stores, crew and effects that the ship can carry when loaded to its designed draft. | 1 |
| **Displacement ($\Delta$)** | The total weight of water displaced by the ship's underwater hull, equal to the actual weight of the ship at any given loading condition. | 0.5 |
| **Relationship** | $\Delta = LWT + DWT$ | 0.5 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
State **Watson's formula** for estimating the steel weight of a ship. What are the units of the dimensions in the formula?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Watson's formula for estimating the **steel weight (hull weight)** of a ship is:

$$W_S = 0.064 \cdot L \cdot (B + D) \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot D}$$

**Units:**
- $W_S$ → **Long Tons**
- $L$, $B$, $D$ → **Feet**

For metric conversion to tonnes and metres, the coefficient must be adjusted to approximately **0.0344**.

Watson also provided analogous formulas for outfit weight ($W_O = 0.026 \cdot \ldots$) and machinery weight ($W_M = 0.024 \cdot \ldots$) using the same dimensional product. **(3 Marks: 1 for formula, 1 for units, 1 for context)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **classification of ship weights** into Lightweight and Deadweight categories. List all the major sub-groups within each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small inland cargo vessel has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars $L = 90$ m
- Breadth $B = 14$ m
- Depth $D = 6$ m
- Draft $T = 4$ m
- Block coefficient $C_B = 0.72$

Estimate the **(i) Displacement**, **(ii) Lightweight using Watson's formula** (use metric equivalent), and **(iii) Deadweight** of the vessel. Take seawater density = 1.025 t/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Classification of Ship Weights:**

The total displacement ($\Delta$) of a ship is divided into two broad categories:

**1. Lightweight (LWT) — weight of the empty ship:**

| Sub-group | Examples | Contribution |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Steel Weight ($W_S$) | Hull plating, frames, girders, bulkheads, decks, superstructure, stern frame, rudder | 60–70% of LWT |
| Outfit Weight ($W_O$) | Anchor, chain, cables, deck machinery, winches, lifeboats, paint, insulation, joinery, M&E equipment | 20–25% of LWT |
| Machinery Weight ($W_M$) | Main engine, generators, boilers, pumps, compressors, propelling machinery, shafting | 10–15% of LWT |

$$LWT = W_S + W_O + W_M$$

**2. Deadweight (DWT) — weight of all variable loads:**

| Sub-group | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| Cargo | The paying load (e.g., containers, grain, oil) |
| Fuel Oil (FO) | For main propulsion boilers/engine |
| Diesel Oil (DO) | For auxiliary generators |
| Lubricating Oil (LO) | For machinery lubrication |
| Fresh Water (FW) | Potable water for crew |
| Stores | Food, spares, consumables |
| Crew & Effects | Personnel + luggage |
| Miscellaneous | Passengers (in ferries), mail |

$$DWT = W_{cargo} + W_{FO} + W_{DO} + W_{LO} + W_{FW} + W_{stores} + W_{crew} + W_{misc}$$

**Master Equation:**

$$\Delta = LWT + DWT$$

**(Marks: 2 for LWT sub-groups with table, 2 for DWT sub-groups with table, 1 for master equation, 2 for clear schematic explanation — sketch optional.)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
$L = 90$ m, $B = 14$ m, $D = 6$ m, $T = 4$ m, $C_B = 0.72$, $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³

**(i) Displacement:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 0.72 \times 90 \times 14 \times 4$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 0.72 \times 5040$$

$$\Delta = 3{,}719.76 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**(ii) Lightweight (Watson's metric equivalent):**

Base dimensional product:

$$L \cdot (B + D) \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot D} = 90 \times (14 + 6) \times \sqrt{90 \times 6}$$

$$= 90 \times 20 \times \sqrt{540}$$

$$= 90 \times 20 \times 23.2379$$

$$= 41{,}828.2$$

Steel weight ($W_S = 0.0344 \cdot \text{product}$):

$$W_S = 0.0344 \times 41{,}828.2 = 1{,}438.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

Outfit weight ($W_O = 0.0140 \cdot \text{product}$):

$$W_O = 0.0140 \times 41{,}828.2 = 585.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

Machinery weight ($W_M = 0.0129 \cdot \text{product}$):

$$W_M = 0.0129 \times 41{,}828.2 = 539.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Total Lightweight:**

$$LWT = 1438.9 + 585.6 + 539.6 = 2{,}564.1 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(3 Marks)**

**(iii) Deadweight:**

$$DWT = \Delta - LWT = 3719.76 - 2564.1 = 1{,}155.66 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Deadweight Ratio:**

$$DWR = \frac{DWT}{\Delta} = \frac{1155.66}{3719.76} = 0.311$$

**(2 Marks — 1 for DWT value, 1 for DWR interpretation)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using Watson's imperial coefficient (0.064) directly with metric dimensions without conversion. Result: ~4× the correct value.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to state the formula being used and the units assumed.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Not verifying that $\Delta > LWT$ (i.e., that the estimate is physically realistic). Here $DWR = 0.311$ is sensible for a small coastal vessel.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Failing to convert $C_B$ into a correct volume; some students confuse $C_B$ with $C_P$ or $C_M$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **displacement of a ship** from Archimedes' principle. How does the **block coefficient** modify this expression? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An inland tanker barge has the following particulars:
- $L = 80$ m, $B = 12$ m, $D = 5$ m, $T = 3.5$ m, $C_B = 0.82$

Estimate the **lightweight** using the **Mitsubishi formula** and verify whether a DWT of 2000 tonnes is feasible. Assume Mitsubishi constant $k_S = 0.052$ (combined for steel + outfit + machinery in metric form) and $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- Archimedes' Principle derivation: buoyancy = weight of displaced fluid (3 steps).
- Introduction of $C_B$: physical meaning (1 mark).
- Final expression with all terms defined (2 marks).
- One numerical example to anchor understanding (1 mark).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

- Compute displacement $\Delta = 1.025 \times 0.82 \times 80 \times 12 \times 3.5 = 2{,}821.66$ t (2 marks).
- Apply Mitsubishi: $W_L = 0.052 \times L \times B \times D = 0.052 \times 80 \times 12 \times 5 = 249.6$ t (3 marks).
- Compute DWT = $\Delta - LWT = 2821.66 - 249.6 = 2572.06$ t (1 mark).
- Verify: DWT required = 2000 t ≤ 2572 t → **Feasible** (1 mark).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weight Estimation (KTU Module 2)**

- [ ] **Three Master Definitions:** Lightweight, Deadweight, Displacement — and the master equation $\Delta = LWT + DWT$.
- [ ] **Archimedes' Principle** is the foundation: $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$.
- [ ] **Block Coefficient** $C_B$ converts a rectangular volume into the true underwater hull volume.
- [ ] **Watson's Three Formulas** (Steel, Outfit, Machinery) use the same base term $L \cdot (B+D) \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot D}$ with coefficients 0.064, 0.026, 0.024 (Long Tons + Feet).
- [ ] **Metric Conversion** for Watson: divide the imperial result by ~15.94, or use the metric-equivalent coefficients (0.0344, 0.0140, 0.0129).
- [ ] **Mitsubishi Formula** $W_S = k_S \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$ is preferred for tankers and bulkers.
- [ ] **Gilfillan** relates steel weight to displacement: $W_S = K_G \cdot \Delta^{2/3}$.
- [ ] **DWR (Deadweight Ratio)** = $DWT/\Delta$. Typical range: 0.60–0.80 for ocean-going merchant ships; 0.25–0.40 for small inland vessels.
- [ ] **Seawater density** = **1.025 t/m³**; freshwater = 1.000 t/m³. **Always state which is used.**
- [ ] **Typical $C_B$ values:** Tanker ≈ 0.80–0.85; Bulker ≈ 0.75–0.82; Container ≈ 0.60–0.70; Naval ≈ 0.55–0.65.
- [ ] **Estimation is iterative:** weight feeds back into displacement, which feeds back into draft, which may force dimensional revisions.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** Always check that $W_L < \Delta$ after computation. A negative DWT is non-physical and indicates either wrong formula coefficients or wrong units.
- [ ] **KTU Exam Tip:** Carry a small **formula sheet** with the Watson / Mitsubishi equations and their metric coefficients; examiners award 1–2 marks just for correctly stating the formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of a Floating Vessel (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> Stability of a vessel is defined as the ability of the vessel to return to its original upright position (equilibrium) after being subjected to an external heeling moment caused by wind, waves, cargo shift, or turning forces. In inland water transportation, the stability of barges, tugboats, and cargo vessels is governed primarily by the position of the **Metacentre (M)** relative to the **Centre of Gravity (G)**.

For any floating body, three conditions of equilibrium exist:

1. **Stable Equilibrium:** The metacentre M lies above the centre of gravity G (i.e., **GM > 0**). The vessel returns to upright when disturbed.
2. **Neutral Equilibrium:** The metacentre M coincides with the centre of gravity G (i.e., **GM = 0**). The vessel remains in the new tilted position.
3. **Unstable Equilibrium:** The metacentre M lies below the centre of gravity G (i.e., **GM < 0**). The vessel continues to capsize.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Module 2 Vocabulary):**
> - **G (Centre of Gravity):** Point through which the total weight of the vessel acts vertically downward.
> - **B (Centre of Buoyancy):** Centroid of the displaced volume of water; total buoyancy acts vertically upward through this point.
> - **M (Metacentre):** Intersection point of the vertical line through the *new* centre of buoyancy (B₁) with the original vertical centreline when the vessel is slightly inclined.
> - **GM (Metacentric Height):** The vertical distance between G and M — the master indicator of initial transverse stability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine pushing a tall, narrow glass (like a champagne flute) versus a short, wide tumbler on a table.

- The **wide tumbler** rocks a little and springs back upright. Its "stability" is high because it has a **broad base** relative to its height.
- The **narrow flute** tips over easily. Its stability is poor because it is top-heavy.

In a vessel, the same principle applies: a **low centre of gravity (G)** and a **broad waterplane area** produce a high metacentric height (GM). A barge is a textbook example of a stable inland waterway vessel — flat-bottomed, wide, and shallow-drafted, giving it a large **B²/T ratio** and therefore excellent initial stability.

## 1.3 Physical Constants (Bold-Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants Used in KTU Stability Calculations:**
> - **ρ (fresh water) = 1000 kg/m³**
> - **ρ (sea water) = 1025 kg/m³**
> - **g (acceleration due to gravity) = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **1 tonne = 1000 kg = 9.81 kN (weight)**

## 1.4 Why Stability Matters in Inland Water Transportation

Inland barges operate in rivers, canals, and shallow waterways where they frequently experience:
- **Wind heeling** on wide river stretches
- **Turning moments** during navigation bends
- **Asymmetric loading** at cargo terminals
- **Free-surface liquid** in partially filled ballast or fuel tanks

A vessel with **insufficient GM** will list dangerously or capsize. A vessel with **excessive GM** will have a "stiff" ride, causing violent rolling that can damage cargo and stress the hull. The goal is an **optimal GM** (typically **0.15 m to 1.5 m** for inland vessels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometry of a Slightly Inclined Vessel

When a vessel heels (rolls sideways) by a small angle **θ**:
- The original centre of buoyancy **B** shifts to a new position **B₁**.
- The vertical line through **B₁** intersects the original vertical centreline at point **M** (the metacentre).
- The line **GZ** drawn from G to the new buoyancy vertical is called the **righting lever (GZ)**.
- The product **W × GZ** gives the **Righting Moment (RM)**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Stability Calculation

The determination of GM proceeds in the following logical sequence:

- **Step 1 — Compute Displaced Volume (V):**
  For a rectangular barge: **V = L × B × T**

- **Step 2 — Locate the Centre of Buoyancy (KB):**
  For a rectangular barge: **KB = T/2**
  For ship-shaped vessels, KB is obtained from hydrostatic curves.

- **Step 3 — Compute Second Moment of Waterplane Area (I_T):**
  For a rectangular waterplane: **I_T = L × B³ / 12**

- **Step 4 — Compute BM:**
  **BM = I_T / V**

- **Step 5 — Compute KM:**
  **KM = KB + BM**

- **Step 6 — Compute GM:**
  **GM = KM − KG**

- **Step 7 — Verify Sign of GM:**
  GM > 0 → Stable | GM = 0 → Neutral | GM < 0 → Unstable

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the complete formula bank for Module 2 — Stability. Memorise these for the KTU board exam.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Displaced Volume (rectangular barge) | V = L × B × T | Volume of water displaced |
| 2 | Displacement (weight) | W = V × ρ × g | Total weight of vessel |
| 3 | Centre of Buoyancy (rectangular) | KB = T/2 | Height of B above keel |
| 4 | Second Moment of Waterplane (rectangular) | I_T = L × B³ / 12 | Athwartships moment of inertia |
| 5 | BM (Transverse) | BM = I_T / V | Distance B to M |
| 6 | KM | KM = KB + BM | Distance keel to M |
| 7 | GM (Initial Transverse) | GM = KM − KG | Master stability index |
| 8 | Righting Lever (small angles) | GZ = GM × sin(θ) | For θ ≤ ~10° |
| 9 | Righting Lever (large angles) | GZ = KN − KG × sin(θ) | From cross curves of stability |
| 10 | Righting Moment | RM = W × GZ | Restoring moment |
| 11 | Free Surface Correction | ΔGM = i_L / V | Loss of GM due to slack tanks |
| 12 | Shift of G due to load | KG_new = (W×KG + w×h) / (W+w) | Parallel-axis moment principle |
| 13 | Angle of Loll | tan(θ) = GG′ / GM | Heel angle when GM goes negative |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are used daily by naval architects and marine surveyors worldwide. Specifically:
- **Barge Design:** Determining safe loading limits on river cargo barges.
- **Classification Societies:** Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) and Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) mandate minimum GM values.
- **Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI):** Uses stability checks before issuing movement clearances for heavy cargo on National Waterways (NW-1, NW-2, etc.).
- **Accident Investigation:** Post-capsize analyses always reconstruct GM using the formulas above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BM = I_T / V (The Master Transverse Stability Formula)

**Geometric Setup:**
Consider a vessel of displaced volume **V** floating in still water. The waterplane area is **A_wp** with second moment of area **I_T** about the longitudinal centreline. The vessel is given a small heel angle **θ** (in radians, very small).

**Step 1 — Define the Heeling Action:**
On heeling, one side submerges by a small wedge while the other side emerges by an equal volume wedge. Let **dV** be an elemental volume of the wedge at transverse distance **y** from the centreline.

The vertical rise of any point at distance y on the waterplane during heel = **y × tan(θ) ≈ y × θ** (for very small θ).

**Step 2 — Elemental Wedge Volume:**
The elemental volume of the immersed/emerged wedge is:
$$dV = y \cdot \theta \cdot dA$$

**Step 3 — Total Wedge Volume Check (sanity verification):**
$$\int dV = \theta \cdot \int y \, dA = 0$$

This is zero because the waterplane is symmetric about the centreline (definition of the centreline), confirming the vessel neither gains nor loses total displacement at small heel.

**Step 4 — Moment of the Wedges About the Original Centreline:**
The horizontal shift of the centre of buoyancy, call it **BB₁**, must satisfy the moment balance of these wedges about the original centreline:

$$V \cdot BB_1 = \int y \cdot dV = \int y \cdot (y \cdot \theta) \, dA = \theta \cdot \int y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Second Moment of Area:**
By definition, the transverse second moment of area of the waterplane about its centreline is:
$$I_T = \int y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify:**
$$V \cdot BB_1 = \theta \cdot I_T$$
$$BB_1 = \frac{\theta \cdot I_T}{V}$$

**Step 7 — Relate BB₁ to BM Geometrically:**
From the geometry of the heel, the line joining B to B₁ is approximately perpendicular to the heeled waterline. For small angles:
$$BB_1 = BM \cdot \tan(\theta) \approx BM \cdot \theta$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**
Equating the two expressions for BB₁:
$$BM \cdot \theta = \frac{\theta \cdot I_T}{V}$$

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I_T}{V}}$$

This is the **foundation formula** of transverse initial stability. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Righting Lever GZ for Small Angles (sin(θ) Approximation)

**Setup:** Consider the vessel heeled by angle θ. The metacentre M is fixed for small angles. The righting lever GZ is the horizontal distance from G to the vertical through B₁.

From triangle GMZ (right-angled at the foot of perpendicular from G):

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

**Justification:** This follows directly from the geometric definition of M as the point about which the vessel rotates at small heel. The arc traced by G lies on a circle of radius GM centred at M.

**Validity:** This is accurate for **θ ≤ 10°**. Beyond this, the metacentre M itself shifts, and we must use the **KN cross-curve method**:
$$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin(\theta)$$
where KN is read off the cross curves of stability for the given displacement and heel angle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Free-Surface Effect (Loss of GM)

**Setup:** A compartment is partially filled with liquid (e.g., partially full ballast tank) having a free surface. When the vessel rolls, the liquid sloshes, shifting its centre of mass and creating an **effective shift of G** as if the liquid were a "virtual rise of G."

**The Free-Surface Correction Formula:**
$$\Delta GM = \frac{i_L \cdot \rho_{liquid}}{W}$$

where:
- **i_L** = transverse second moment of area of the free surface of the liquid
- **ρ_liquid** = density of the liquid
- **W** = displacement of the vessel

For a rectangular free surface of length l and breadth b:
$$i_L = \frac{l \cdot b^3}{12}$$

**Effective GM with free surface:**
$$GM_{eff} = GM - \Delta GM = GM - \frac{i_L \cdot \rho_{liquid}}{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The free-surface effect can completely destroy stability. A vessel that appears stable (GM > 0) on paper may become unstable once partially filled tanks are considered. This is a frequent KTU problem scenario.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operations on GM

| Operation | Effect on G | Effect on M | Net Effect on GM |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| Adding weight **below** G | G moves down | No change | **GM increases** ↑ |
| Adding weight **above** G | G moves up | No change | **GM decreases** ↓ |
| Suspending weight from hook | G moves down | No change | **GM increases** ↑ |
| Moving weight transversely | G shifts sideways | No change | Creates **list** |
| Free surface in tank | Virtual G rises | No change | **GM decreases** ↓ |
| Increasing beam (B) | No change | BM increases | **GM increases** ↑ |
| Increasing draft (T) | KB rises | BM decreases | Complex — see below |
| Offloading from above G | G drops | M may shift | **GM increases** ↑ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Draft vs. Beam Trade-off (Critical for Barge Design):**
> Increasing draft T raises KB and lowers BM, but for typical barge proportions (B² >> T), the BM loss dominates the KB gain, so **GM usually increases with draft** for a rectangular barge.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stability Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vessel Data L, B, T, KG] --> B[Compute Displacement V and W]
    B --> C[Compute I_T from Waterplane]
    C --> D[Compute BM = I_T / V]
    D --> E[Compute KB from Shape]
    E --> F[Compute KM = KB + BM]
    F --> G[Compute GM = KM minus KG]
    G --> H{GM Sign Check}
    H -->|GM greater than 0| I[Stable Equilibrium]
    H -->|GM equals 0| J[Neutral Equilibrium]
    H -->|GM less than 0| K[Unstable Equilibrium]
    I --> L[Check Free Surface Effect]
    L --> M[Compute Loss of GM]
    M --> N{GM_eff greater than 0}
    N -->|Yes| O[Vessel Remains Stable]
    N -->|No| P[Apply Ballast or Reload]
    K --> P
    J --> P
    P --> Q[Re-evaluate Stability]
    Q --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transverse Stability Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Vessel Upright] --> B[Apply Heel Angle theta]
    B --> C[Centre of Buoyancy Shifts B to B1]
    C --> D[Vertical Through B1 Intersects Centreline at M]
    D --> E[Metacentre M Located]
    E --> F[Righting Lever GZ Drawn]
    F --> G[Righting Moment W times GZ]
    G --> H{Vessel Returns Upright}
    H -->|GM positive| I[YES - Stable]
    H -->|GM negative| J[NO - Capsize]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Stability Assessment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Input Vessel Particulars] --> stepB[Hydrostatic Data Lookup]
    stepB --> stepC[Calculate KG from Load Plan]
    stepC --> stepD[Calculate KB from Draft]
    stepD --> stepE[Calculate BM from Waterplane]
    stepE --> stepF[Determine KM and GM]
    stepF --> stepG[Apply Free Surface Correction]
    stepG --> stepH[Apply Trim Correction if Required]
    stepH --> stepI[Compare GM_eff with Regulation Minimum]
    stepI --> stepJ{Meets Criteria}
    stepJ -->|Yes| stepK[Issue Stability Certificate]
    stepJ -->|No| stepL[Modify Loading Plan]
    stepL --> stepA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above block diagrams represent the **standard stability assessment workflow** prescribed by classification societies. For Module 2 of PENST413, the KTU board typically tests the stages from **stepA to stepF** with a numerical problem, and then conceptually tests stepG (free surface effect) in Part A questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define the terms: Centre of Buoyancy, Metacentre, and Metacentric Height. Explain how the position of the metacentre relative to the centre of gravity determines the condition of equilibrium of a floating body.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** The centroid of the volume of water displaced by the floating body. The total buoyant force acts vertically upward through this point. **(1 mark)**

- **Metacentre (M):** It is the point of intersection of the vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy (B₁) with the original vertical centreline of the vessel when it is given a small angle of heel. **(1 mark)**

- **Metacentric Height (GM):** It is the vertical distance between the centre of gravity (G) and the metacentre (M). It is the measure of initial transverse stability. **(0.5 mark)**

- **Conditions of Equilibrium:** When **M lies above G (GM > 0)**, the vessel is in **stable equilibrium** and returns upright after disturbance. When **M coincides with G (GM = 0)**, the vessel is in **neutral equilibrium** and remains in the inclined position. When **M lies below G (GM < 0)**, the vessel is in **unstable equilibrium** and will capsize. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is the free-surface effect? How does it reduce the metacentric height of a vessel?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **free-surface effect** is the phenomenon in which a partially filled tank containing liquid with a free surface (not pressed against the tank top) reduces the effective metacentric height of a vessel. When the vessel rolls, the liquid inside the tank shifts, and its centre of mass moves towards the low side. This effectively raises the virtual centre of gravity of the vessel. **(1.5 marks)**

The loss of GM due to the free-surface effect is given by the formula:
$$\Delta GM = \frac{i_L \cdot \rho_{liquid}}{W}$$

where **i_L** is the second moment of the free-surface area and **W** is the displacement. The corrected effective GM is:
$$GM_{eff} = GM - \Delta GM$$ **(1.5 marks)**

This correction is critical because, in extreme cases, a vessel with positive GM on paper may become unstable (GM_eff < 0) due to slack tanks, which is a leading cause of capsize accidents in inland barges.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

> **Question:**
> A rectangular inland cargo barge has the following particulars:
> - Length (L) = 50 m
> - Breadth (B) = 10 m
> - Draft (T) = 2.0 m
> - Centre of gravity above keel (KG) = 1.5 m
> - Water density ρ = **1000 kg/m³**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the metacentric height (GM) of the barge and comment on its stability. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cargo of 40 tonnes is loaded onto the deck at a height of 3.5 m above the keel on the centreline. Determine the new metacentric height. State any assumption made. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part A (a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- L = 50 m, B = 10 m, T = 2.0 m, KG = 1.5 m
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute Displaced Volume V:**
$$V = L \times B \times T = 50 \times 10 \times 2.0 = 1000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Displacement W:**
$$W = V \times \rho \times g = 1000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 9.81 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 9810 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Second Moment of Waterplane Area I_T (rectangular):**
$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{12} = \frac{50000}{12} = 4166.67 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 4 — Compute BM:**
$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{4166.67}{1000} = 4.167 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute KB (for rectangular barge, centroid of rectangular block):**
$$KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{2.0}{2} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute KM:**
$$KM = KB + BM = 1.0 + 4.167 = 5.167 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Compute GM:**
$$GM = KM - KG = 5.167 - 1.5 = 3.667 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Stability Comment:**
Since **GM = 3.667 m > 0**, the vessel is in **stable equilibrium**. However, this GM is quite high for an inland barge, indicating a "stiff" vessel that may roll abruptly. A more comfortable value for inland operations is in the range of 0.5 m to 2.0 m. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Addition:**
- Cargo weight w = 40 tonnes = 40,000 kg → w·g = 40,000 × 9.81 = **392,400 N**
- Height of load above keel, h = 3.5 m

**Step 1 — Compute New Displacement W_new:**
$$W_{new} = W + w \cdot g = 9{,}810{,}000 + 392{,}400 = 10{,}202{,}400 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute New KG by Moments About the Keel:**
$$KG_{new} = \frac{W \cdot KG + w \cdot g \cdot h}{W_{new}}$$
$$KG_{new} = \frac{(9{,}810{,}000)(1.5) + (392{,}400)(3.5)}{10{,}202{,}400}$$
$$KG_{new} = \frac{14{,}715{,}000 + 1{,}373{,}400}{10{,}202{,}400} = \frac{16{,}088{,}400}{10{,}202{,}400} = 1.577 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute New Draft T_new:**
For a rectangular barge, V = L·B·T, so T_new = V_new / (L·B):
$$V_{new} = \frac{W_{new}}{\rho \cdot g} = \frac{10{,}202{,}400}{1000 \times 9.81} = 1039.9 \text{ m}^3$$
$$T_{new} = \frac{1039.9}{50 \times 10} = 2.080 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute New KB:**
$$KB_{new} = \frac{T_{new}}{2} = 1.040 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute New BM:**
The waterplane dimensions L and B are unchanged for a rectangular barge, so I_T is constant.
$$BM_{new} = \frac{I_T}{V_{new}} = \frac{4166.67}{1039.9} = 4.007 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute New KM:**
$$KM_{new} = KB_{new} + BM_{new} = 1.040 + 4.007 = 5.047 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Compute New GM:**
$$GM_{new} = KM_{new} - KG_{new} = 5.047 - 1.577 = 3.470 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Conclusion:**
The new GM = **3.470 m**, which is **less than** the original GM (3.667 m). This is because the load was placed **above** the original centre of gravity, raising G. The vessel remains stable but slightly less stiff. **(7 marks awarded)**

**Assumption:** The barge hull is assumed to be a perfect rectangular prism (constant cross-section); the waterplane area does not change appreciably with the small change in draft.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

> **Question:**
> A river vessel has a displacement **W = 1500 tonnes**, **KG = 3.0 m**, **KB = 1.8 m**, and **BM = 1.5 m**.
>
> **(a)** Determine the metacentric height of the vessel. Is the vessel stable? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **righting moment** when the vessel heels by **10°**. Also, find the righting moment if a free-surface liquid in a tank causes an effective loss of GM of 0.3 m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute KM:**
$$KM = KB + BM = 1.8 + 1.5 = 3.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute GM:**
$$GM = KM - KG = 3.3 - 3.0 = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Stability Verdict:**
Since **GM = 0.3 m > 0**, the vessel is in **stable equilibrium**, but the GM is **marginal**. For inland river vessels, the recommended minimum GM is typically 0.15 m. The vessel barely meets the criterion, indicating sensitivity to free-surface effects, loading errors, or transverse shift of cargo. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute the Righting Lever (small angle approximation):**
For θ = 10°:
$$GZ = GM \times \sin(\theta) = 0.3 \times \sin(10°) = 0.3 \times 0.1736 = 0.0521 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Righting Moment:**
$$RM = W \times GZ = 1500 \times 0.0521 = 78.15 \text{ tonne-metres}$$
$$RM = 78.15 \times 9.81 = 766.6 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Free-Surface Correction:**
Effective GM after loss:
$$GM_{eff} = GM - 0.3 = 0.3 - 0.3 = 0.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — New Righting Lever at 10°:**
$$GZ_{new} = GM_{eff} \times \sin(10°) = 0.0 \times 0.1736 = 0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — New Righting Moment:**
$$RM_{new} = 1500 \times 0 = 0 \text{ tonne-metres}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
With the free-surface loss, the vessel loses **all initial transverse stability** and is in **neutral equilibrium**. Any further disturbance or additional weight shift above G will make the vessel unstable. This demonstrates why **partially filled tanks must be avoided** in inland water transport operations. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert tonnes to Newtons (or vice versa):** Displacement is given in tonnes, but formulas for BM use V in m³. Converting correctly is critical. If the question gives mass (tonnes), multiply by **g = 9.81** to get weight, OR keep it as mass and use consistent units throughout.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong axis for I_T:** Transverse stability uses I_T about the **longitudinal centreline** (= L·B³/12). Longitudinal stability uses I_L about the **transverse centreline** (= B·L³/12). Students mix these up frequently.
>
> 3. **Applying sin(θ) at large angles:** The formula GZ = GM·sin(θ) is valid **only for θ ≤ 10°**. For larger angles, use cross curves of stability.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the free-surface effect:** Many students compute GM correctly but forget to subtract the free-surface loss. In KTU problems involving partially filled tanks, **always check for free-surface correction**.
>
> 5. **Sign convention errors:** "GM" in a written answer must be expressed as a positive number with a clear statement ("stable" or "unstable"). A negative GM without explanation loses marks.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency in KG vs. KB:** Both must be measured from the **same reference line** (usually the keel). Mixing KG measured from the baseline and KB measured from the keel is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 — Stability: Final Revision Bullets**
>
> - Stability = tendency of a vessel to return to upright after heeling.
> - **Stable:** M above G → GM > 0 | **Neutral:** M at G → GM = 0 | **Unstable:** M below G → GM < 0.
> - **BM = I_T / V** is the master transverse stability formula (derived from wedge moments).
> - **I_T for rectangular waterplane = L·B³ / 12**; for ship-shaped, use hydrostatic tables.
> - **GM = KM − KG = (KB + BM) − KG**.
> - For a rectangular barge: **KB = T/2** and **GM ≈ B²/(12T) − (KG − T/2)**.
> - **Righting lever (small angles):** GZ = GM·sin(θ).
> - **Righting moment:** RM = W × GZ.
> - **Free-surface correction:** ΔGM = i_L·ρ_liquid / W; final GM = GM − ΔGM.
> - **Adding weight above G reduces GM**; **adding weight below G increases GM**.
> - **Transverse stability** is governed by beam²/draft ratio; **longitudinal stability** by length²/draft ratio.
> - **KN cross curves** are used for large-angle stability (GZ = KN − KG·sinθ).
> - **Minimum safe GM for inland vessels:** 0.15 m to 0.5 m (as per IWAI/class guidelines).
> - **Angle of loll** occurs when GM becomes negative due to free surface or top-loading — vessel sits at a permanent angle.
> - **Suspending a heavy load from a hook** (e.g., crane barge) effectively lowers G, **increasing GM**.
> - Always check **sign of GM** before declaring vessel safe.
> - In exam, **show all steps**: V, W, I_T, BM, KB, KM, GM, comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heel Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Heel Test # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Heel Test (Inclining Experiment)** is a standard, mandatory stability trial conducted on every newly built or substantially modified ship/vessel to determine the **transverse metacentric height (GM)** — the single most important parameter governing the initial transverse stability of a floating vessel. In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme naval-architecture vocabulary, the Heel Test is defined as: > *"An experimental procedure in which a known heeling weight ($W$) is moved transversely across the deck of a freely floating vessel by a measured distance ($d$), and the resulting angle of heel ($\theta$) is observed. From these three measured quantities, the metacentric height $GM$ of the vessel at its operating displacement ($W_0$) is computed."* The test is performed **in still water**, **in calm weather**, **with the vessel moored or freely floating**, **with no wind or current**, and **with all tanks either empty, full, or pressed up (no slack/free surfaces)**. It is governed internationally by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Code on Intact Stability, 2008 (MSC.267(85))** and the **ISO 16859 / ISO 12217** series of standards for inland and small craft. The governing relationship (the **Heel-Test Equation**) is: $$GM \;=\; \frac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \tan(\theta)}$$ where the symbols are as listed in the table below. | Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $GM$ | Metacentric height (transverse) | **m** (metres) | | $W$ | Heeling (test) weight | **kN** or **t (tonnes-force)** | | $d$ | Transverse distance the test weight is moved | **m** | | $W_0$ | Displacement of the vessel at the test condition | **kN** or **t** | | $\theta$ | Angle of heel produced by the movement of $W$ | **degrees** or **radians** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the Inland Water Transportation course, the Heel Test is studied because it directly determines whether an inland vessel (barge, river boat, ferry, tug) satisfies the **stability criteria of the Central Inland Water Transport Corporation (CIWTC)** and the **Inland Vessels Act, 1917 (as amended 2007)**. Every inland vessel plying Indian waterways must have a valid *Stability Booklet* that is, by definition, derived from a heel test. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on a small floating **inflatable raft** in a swimming pool. You are the **"heeling weight."** If you carefully walk **from the centre of the raft toward one edge**, the raft tilts (heels) — your raised end goes down, the opposite end goes up. The further you walk, the more it tilts. Now reverse your thinking: if a friend (who does not know your weight) watches the raft tilt when you walk, they can **work out how stable the raft is** by observing how little or how much it tilted for a known weight moved through a known distance. That is exactly what the heel test does to a ship — but with measurable precision, using plumb-bobs, pendulum instruments, or modern **inclinometer/data-logger** systems instead of just "eyeballing." > [!TIP] > **Plain-English take-away:** The Heel Test is essentially *“the ship’s way of telling us, through a measurable tilt, how stiff or floppy it is against overturning.”* A stiff, stable ship heels very little for a given moment → **large GM**. A floppy, tender ship heels a lot → **small GM** (which may even be *negative* if unstable). --- ## 1.3 Why Inland-Water-Transport Vessels Specifically Need This Test Inland vessels (river barges, push-boats, country boats, ferry craft) operate in: - Shallow, narrow channels (low freeboard). - Frequently loaded/unloaded asymmetric cargo (e.g. one hold full, the other empty). - Cross-currents and sudden wind gusts at bends. - Passenger-carrying configurations where the *centre of gravity* of the human load shifts continuously. These conditions make **transverse GM extremely critical**. The heel test quantifies this GM directly, and the result is fed into the vessel's *trim and stability booklet*, which is the document a KTU-trained Marine/Naval Architect would prepare. > [!WARNING] > **Stability pitfall unique to inland vessels:** A heel test performed in **river water (density ≈ 1000–1015 kg/m³)** gives a *different* GM than one performed in **sea water (density ≈ 1025 kg/m³)** because $W_0 = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$, and the same vessel displaces *more weight* in denser water for the same draught. Always specify the water density at the time of the test in the final report. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle A floating vessel in equilibrium is acted upon by exactly two forces: 1. **Gravity**, $W_0$, acting downwards through the **centre of gravity $G$**. 2. **Buoyancy**, $B = W_0$, acting upwards through the **centre of buoyancy $B$**. When these two lines of action coincide, the vessel is upright (heel angle $\theta = 0$). When the vessel is artificially inclined, the centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts to a new position $B'$, and the vertical line through $B'$ intersects the original vertical centreline at a fixed point called the **metacentre $M$**. The horizontal distance $G M$ is the **metacentric height**. The Heel Test exploits the fact that **the righting arm (GZ)** for a small heel angle $\theta$ is: $$GZ \;=\; GM \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ For small angles (KTU-accepted small-angle limit: **$\theta \le 10°$**), $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta_{\text{rad}}$, so: $$GZ \;\approx\; GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$ The corresponding **righting moment** is: $$\text{Righting Moment} \;=\; W_0 \cdot GZ \;=\; W_0 \cdot GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$ During the test, the **heeling moment** created by moving weight $W$ across by distance $d$ is: $$\text{Heeling Moment} \;=\; W \cdot d$$ Equating the two at static equilibrium: $$W \cdot d \;=\; W_0 \cdot GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$ which rearranges directly to the canonical heel-test equation. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition (The "Why & How" Behind Each Step) 1. **Why do we measure $\tan(\theta)$, not $\theta$ alone?** Because the ship heels through a measurable **pendulum deflection** or **inclinometer reading**. The **tangent of the angle** is, in fact, the *ratio* of horizontal deflection to the pendulum length — easier to record than the angle itself. 2. **Why must the weight $W$ be moved in *both* directions?** To cancel out any **initial list** (e.g. due to asymmetric loading). You take a reading, move the weight to the other side, take another reading, and use the **mean** shift. 3. **Why is the vessel moored with slack lines?** Tight mooring lines would **absorb** part of the heeling moment, giving a *falsely low* heel angle and a *falsely high* GM. Slack lines ensure the hull is free to rotate about its roll axis. 4. **Why are free-surface effects eliminated?** A partially filled tank behaves like a moving weight as the ship rolls, drastically *reducing* the effective GM. IMO regulations require all tanks to be either empty, completely full, or pressed-up (filled to the overflow). 5. **Why is the test repeated with increasing $d$?** To verify the **linearity** of $W\cdot d$ vs. $\tan(\theta)$ — i.e. to confirm the *small-angle regime* holds and the calculated GM is the same at each step. If GM changes with $\theta$, the test is being conducted beyond the linear range. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE — at least 70 % of KTU Part-B marks in Module-2 come from these expressions.** | # | Formula | Meaning / When Used | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $GM \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \tan\theta}$ | **Primary Heel-Test Equation** — used directly after the test | | 2 | $\tan\theta \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot GM}$ | Rearranged form to predict heel | | 3 | $GZ \;=\; GM \cdot \sin\theta \;\approx\; GM\cdot\tan\theta$ | Righting arm, small-angle regime | | 4 | $\text{Righting Moment} \;=\; W_0 \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Stability moment at any heel | | 5 | $\text{Heeling Moment} \;=\; W \cdot d$ | Moment applied by the test weight | | 6 | $\Delta GM_{\text{FSE}} \;=\; \dfrac{i \cdot \rho_{\text{fluid}}}{W_0}$ | Free-surface correction to GM | | 7 | $KM \;=\; KB + BM$ | Where $BM \;=\; \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | | 8 | $GM \;=\; KM - KG$ | True position of $G$ (with respect to keel) | | 9 | $\tan\theta_{\text{mean}} \;=\; \dfrac{\delta}{\ell}$ | $\delta$ = mean pendulum deflection, $\ell$ = pendulum length | | 10 | $W_0 \;=\; \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement from displaced volume $\nabla$ | > [!NOTE] > **Notation reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):** $W$ and $W_0$ denote *weights* (forces) in **kN** or **tonnes-force**. Some textbooks write them as masses $m$ in tonnes; in that case the formula becomes $GM = (m\,d)/(m_0\,\tan\theta)$ with consistent mass units on both sides. **Do not mix** mass on one side and force on the other in the KTU answer script. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application - **Stability Booklet Generation:** The heel test provides the experimental anchor value of $KG$ (and hence $GM$) used by naval architects to draft the vessel's *Trim & Stability Information* required by IMO Resolution A.749(18). - **Damage-Stability & Subdivision:** SOLAS Chapter II-1 calculations begin with the *light-service* $GM$ established by the heel test. - **Inland-Vessel Certification in India:** Under the Inland Vessels Rules 2017 (replacing the 1957 Rules), every passenger ferry, dredger, and barge over 12 m must submit a heel-test report for issue of the *Certificate of Survey*. - **Validation of Design Calculation:** Theoretical $KG$ from *weight-estimation curves* is invariably corrected by the test; the difference between calculated and measured $GM$ is a critical design-feedback metric. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Heel-Test Equation (from First Principles) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Derivation — every step is shown. No skipping is permitted in KTU 14-mark answers.** ### Setup Consider a vessel floating upright in still water. Let: - $W_0$ = weight of the vessel (= displacement) acting vertically **down** through $G$. - $B$ = centre of buoyancy, where the buoyancy force $B = W_0$ acts vertically **up**. - $G$ and $B$ initially lie on the same vertical line (equator of the ship, the *centreline plane*). ### Step 1 — Apply a known heeling moment Place a heeling weight $W$ on the deck at a transverse distance $a$ from the centreline. Move it further out by distance $d$ (so the new distance is $a+d$). The **additional heeling moment** about the original centreline is: $$M_{\text{heel}} \;=\; W \cdot d$$ This moment tips the vessel. The vessel rolls until a balancing righting moment is developed. ### Step 2 — Vessel heels by a small angle $\theta$ When the vessel rotates (rolls) by a small angle $\theta$ to starboard, two things happen: (a) The centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts **laterally** to a new position $B'$. (b) The line of action of the buoyancy force $B'$ (still vertical) no longer passes through $G$. The point where the new vertical line of $B'$ intersects the original vertical through $G$ is the **metacentre $M$**. The horizontal lever between $G$ and the new line of action of buoyancy is called the **righting arm $GZ$**. From similar triangles (the so-called *metacentric locus*), for small angles: $$\tan(\theta) \;=\; \dfrac{GZ}{GM} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad GZ \;=\; GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$ ### Step 3 — Write the equilibrium of moments For the heeled vessel to be in static equilibrium, the **heeling moment** must be exactly balanced by the **righting moment**: $$\underbrace{W \cdot d}_{\text{Heeling moment}} \;=\; \underbrace{W_0 \cdot GZ}_{\text{Righting moment}} \;=\; W_0 \cdot GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for the unknown $GM$ $$\begin{aligned} W \cdot d & \;=\; W_0 \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta \\[4pt] GM \cdot \tan\theta & \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0} \\[4pt] GM & \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \tan\theta} \qquad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ This is the **Heel-Test Equation**. It is the single most tested formula in KTU Module-2 stability questions. ### Step 5 — Small-angle (radian) approximation For $\theta \le 10°$, $\tan\theta \approx \theta_{\text{rad}}$ (error < 1.5 %): $$GM \;\approx\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \theta_{\text{rad}}} \qquad (\theta \text{ in radians})$$ To convert degrees to radians: $$\theta_{\text{rad}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{180}\cdot\theta_{\text{deg}}$$ ### Step 6 — Practical evaluation using pendulum deflection A long pendulum of length $\ell$ (typically 4–6 m) is hung in the ship. When the ship heels by $\theta$, the pendulum bob deflects by $\delta$. Geometry gives: $$\tan\theta \;=\; \dfrac{\delta}{\ell}$$ Substituting into the heel-test equation: $$GM \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \dfrac{\delta}{\ell}} \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d \cdot \ell}{W_0 \cdot \delta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Show this substitution in your 14-mark answers — it earns an extra mark for "**practical applicability**". --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem (typical 7-mark sub-question):** A river barge of displacement $W_0 = 1800$ kN is subjected to a heel test. A weight $W = 25$ kN is moved transversely across the deck by a distance $d = 6$ m. A pendulum of length $\ell = 5$ m gives a mean deflection of $\delta = 0.25$ m. Compute the metacentric height $GM$. ### Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Compute the tangent of the heel angle.** $$\tan\theta \;=\; \dfrac{\delta}{\ell} \;=\; \dfrac{0.25 \text{ m}}{5 \text{ m}} \;=\; 0.05$$ **Step 2 — Verify the small-angle regime.** $$\theta \;=\; \arctan(0.05) \;\approx\; 2.86° \;\;\;(\ll 10°,\; \text{so small-angle OK})$$ **Step 3 — Compute the heeling moment.** $$W \cdot d \;=\; 25 \text{ kN} \times 6 \text{ m} \;=\; 150 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the heel-test equation.** $$\begin{aligned} GM & \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \tan\theta} \\[4pt] & \;=\; \dfrac{150 \text{ kN·m}}{1800 \text{ kN} \times 0.05} \\[4pt] & \;=\; \dfrac{150}{90} \text{ m} \\[4pt] & \;=\; 1.667 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** $GM = 1.67$ m — this is comfortably above the IMO minimum recommended value of **0.15 m for cargo ships** and meets the **CIWTC criterion of $GM \ge 0.30$ m** for inland barges. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error & Corrections | Source of Error | Effect on Measured $GM$ | Correction Method | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Mooring-line tension** | Falsely small $\theta$ → falsely large $GM$ | Use slack lines; record line tension if possible | | **Free-surface effect (FSE)** | Reduces effective $GM$ | Press up all partially filled tanks; apply $\Delta GM_{FSE}$ | | **Wind / current during test** | Random scatter in readings | Conduct in calm conditions; repeat readings | | **Asymmetric initial list** | Adds constant offset | Average readings port & starboard | | **Density of water not equal to design density** | Wrong $W_0$ | Re-evaluate $W_0 = \rho g \nabla$ for actual $\rho$ | | **Pendulum damping / friction** | Slightly low $\delta$ | Use damped-inclination pendulum or modern inclinometer | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU answer-script mistake:** Students often forget to convert the heel angle from **degrees to radians** when using the small-angle approximation $GM \approx Wd/(W_0\,\theta_{\text{rad}})$. If $\theta$ is left in degrees in the denominator, $GM$ will come out **57.3 times too small**. Always write $\theta_{\text{rad}} = (\pi/180)\cdot\theta_{\text{deg}}$ explicitly. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Heel-Test Procedure > [!NOTE] > The diagram below traces the complete logical workflow of a heel test, from preparation through to the derivation of the stability booklet. It is the answer-image most frequently requested in KTU 14-mark questions on Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vessel ready for Heel Test] --> B[Confirm environmental conditions:
Still water, no wind, slack moorings] B --> C[Verify displacement W0 from
draft marks and water density rho] C --> D[Eliminate free-surface effects:
press up or empty all tanks] D --> E[Place heeling weight W at
centreline reference position] E --> F[Record initial pendulum
deflection delta_0 port side] F --> G[Move heeling weight W by
known transverse distance d] G --> H[Record new pendulum
deflection delta_1 port side] H --> I[Return weight to centre,
then move to starboard side] I --> J[Record deflection delta_2
on the starboard side] J --> K[Compute mean deflection
delta = mean of delta_1 and delta_2] K --> L[Calculate tan theta = delta divided by pendulum length ell] L --> M[Apply Heel Test equation:
GM = W times d divided by W0 times tan theta] M --> N[Repeat for at least 3 values of d
and verify linearity] N --> O[Compute KG = KM minus GM
using KM from hydrostatics] O --> P[Generate Stability Booklet
and submit to certifying authority] P --> Q[End of procedure:
vessel is seaworthy for issue of COI] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of Heel-Test Geometry (ASCII Representation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 high-yield visual:** This is the geometry the examiner expects you to draw when a question says *"With the aid of a sketch, explain the heel test."* Memorise the symbols and angles. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Ship["Vessel section view (transverse plane)"] direction TB WL[Waterline] CB[Centre of Buoyancy B] CG[Centre of Gravity G] CM[Metacentre M] KL[Keel Line K] axis[Vertical centreline through B] end Ship --> note1[Initial upright: G and B coincide on the vertical centreline] note1 --> note2[After heeling by angle theta:
B shifts to B prime
Righting arm GZ appears between G and the new vertical through B prime] note2 --> note3[GM equals horizontal distance from G to M
where M is intersection of original and new verticals] ``` ## 4.3 Logical Cause-Effect Map of $GM$ on Vessel Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Displacement W0 fixed by loading] --> GM{Inertial GM} GM --> Small[Small GM < 0.15 m] GM --> Medium[Medium GM 0.15 to 0.50 m] GM --> Large[Large GM > 0.50 m] Small --> Tend[Excessively tender, slow roll period,
cargo shift hazard] Medium --> IDEAL[Satisfies CIWTC inland vessel criterion,
recommended operating range] Large --> Stiff[Stiff, fast roll, possible heavy
shipping of green water on deck] Tend --> Fail[May fail Stability Criterion] Stiff --> Crew[Discomfort to crew and passengers] IDEAL --> OK[Operational approval granted] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the heel test. State the formula used to compute the metacentric height from the test data.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The heel test, also known as the *inclining experiment*, is a stability trial conducted on a freely floating vessel in calm water, in which a known heeling weight ($W$) is moved transversely across the deck by a measured distance ($d$) and the resulting angle of heel ($\theta$) is observed through pendulum or inclinometer readings. The metacentric height is calculated from the heel-test equation: > $$GM \;=\; \frac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \tan\theta}$$ > where $W_0$ is the displacement of the vessel at the test condition and $\tan\theta = \delta/\ell$ is obtained from the pendulum deflection $\delta$ and pendulum length $\ell$. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any four precautions to be observed while conducting a heel test.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. The vessel must be moored with **slack mooring lines** so that the hull is free to heel. **(1 m)** > 2. The test must be carried out in **calm weather, still water, no wind or current**. **(½ m)** > 3. **Free-surface effects must be eliminated** by pressing-up or emptying all partially filled tanks. **(½ m)** > 4. **All loose weights, cranes, and movable gear must be secured** and their positions recorded. **(½ m)** > 5. (Bonus point) The heeling weight must be moved by **at least three different distances** to confirm linear response. **(½ m)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice between two alternatives. **Both alternatives are provided below** so that you can practise either branch of the choice. --- ### 🔵 Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the equation for the metacentric height $GM$ of a vessel from the heel test, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1200-tonne inland river barge has a metacentric height of 0.6 m at full load. If a 4-tonne cargo shift occurs, causing an off-centre gravity shift of 1.2 m, calculate (i) the resulting list angle, and (ii) the new righting arm $GZ$ at 5° of heel. Comment on the vessel's stability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) 1. State the setup: vessel upright in still water, $W_0$ = displacement, $G$ = CoG, $B$ = CoB. **(1 m)** 2. Apply heeling moment $M_h = W \cdot d$ by moving weight $W$ across distance $d$. **(1 m)** 3. Vessel heels by small angle $\theta$; CoB shifts to $B'$; line of buoyancy intersects original centreline at $M$. **(1 m)** 4. Righting arm $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx GM \cdot \tan\theta$ for small angles. **(1 m)** 5. Righting moment = $W_0 \cdot GZ = W_0 \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$. **(1 m)** 6. Equilibrium: $W \cdot d = W_0 \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$. **(1 m)** 7. Final equation: $GM = Wd/(W_0\tan\theta)$. **(1 m)** **Assumptions to be stated (carry 1 mark from above if listed):** - Small heel angle ($\theta \le 10°$). - Free-surface effects absent. - Vessel is symmetric about the centreline. - Calm water, no wind, slack moorings. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $W_0 = 1200$ t, $GM = 0.6$ m, $W = 4$ t, shift $y = 1.2$ m. **Step (i) — Resulting list angle:** The cargo shift creates a permanent heeling moment: $$M_h \;=\; W \cdot y \;=\; 4 \text{ t} \times 1.2 \text{ m} \;=\; 4.8 \text{ t·m}$$ This is balanced by the righting moment at small angles: $$\tan\theta_{\text{list}} \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot y}{W_0 \cdot GM} \;=\; \dfrac{4.8}{1200 \times 0.6} \;=\; \dfrac{4.8}{720} \;=\; 0.00667$$ $$\theta_{\text{list}} \;=\; \arctan(0.00667) \;\approx\; 0.382°$$ **Step (ii) — Righting arm $GZ$ at 5° heel:** $$GZ \;=\; GM \cdot \sin(5°) \;=\; 0.6 \times 0.08716 \;=\; 0.0523 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 5.23 \text{ cm}$$ **Comment on stability:** The new $GM$ after the cargo shift becomes: $$GM_{\text{new}} \;=\; GM - \dfrac{W \cdot y}{W_0 \cdot \tan\theta}$$ For a permanent shift, the centre of gravity $G$ effectively moves, reducing $GM$ by approximately: $$\Delta GM \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot y}{W_0} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 1.2}{1200} \;=\; 0.004 \text{ m} \;=\; 4 \text{ mm}$$ So $GM_{\text{new}} \approx 0.596$ m, which is **still within the safe operating range** (> 0.30 m). The vessel remains stable, but the master must initiate corrective counter-flooding to bring $G$ back to the centreline. **(1 mark for the conclusion.)** --- ### 🟢 Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure of conducting a heel test on a newly built vessel. Mention the role of the pendulum. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During a heel test on a 2500 kN displacement tug, a heeling weight of 30 kN was moved 8 m across the deck. The mean pendulum deflection was 0.40 m on a 6 m pendulum. Compute (i) the angle of heel, (ii) the metacentric height $GM$, and (iii) the minimum additional cargo shift (in tonnes) that would make the vessel critically unstable ($GM = 0$). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Procedure (in numbered steps):** 1. Confirm vessel is in still, calm water; remove all loose gear. **(1 m)** 2. Press-up or empty all tanks to eliminate free surface. **(1 m)** 3. Slacken all mooring lines. **(1 m)** 4. Suspend long pendulums (typically 4–6 m) fore and aft, in port and starboard limber holes, marking a reference scale at the bottom of each. **(1 m)** 5. Place a known heeling weight $W$ at the deck centreline and record initial pendulum readings. **(1 m)** 6. Move $W$ transversely by a measured distance $d$ to port, record new readings; repeat for starboard. **(1 m)** 7. Compute $GM$ from $GM = Wd\ell/(W_0\delta)$ where $\delta$ is the mean deflection and $\ell$ is pendulum length. **(1 m)** **Role of the pendulum:** It measures the **heel angle $\theta$** indirectly through the deflection $\delta$. For small angles, $\tan\theta = \delta/\ell$. By using a long pendulum, the deflection $\delta$ is amplified and measured with high precision. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $W_0 = 2500$ kN, $W = 30$ kN, $d = 8$ m, $\delta = 0.40$ m, $\ell = 6$ m. **Step (i) — Angle of heel:** $$\tan\theta \;=\; \dfrac{\delta}{\ell} \;=\; \dfrac{0.40}{6} \;=\; 0.0667$$ $$\theta \;=\; \arctan(0.0667) \;\approx\; 3.82°$$ **Step (ii) — Metacentric height $GM$:** $$\begin{aligned} GM & \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot d}{W_0 \cdot \tan\theta} \\[4pt] & \;=\; \dfrac{30 \times 8}{2500 \times 0.0667} \\[4pt] & \;=\; \dfrac{240}{166.67} \;\approx\; 1.44 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step (iii) — Critical cargo shift for $GM = 0$:** For the vessel to become critically unstable, the off-centre shift must reduce the effective $GM$ to zero. The reduction in $GM$ caused by an off-centre shift $\Delta y$ of mass $m$ is: $$\Delta GM \;=\; \dfrac{m \cdot \Delta y}{W_0 / g}$$ If the shift occurs by 1 m for simplicity, the additional weight required is: $$m \;=\; \dfrac{GM_{\text{old}} \cdot W_0 / g}{1} \;=\; \dfrac{1.44 \times 2500/9.81}{1} \;\approx\; 367 \text{ kN/g} \;\approx\; 37.4 \text{ tonnes (mass)}$$ Equivalently, in weight units: $W_{\text{shift}} = GM_{\text{old}} \cdot W_0 = 1.44 \times 2500 = 3600$ kN·m, which means a 1-m shift of **3600 kN ≈ 367 tonnes** would neutralise the existing $GM$. For smaller lateral shifts, the required weight scales inversely. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on Heel-Test questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to take the mean of port- and starboard deflections** — costs 1 mark in every 7-mark sub-question. Always compute $\delta_{\text{mean}} = (\delta_{\text{port}} + \delta_{\text{starboard}})/2$. > 2. **Mixing up weight units** ($kN$) with mass units ($t$ or $kg$) inside the equation. Both numerator and denominator must use *consistent force units* (or *consistent mass units*). > 3. **Not stating the small-angle assumption.** Examiners award at least 1 mark for explicitly writing *"assuming small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta$"*. > 4. **Ignoring free-surface correction.** If the question gives tank particulars, you must compute $\Delta GM_{\text{FSE}} = i \rho_{\text{fluid}} / W_0$ and adjust. > 5. **Wrong water-density assumption.** A common KTU trick: the question specifies *"the test was carried out in brackish water of density 1.015 t/m³"* — students who use the standard sea-water density 1.025 t/m³ lose 1 mark. > 6. **Drawing a vague sketch without labels.** The sketch must show $G$, $B$, $B'$, $M$, $GZ$, $\theta$, and the centreline — each labelled. --- ## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Heel Test = Inclining Experiment** = the standard trial for measuring transverse $GM$. - **Governing equation:** $GM = (W \cdot d) / (W_0 \cdot \tan\theta)$. - **Pendulum geometry:** $\tan\theta = \delta / \ell$. - **Small-angle limit:** $\theta \le 10°$ (KTU-accepted). - **Minimum three heeling positions** to verify linearity. - **Slack moorings**, **no wind**, **no current**, **no free surface** — these four conditions are **non-negotiable**. - **Slope to weight-and-distance graph** must be constant; if not, the test is invalid. - **Free-surface correction:** $\Delta GM_{\text{FSE}} = i \rho / W_0$, where $i$ is the transverse moment of inertia of the slack tank's free surface. - **Indian regulatory framework:** Inland Vessels Rules 2017 (replacing 1957 Rules), CIWTC stability criteria. - **International benchmark:** IMO Code on Intact Stability 2008 (Resolution MSC.267(85)), SOLAS Chapter II-1. - **Practical unit check:** $W$ in kN, $d$ in m, $W_0$ in kN, $\tan\theta$ dimensionless → $GM$ in **m**. ✔️ - **Critical stability threshold:** $GM_{\min} = 0.15$ m (IMO, general cargo); $GM_{\min} = 0.30$ m (CIWTC, inland barges). - **Critical negative GM** ($GM < 0$) → vessel is **capsize-prone** — heel test result must be acted upon immediately by relocating weights or adding ballast. - **Heel test gives $GM$, not $KG$ directly.** To get $KG$, you first compute $KM = KB + BM$ (where $BM = I_T/\nabla$) and then $KG = KM - GM$. - **Result is recorded** in the vessel's *Trim & Stability Booklet* — a mandatory document on every commercial inland vessel.

topics.title: Resistance and Propulsion of Inland Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance and Propulsion of Inland Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Resistance and Propulsion

In the context of **Inland Water Transportation**, the term **Resistance** refers to the total hydrodynamic force opposing the forward motion of a vessel through water, measured along the longitudinal axis of the ship. For inland vessels operating in rivers, canals, and restricted waterways, this resistance is a critical design parameter that directly determines the required propulsive power, fuel consumption, and hence the economic viability of the transport operation.

**Resistance (R)** is the longitudinal force (in Newtons, **N**) that the water exerts against a vessel's hull as it moves forward at a given speed, which the propulsion system must overcome to maintain a steady velocity.

**Propulsion**, conversely, is the engineering discipline concerned with converting the rotational mechanical energy of a prime mover (engine) into translational thrust that drives the vessel forward, primarily through the action of a propeller or water-jet system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> "Resistance and Propulsion of Inland Vessels" deals with the quantitative estimation of the total resistance offered by water to a vessel's motion in restricted and shallow waterways, the determination of effective and delivered propulsive power, and the design considerations of propulsors suited for low-draft, slender inland vessels such as barges, push-tows, and river-sea vessels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pushing a large rectangular wooden block across a calm lake. You will immediately feel two distinct sensations:
1. A continuous rubbing resistance along the bottom and sides of the block — this is like **Frictional Resistance**.
2. As the block speeds up, you will notice waves forming at its front and stern, and a visible depression behind it — this is like **Wave-Making Resistance**.

Now imagine pushing the same block in a narrow canal with shallow water. Suddenly, the block feels *much* heavier to push. The bottom of the canal is "squeezing" the water flow under the hull, and the canal banks are pulling the block sideways. This is the essence of **shallow-water and restricted-water effects** that make inland vessel resistance fundamentally different from deep-sea ship resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Density of fresh water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Density of sea water: **ρ = 1025 kg/m³**
> - Kinematic viscosity of water: **ν ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** (fresh, 20°C)
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s**

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Inland Water Transport

Inland vessels are fundamentally different from ocean-going ships in three resistance-relevant ways:

| Feature | Ocean-Going Ship | Inland Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| Depth ratio (h/T) | > 5 (very deep) | 1.2 – 3.0 (shallow) |
| Block coefficient (C_B) | 0.60 – 0.85 | 0.75 – 0.95 (full) |
| Speed-length ratio (Fn) | 0.20 – 0.40 | 0.10 – 0.30 (slow) |
| Waterway width | Unlimited | Often < 5 × beam |

These differences mean the standard Froude-based deep-water resistance methods (e.g., ITTC-1978) must be modified using **shallow-water correction factors** and **slender-body correlations** such as the **Dand-Ferguson** or **Lackenby** approaches for accurate power prediction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Total Resistance (R_T)

The total resistance of a vessel is the sum of multiple physically distinct contributions acting along the longitudinal centerline:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A + R_{APP}$$

Where each term represents a different physical phenomenon:

- **R_F — Frictional Resistance**: Viscous shear stress acting on the wetted surface of the hull. Dominant for full-form inland vessels with large wetted area.
- **R_R — Residual Resistance**: The combined effect of wave-making (R_W), eddy-making (R_E), and pressure-resistance due to flow separation. For slow inland vessels, eddy resistance from the bluff stern dominates the residual term.
- **R_A — Air Resistance**: Aerodynamic drag on the superstructure. Typically 3–5 % of R_T for low-speed inland vessels, and almost negligible for unmanned barges.
- **R_APP — Appendage Resistance**: Resistance due to rudders, struts, shaft brackets, and bilge keels. Approximated as a fraction (5 % – 10 %) of bare-hull frictional resistance.

## 2.2 Frictional Resistance — The ITTC-1978 Correlation Line

The most widely used correlation in KTU board problems is the **ITTC-1978 Formula** for frictional resistance coefficient:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$$

where the **Reynolds Number** is based on the length of the vessel:

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$

The frictional resistance is then:

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$$

where **S** is the wetted surface area (m²), estimated by the **Berteaux formula** (standard KTU reference):

$$S = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot T + \frac{\nabla}{T}$$

with **L** = waterline length, **T** = draft, and **∇** = volume of displacement.

## 2.3 Form Factor and Correlation Allowance (1 + k)

Because the actual frictional flow around a ship hull deviates from a flat plate, a **form factor (k)** is introduced. The viscous resistance becomes:

$$R_V = R_F \cdot (1 + k)$$

Typical values of **k** for inland vessels:
- **Slender barge hulls** → k = 0.10 – 0.20
- **Full-form river-sea vessels** → k = 0.20 – 0.40
- **Rounded tugboat hulls** → k = 0.15 – 0.25

A **correlation allowance (C_A)** is added to account for hull roughness, paint degradation, and appendage effects. The total viscous + residual approach is:

$$R_T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot (C_F \cdot (1+k) + C_A + C_R)$$

where **C_R** is the residual resistance coefficient (obtained from model tests or from standard series data such as **Series 60** or **Dand-Ferguson**).

## 2.4 Froude Number and Wave Resistance

The **Froude Number** is the dimensionless ratio of inertial to gravitational forces and is THE most important parameter for inland vessels:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

For inland vessels, the practical range is **Fn = 0.10 – 0.30**. At low Froude numbers, wave-making is small, and most of the residual resistance comes from **viscous-pressure (eddy) effects**. As Fn exceeds 0.30, wave resistance grows rapidly with the fourth power of velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hull Speed Concept:** For a displacement inland vessel, the practical maximum speed is reached near **Fn ≈ 0.40**, beyond which the wave-making resistance explodes. This is why inland push-tows and barges are designed to operate at **Fn = 0.15 – 0.25** for fuel economy.

## 2.5 Shallow-Water Resistance Corrections

When the depth-to-draft ratio **h/T < 3.0**, the water flow under the hull is restricted, and the following corrections are required:

1. **Shallow-water frictional correction**: A multiplicative factor of **1.10 – 1.25** on R_F, accounting for increased boundary-layer thickness.
2. **Squat effect**: A sinkage (typically **0.05 × T** to **0.15 × T**) increases the effective wetted area.
3. **Bank effect**: When a vessel passes close to a canal bank, the low-pressure zone on the far side pulls the vessel toward the bank. This is quantified by the **ICORELS (1980)** formulas.
4. **Ship-Ship interaction**: In a narrow canal, the passing of two vessels creates **Bernoulli suction** that pulls them together; velocity limitation rules (e.g., 10 km/h in a 50-m canal) are mandatory.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $R_n = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless; **L** in m, **V** in m/s |
| 2 | Froude Number | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$ | Dimensionless; **g = 9.81 m/s²** |
| 3 | ITTC-1978 Friction Coefficient | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | Use log base 10 |
| 4 | Frictional Resistance | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | Wetted Surface (Berteaux) | $S = 1.7 L T + \dfrac{\nabla}{T}$ | m² |
| 6 | Total Resistance | $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A + R_{APP}$ | N |
| 7 | Viscous Resistance | $R_V = R_F (1+k)$ | N |
| 8 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | Watts |
| 9 | Thrust Power | $P_T = R_T \cdot V_S / (1 - t)$ | Watts |
| 10 | Wake Fraction | $w = 1 - V_S / V$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Thrust Deduction | $t = (T - R_T) / T$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Hull Efficiency | $\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}$ | 1.0 – 1.10 (typical) |
| 13 | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O \cdot \eta_R$ | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| 14 | Advance Ratio | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{n \cdot D}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Thrust Coefficient | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | From B-series / Wageningen |
| 16 | Torque Coefficient | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | From B-series / Wageningen |
| 17 | Open-Water Efficiency | $\eta_O = \dfrac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| 18 | Delivered Power | $P_D = P_E / \eta_D = 2\pi n Q$ | Watts |
| 19 | Brake Power | $P_B = P_D / \eta_{Shaft}$ | Watts; shaft efficiency ≈ 0.97 |
| 20 | Shallow-Water Fn Correction | $F_{n,h} = F_n / \sqrt{(h/T - 1)/h/T}$ | Use when **h/T < 3** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When answering a numerical problem, ALWAYS compute and quote the Froude number first, then state whether the flow regime is **subcritical (Fn < 0.4)**, **transcritical (0.4 < Fn < 0.5)**, or **supercritical (Fn > 0.5)**. For inland vessels, you are almost always in the subcritical regime.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The resistance and propulsion analysis of inland vessels is the foundation for:
- **Fuel-bunker calculation** for a river-sea voyage (e.g., a 1000-TEU container vessel sailing Rotterdam–Bucharest on the Rhine).
- **Push-tow fleet design**: a 4-barge push-tow unit (typical of Mississippi and Yangtze operations) requires careful matching of push-boat bollard-pull to total train resistance.
- **Engine selection**: inland vessels need high-torque, low-RPM diesel engines coupled to large-diameter, low-RPM propellers — fundamentally different from a high-speed container ship's propulsion.
- **Canal lock and waterway capacity planning**: resistance and speed dictate the time taken to pass through a lock chamber, which determines the daily throughput of the waterway.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Resistance and Effective Power of an Inland Barge

**Problem Statement:**
A river barge has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars, **L = 60 m**
- Draft, **T = 2.5 m**
- Breadth, **B = 9 m**
- Wetted surface area, **S = 950 m²**
- Form factor, **1 + k = 1.15**
- Correlation allowance, **C_A = 0.0004**
- Service speed, **V = 4.5 m/s** (≈ 8.75 knots)
- Residual-resistance coefficient from the standard series, **C_R = 0.0010** at the given Fn
- Kinematic viscosity, **ν = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- Water density, **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**

**Compute the total resistance and the effective power.**

---

### Step 1: Compute the Reynolds Number

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} = \frac{4.5 \times 60}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_n = 2.7 \times 10^{8}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Froude Number

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}} = \frac{4.5}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 60}}$$

$$F_n = \frac{4.5}{\sqrt{588.6}} = \frac{4.5}{24.26} = 0.1855$$

The vessel is operating in the **subcritical regime**, which is normal for an inland barge. The residual-resistance coefficient **C_R** is therefore valid as given.

### Step 3: Compute the ITTC-1978 Frictional Resistance Coefficient

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(2.7 \times 10^{8}) - 2)^2}$$

$$\log_{10}(2.7 \times 10^{8}) = 8.4314$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(8.4314 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.4314)^2} = \frac{0.075}{41.36}$$

$$C_F = 0.001813$$

### Step 4: Compute the Frictional Resistance

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (4.5)^2 \times 950 \times 0.001813$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 20.25 \times 950 \times 0.001813$$

$$R_F = 500 \times 20.25 \times 950 \times 0.001813$$

$$R_F = 500 \times 19,237.5 \times 0.001813$$

$$\boxed{R_F = 17,437.7 \text{ N} \approx 17.44 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Viscous Resistance with Form Factor

$$R_V = R_F \cdot (1 + k) = 17,437.7 \times 1.15$$

$$R_V = 20,053.3 \text{ N}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Correlation Allowance Resistance

$$R_A = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_A$$

$$R_A = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 20.25 \times 950 \times 0.0004$$

$$R_A = 3,847.5 \text{ N}$$

### Step 7: Compute the Residual Resistance

$$R_R = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_R$$

$$R_R = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 20.25 \times 950 \times 0.0010$$

$$R_R = 9,618.75 \text{ N}$$

### Step 8: Total Resistance

$$R_T = R_V + R_A + R_R$$

$$R_T = 20,053.3 + 3,847.5 + 9,618.75$$

$$\boxed{R_T = 33,519.6 \text{ N} \approx 33.52 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 9: Effective Power

$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 33,519.6 \times 4.5$$

$$\boxed{P_E = 150,838 \text{ W} \approx 150.8 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Propulsive Power and Propeller Sizing

**Continuation Problem:** For the same barge, the model tests gave:
- Wake fraction, **w = 0.20**
- Thrust deduction, **t = 0.15**
- Shaft efficiency, **η_shaft = 0.97**

We need to find:
1. Thrust power P_T
2. Delivered power P_D (assume propulsive efficiency η_D = 0.62, OR back-calculate)
3. Brake power P_B

### Step 1: Hull Efficiency

$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{1 - 0.15}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{0.85}{0.80}$$

$$\eta_H = 1.0625$$

The hull efficiency > 1 is a common feature of **single-screw vessels**, indicating that the propeller acts on accelerated flow (low wake) and the thrust deduction is small.

### Step 2: Thrust Required at the Propeller

The thrust that the propeller must deliver equals the total resistance divided by (1 - t):

$$T = \frac{R_T}{1 - t} = \frac{33,519.6}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{33,519.6}{0.85}$$

$$T = 39,434.8 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3: Thrust Power

The effective velocity at the propeller is the ship speed divided by (1 - w):

$$V_A = V \cdot (1 - w) = 4.5 \times 0.80 = 3.60 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P_T = T \cdot V_A = 39,434.8 \times 3.60 = 141,965 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_T = 141.97 \text{ kW}}$$

### Step 4: Delivered Power via Propulsive Efficiency

Using η_D = η_H × η_O × η_R, if we assume η_O × η_R = 0.5835 (rotative + open-water), then:

$$P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{150,838}{0.62} = 243,287 \text{ W} \approx 243.3 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5: Brake Power

$$P_B = \frac{P_D}{\eta_{shaft}} = \frac{243,287}{0.97}$$

$$\boxed{P_B = 250,812 \text{ W} \approx 250.8 \text{ kW}}$$

This is the **rated output of the main diesel engine** that must be installed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Propeller Selection (Wageningen B-Series)

For a 4-bladed Wageningen B-series propeller chosen for the above barge:

**Given / Assumed:**
- Bar P/D (pitch-to-diameter ratio) = 0.70
- Blade-area ratio (EAR) = 0.55
- Required thrust T = 39,435 N
- Ship speed V = 4.5 m/s
- Estimated open-water efficiency η_O = 0.62
- Rotational speed n = 180 RPM = 3.0 rev/s

**Find the propeller diameter D.**

### Step 1: Express Thrust Coefficient in Terms of Diameter

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4}$$

We need a second equation. For a B-series propeller, the open-water efficiency is related to K_T and the advance coefficient J by:

$$\eta_O = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$
