**Step 4.** Hydraulic (water) power:

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho_{liq} \cdot g \cdot Q_p \cdot H_{tot}
$$

**Step 5.** Brake (shaft) power after pump efficiency:

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}}
$$

with $\eta_{pump}$ typically **0.65–0.85** for centrifugal, **0.85–0.92** for positive displacement.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Continuous vs Cyclic

| Parameter | Cyclic (Crane + Grab) | Continuous (Bucket-Wheel) |
|-----------|-----------------------|---------------------------|
| Capital cost per t/h | **High (₹)** | Moderate |
| Capacity range | 100–600 t/h | 500–4000 t/h |
| Cycle time / unit operation | 45–60 s | < 1 s/lump |
| Damage to cargo | Moderate (drop impact) | High (abrasion) |
| Suitability for fine powders | Poor (dust) | Poor |
| Mobility between berths | High (rubber-tyred) | Low (rail/slewing) |
| Energy per tonne | 0.6 kWh/t | 0.25 kWh/t |
| Best cargo | Coal, ore, scrap | Coal, ore, free-flowing bulk |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Inland Terminal Cargo Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["River Barge Push-Tow Unit"] --> nodeB["Quay Edge Berth"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Cargo Type Decision Matrix"}
    nodeC -- Dry Bulk Free Flowing --> nodeD["Bucket Wheel Unloader"]
    nodeC -- Dry Bulk Sticky --> nodeE["Grab Crane With Clamshell"]
    nodeC -- Liquid Cargo --> nodeF["Pipeline Manifold And Pump Skid"]
    nodeC -- Containerised --> nodeG["Ship To Shore Gantry Crane"]
    nodeC -- Packaged --> nodeH["Mobile Harbour Crane"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["Hopper Buffer"]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Belt Conveyor Gallery"]
    nodeF --> nodeK["Storage Tank Farm"]
    nodeG --> nodeL["Yard Stack Using RTG"]
    nodeH --> nodeM["Truck Direct Loading"]
    nodeJ --> nodeN{"Final Destination Routing"}
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO["Rail Siding Or Truck Weighbridge"]
    nodeN --> nodeP["Stockyard Stacker Reclaimer"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Cyclic Grab Crane Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Step 1 Hoist Empty"] --> nodeB["Step 2 Slew Out"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 3 Lower To Hatch"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 4 Dig And Close Grab"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 5 Hoist Loaded"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 6 Slew Back"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 7 Lower And Release"]
    nodeG --> nodeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow represents a *time block* $t_i$. Total $T_c$ is the sum of the seven blocks; reducing any single block (e.g., faster hoist) reduces $T_c$ and *linearly* increases productivity $P$ (since $P \propto 1/T_c$).

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Self-Unloading Barge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Holds With Bulk Cargo"] --> nodeB["Hopper Feeds At Bottom Of Hold"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Conveyor Inside Tunnel"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Deck Belt Conveyor Luffable"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Boom Swings Ashore"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Shore Hopper"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Stockyard Or Truck"]
    nodeH["Hydraulic Power Pack"] --> nodeB
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeH --> nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why self-unloaders?** On draft-restricted inland waterways (NW-1, NW-2), shore cranes cannot always reach the centre of the hold. A *self-unloading barge* carries its own conveyor and boom, eliminating dependence on quay-side infrastructure — vital for **river-sea trade on shallow Indian waterways**.

## 4.4 Equipment Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Cargo Arrives At Terminal"] --> nodeB{"Physical State"}
    nodeB -- Solid --> nodeC{"Lump Size And Abrasiveness"}
    nodeC -- Coarse And Free Flowing --> nodeD["Bucket Wheel"]
    nodeC -- Fine And Cohesive --> nodeE["Pneumatic Or Screw"]
    nodeC -- Mixed And Sticky --> nodeF["Grab Crane"]
    nodeB -- Liquid --> nodeG["Pipeline And Pump"]
    nodeB -- Packaged --> nodeH["Mobile Crane Or Forklift"]
    nodeB -- Container --> nodeI["Gantry Or Reach Stacker"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define *grab payload* and state how it is computed for a clamshell bucket unloading coal of bulk density 0.85 t/m³ if the bucket volume is 4 m³ and fill factor is 0.78.

**Model Answer:**

> **Grab payload** is the actual mass of cargo lifted in one grab cycle. It accounts for imperfect filling of the bucket.

$$
m_{grab} = V_g \cdot \rho_b \cdot \eta_{fill}
$$

$$
m_{grab} = 4 \times 0.85 \times 0.78 = 2.652 \;\text{t}
$$

**∴ Grab payload = 2.65 t** (3 marks: definition + formula + substitution).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** List any **four** criteria that govern the selection of cargo-handling equipment at an IWT terminal.

**Model Answer (any four valid points):**

1. **Physical nature of cargo** — bulk density, lump size, abrasiveness, moisture.
2. **Required throughput** — design tonnage per hour and per year.
3. **Capital & operating cost** — ₹ per tonne installed capacity.
4. **Available waterfront & quay strength** — bearing capacity for rail-mounted cranes.
5. **Vessel size & turnaround time** — Class IV vs Class VII push-tows.
6. **Environmental constraints** — dust, noise, spillage regulations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When solving productivity problems, students often *forget to include the availability factor $k_{av}$* and *omit units in intermediate steps*. Both omissions cost 1–2 marks each. Always carry units till the final answer and quote $k_{av}$ from the question.

---

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **bucket-wheel continuous unloader**. State its typical capacity range and two limitations.

**Model Solution:**

A **bucket-wheel unloader** is a rotary continuous-bulk-discharge machine. A wheel mounted on a slewing/luffing boom carries peripheral buckets. As the wheel rotates, buckets **scoop** material from the barge hold at the leading face and **discharge** it by gravity into an integral chute at the top.

**Key features:**
- Rotation speed ω (rpm) determines discharge frequency.
- Wheel diameter D_w governs maximum reach into hold.
- Boom luffing angle controls discharge height to shore hopper.

**Typical capacity:** **500 – 4 000 t/h** for dry bulk.

**Two limitations:**
1. **Not suitable for sticky/wet cargo** — buckets fail to discharge due to adhesion.
2. **Generates significant dust & abrasion damage** to friable cargo (e.g., coal lump degradation).

**Sketch (described):** show barge cross-section, wheel at boom tip, buckets at periphery, chute discharging to shore belt conveyor.

*(7 marks: 2 for description + 2 for sketch + 1 for capacity + 2 for limitations.)*

---

#### (b) [7 marks] A grab crane has a cycle time of **52 s**. The grab volume is **5 m³**, fill factor **0.80**, and the cargo is iron ore with bulk density **2.4 t/m³**. Availability is **0.85**, annual operating hours **4 800 h**, and utilisation factor **0.75**. Compute the **hourly productivity** and **annual throughput** in MTPA.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Grab payload

$$
m_{grab} = 5 \times 2.4 \times 0.80 = 9.6 \;\text{t}
$$

**Step 2:** Effective cycles per hour

$$
N_{eff} = \frac{3600 \times 0.85}{52} = 58.85 \;\text{cycles/h}
$$

**Step 3:** Hourly productivity

$$
P = 9.6 \times 58.85 = 564.96 \;\text{t/h} \approx 565 \;\text{t/h}
$$

**Step 4:** Annual throughput

$$
Q_a = P \times H_{op} \times \eta = 564.96 \times 4800 \times 0.75
$$

$$
Q_a = 2{,}033{,}856 \;\text{t/yr} \approx 2.03 \;\text{MTPA}
$$

**Final answer:** **P ≈ 565 t/h**, **Q_a ≈ 2.03 MTPA**.

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### (a) [7 marks] Describe with a block diagram the **functional architecture of an inland waterway bulk-cargo terminal**, identifying at least **six** major equipment items and their role in the cargo flow chain.

**Model Solution (block diagram + roles):**

1. **Berthing Structure** — quay wall for mooring barges; provides reaction point for cranes.
2. **Bucket-Wheel / Grab Crane** — primary unloader at the water–land interface.
3. **Hopper Buffer** — decouples the cyclic unloader from the continuous downstream belt.
4. **Belt Conveyor Gallery** — transports bulk horizontally / vertically across the yard.
5. **Stacker-Reclaimer** — builds longitudinal stockpiles and recovers cargo on demand.
6. **Ship Loader** — loads outbound barges or trucks for onward distribution.
7. **Weighbridge / Belt Scale** — mass-flow inventory measurement.
8. **Dust Suppression System** — water-spray/fog nozzles for environmental compliance.

**Block-flow description (text equivalent of diagram):**

*Barge → Berth → Crane → Hopper → Belt Conveyor → Stacker-Reclaimer → Truck/Second-Barge → Inland Destination.*

*(7 marks: 1 for each correctly identified equipment item beyond the minimum six, 1 for explaining the functional chain.)*

---

#### (b) [7 marks] A belt conveyor transports coal with bulk density **0.90 t/m³** at a belt speed of **2.5 m/s**. The troughed belt has a width of **1.2 m**, idler trough angle 35°, surcharge factor 1.20, and trough depth **0.35 m**. Calculate the conveyor throughput in **t/h**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Effective cross-section. For 35° trough, $\cot(17.5°) \approx 3.171$.

$$
A_c = (0.371 \times h^{2}) + (b \times h) = 0.371(0.35)^2 + (1.2 \times 0.35)
$$

$$
A_c = 0.371 \times 0.1225 + 0.42 = 0.0454 + 0.42 = 0.4654 \;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply surcharge factor:

$$
A_{c,eff} = 0.4654 \times 1.20 = 0.5585 \;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric flow:

$$
Q_{vol} = A_{c,eff} \times v = 0.5585 \times 2.5 = 1.3962 \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to t/h:

$$
Q_b = 1.3962 \times 0.90 \times 3600 = 4523.7 \;\text{t/h}
$$

**Step 5:** Check lump-speed rule. If $d_{\max} = 200$ mm, $v_{\max} = \sqrt{0.2}/2 = 0.224$ m/s. **Our chosen 2.5 m/s violates the rule** for 200-mm lumps — we must flag this in the answer.

**∴ Throughput ≈ 4524 t/h**, with caveat on belt-speed compliance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ☐ **Four cargo classes** — Bulk Dry, Liquid, Packaged/Break-Bulk, Containerised.
- ☐ **Cyclic vs Continuous** equipment — cycle time difference, productivity formula difference.
- ☐ **Grab payload formula** $m_{grab} = V_g \rho_b \eta_{fill}$ — appears in 70% of past KTU questions.
- ☐ **Crane productivity** $P = m_{grab} \cdot N_{eff}$ — always carry units to the final value.
- ☐ **Belt conveyor** cross-section depends on trough angle (standard 35°).
- ☐ **Belt-speed rule** $v \le \sqrt{d_{max}}/2$ — used as a check on safety/feasibility.
- ☐ **Bucket-wheel unloader** — rotary, continuous, suits free-flowing bulk, capacity up to 4 000 t/h.
- ☐ **Self-unloading barges** — vital for shallow Indian waterways (NW-1, NW-2).
- ☐ **Pump equations** — $Q = Av$ and $P_{pump} = \rho g Q H / \eta$ for liquid cargo.
- ☐ **Selection Triangle** — Capacity ↔ Cost ↔ Cargo Gentleness.
- ☐ **Typical efficiencies** — availability 0.85, utilisation 0.70–0.80, pump η 0.65–0.85.
- ☐ **Always quote units** in $t/h$, $t/yr$, $m^3/h$ — examiners reward dimensional discipline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Onboard Systems - Piping Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Onboard Systems - Piping Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Onboard Piping Systems** on inland water transport vessels (barges, tugs, river-sea cargo vessels, ferries, and dredgers) constitute the integrated network of pipes, valves, pumps, fittings, tanks, and control devices that transport essential fluids — water, fuel, oil, air, and waste — between machinery spaces, hull compartments, and the external environment. These systems are designed in strict compliance with classification society rules (IRS, BV, DNV, Lloyd's Register) and inland waterway authority regulations (IWAI – Inland Waterways Authority of India, and Central Inland Water Transport Corporation – CIWTC).

The piping system is the **"circulatory system"** of a vessel — analogous to blood vessels in a human body. Just as veins and arteries carry blood to organs, shipboard pipes carry fluids to the engines, generators, and auxiliaries that keep the vessel operational.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a large hospital building. It has separate pipe networks for drinking water, sewage, medical gases (oxygen), fire sprinklers, and HVAC cooling. Each network uses different pipe colors, different pressures, and different materials. A vessel follows exactly the same principle — every fluid (sea water, fuel oil, lube oil, sewage, air, steam) gets its **own dedicated pipeline system** with unique design pressure, pipe material, and safety instrumentation.

### 1.3 Why Piping Systems Are Critical on Inland Vessels

Inland vessels operate in **shallow rivers, estuaries, and canal systems** (e.g., Ganges, Brahmaputra, NW-1 National Waterway). Unlike deep-sea ships, they must deal with:

- High sediment load in rivers → **abrasion of sea water piping**
- Shallow drafts → **limited bilge well depth**
- Variable water quality → **corrosion management**
- Confined waterways → **strict pollution control (MARPOL & IWT regulations)**

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Design Pressure (P_des):** **Typically 4–16 bar** for auxiliary systems; **up to 40 bar** for hydraulic systems.
> - **Design Temperature:** **-10 °C to +200 °C** depending on service (steam lines reach 200 °C).
> - **Standard Pipe Sizes (Nominal Bore, NB):** **15 mm to 600 mm** (DN15 to DN600).
> - **Standard Test Pressure:** **1.5 × design pressure** for hydrostatic testing.
> - **Standard Velocity Range:** **1.5–3.0 m/s** for liquids; **15–25 m/s** for compressed air.

### 1.5 Classification of Onboard Piping Systems

| S. No. | System Name | Fluid Carried | Typical Material |
|--------|------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Bilge System | Drainage water, oily water | Galvanized steel / Cu-Ni |
| 2 | Ballast System | Sea/river water | Galvanized steel / Cu-Ni (90/10) |
| 3 | Fuel Oil System | HFO, DO, MDO | Black steel (Sch 40/80) |
| 4 | Lubricating Oil System | Lube oil | Stainless steel / Cu-Ni |
| 5 | Sea Water Cooling | Sea/river water | Cu-Ni 90/10, GRP |
| 6 | Fresh Water Cooling | Treated fresh water | Galvanized steel / SS |
| 7 | Compressed Air | Air (4–30 bar) | Seamless steel |
| 8 | Fire Fighting (CO₂ / Water) | Water, CO₂, foam | Steel / Cu-Ni |
| 9 | Sanitary / Sewage | Black & grey water | PVC, SS, Cu-Ni |
| 10 | Steam System | Saturated steam | Carbon steel (ASTM A106) |
| 11 | Hydraulic System | Hydraulic oil (HLP 46) | SS seamless (high pressure) |
| 12 | Exhaust Gas | Engine exhaust | Mild steel (insulated) |

> [!NOTE]
> Inland vessels (especially those operating on National Waterways under IWAI) frequently use **lighter GRP/PVC piping** for non-critical services to reduce displacement and draft — a major difference from ocean-going vessels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Piping System (Logical Breakdown)

Every piping system, regardless of its service, consists of these fundamental building blocks:

- **Pipes (Tubes):** Main flow carriers, classified by nominal bore (DN) and schedule (Sch).
- **Fittings:** Elbows (90°, 45°), tees, reducers, flanges, couplings.
- **Valves:** Gate, globe, ball, butterfly, check, safety, relief, and non-return valves.
- **Pumps / Compressors:** Energy input devices to drive fluid flow.
- **Strainers & Filters:** Remove particulates (essential on rivers due to silt).
- **Expansion Joints / Bellows:** Compensate for thermal expansion (critical in steam lines).
- **Gauges & Sensors:** Pressure gauges, thermometers, flow meters.
- **Supports / Hangers:** Pipe clips, brackets, and expansion supports every **2–3 m** on average.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter:** Inland vessels face **vibration, hull flexure, and thermal cycling**. A 14 m barge flexes significantly in river currents — pipe supports and flexible joints prevent fatigue fractures.

### 2.2 Major Piping Systems — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Bilge Piping System

**Purpose:** Remove water that accumulates in the bilge well (lowest compartment) due to leaks, condensation, or rain.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Water collects in the **bilge well** (small tank at the lowest hull point).
2. A **float switch** detects high water level.
3. The **bilge pump** (typically 5–25 m³/h for inland vessels) is activated.
4. Water passes through a **strainer** → **bilge manifold** → overboard discharge.
5. **Oily water separator (OWS)** treats oily bilge water to <15 ppm before discharge (MARPOL Annex I compliance).

**Key Components:** Mud box, bilge suction strainer, main bilge line, branch lines, bilge manifold (with international shore connection), oily water separator.

#### 2.2.2 Ballast Piping System

**Purpose:** Adjust vessel draft, trim, and stability by pumping water in/out of dedicated tanks.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Sea chests (with **grating** to block debris — critical for silt-heavy rivers) admit water.
2. **Ballast pump** (typically centrifugal, 50–200 m³/h) pressurizes flow.
3. Water flows through **ballast main** → branch lines → **ballast tanks** (fore-peak, after-peak, wing, double-bottom).
4. Discharge is via **ballast overboard** with non-return valve to prevent backflow.

**River-Specific Design:** In **NW-1 (Ganga)**, ballast tanks often use **GRP lining** to prevent corrosion from brackish water.

#### 2.2.3 Fuel Oil System

**Purpose:** Deliver fuel (HFO / DO / MDO) from storage tanks to the main engine and generator.

**Operational Logic:**
1. **Service tank** (daily use) feeds engine via **fuel transfer pump**.
2. Fuel passes through **fuel oil purifier** (centrifuge, removes water & sediment).
3. **Fuel oil heater** (steam or electric) raises temperature (HFO requires **120–150 °C** for proper viscosity).
4. **Viscosity controller** maintains **15–20 cSt** at engine inlet.
5. **Fine filter** (25–60 μm) protects injectors.
6. Spill-back line returns excess fuel to service tank.

#### 2.2.4 Lubricating Oil System

**Purpose:** Lubricate main engine bearings, cylinder liners, turbochargers.

**Operational Logic:**
1. **Sump tank** → **lube oil pump** → **cooler** (fresh water cooled) → **fine filter** → engine bearings.
2. Spill oil returns to sump via **scavenge pipe**.
3. **Sump heating** maintains oil at **40–50 °C** during standby.

#### 2.2.5 Sea Water Cooling System (SW Cooling)

**Purpose:** Cool main engine jacket, lube oil, charge air, and auxiliary machinery.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Sea chest (low in hull) admits river/sea water.
2. **Sea water pump** (centrifugal) drives flow.
3. Water cools **FW cooler** and **lube oil cooler** (heat exchangers).
4. Discharge back to river/sea via **overboard discharge above waterline**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inland Vessel Note:** Sea water is often replaced by **river water** with high sediment. **Self-cleaning strainers (e.g., Triton-type)** are mandatory to handle **up to 500 mg/l suspended solids** in rivers like the Brahmaputra.

#### 2.2.6 Fire Fighting Piping System

**Purpose:** Provide water, foam, or CO₂ to extinguish fires.

**Operational Logic:**
1. **Fire main** runs along vessel length, always pressurised.
2. **Fire pump** (diesel + electric dual drive) delivers at **~8–10 bar**.
3. **Hydrants** and **hoses** at every 20 m interval.
4. **CO₂ system** for engine room: bottles → manifold → discharge nozzles.
5. **Foam system** for oil fires (in fuel storage area).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Flow rate (volumetric)** | $Q = A \cdot v$ | m³/s | Pipe sizing |
| 2 | **Flow rate (mass)** | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | kg/s | Energy balance |
| 3 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime |
| 4 | **Friction factor (turbulent)** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$ | — | Colebrook-White |
| 5 | **Darcy-Weisbach head loss** | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Pipe friction |
| 6 | **Pressure drop** | $\Delta P = h_f \cdot \rho \cdot g$ | Pa | Pump selection |
| 7 | **Minor losses** | $h_m = K \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Fittings, valves |
| 8 | **Pump power** | $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}$ | W | Power input |
| 9 | **Pipe wall thickness (ASME B31.1)** | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot SE - 1.2 P} + C$ | mm | Pressure design |
| 10 | **Thermal expansion** | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | m | Expansion joints |
| 11 | **Wall drag stress** | $\tau_w = \dfrac{f}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Wall shear |
| 12 | **Hazen-Williams head loss** | $h_f = 10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852} \cdot C^{-1.852} \cdot D^{-4.87}$ | m | Water mains |
| 13 | **Equivalent length (L/D)** | $L_{eq}/D = K/f$ | m/m | Minor loss conversion |
| 14 | **Critical velocity** | $v_c = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho D}$ | m/s | Laminar flow check |
| 15 | **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** | $NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + H_s - h_f$ | m | Cavitation check |

> [!NOTE]
> In **all these formulas**, $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $v$ = velocity (m/s), $D$ = internal diameter (m), $L$ = length (m), $f$ = Darcy friction factor, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $H$ = total head (m), $\eta$ = pump efficiency, $P$ = pressure (Pa), $S$ = allowable stress (MPa), $E$ = joint efficiency, $C$ = corrosion allowance (typically **1.5–3 mm** for inland vessels), $K$ = minor loss coefficient, $C$ (Hazen-Williams) = roughness coefficient (**120–140** for steel).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises. They are the **daily tools of Naval Architects and Marine Engineers** who:
- Size pumps for **tug boats** operating on the Hooghly River.
- Calculate head losses for **long ballast mains** in 90 m cargo barges.
- Determine pipe schedules for **fire fighting systems** on passenger ferries (mandatory under Inland Vessels Rules 2017, India).
- Select materials for **acidic or saline inland waterways** (e.g., Sundarbans, NW-2 Brahmaputra).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Darcy-Weisbach Equation (Foundation of Pipe Sizing)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** This is the single most important equation in marine piping. Every KTU board exam question on pipe flow uses it. The derivation connects energy conservation (Bernoulli) with viscous friction (shear stress on pipe wall).

**Step 1 — Start with Bernoulli's Equation between two sections (1) and (2) of a pipe with no pump/turbine:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2 — For a horizontal constant-diameter pipe ($z_1 = z_2$, $v_1 = v_2$):**

The equation simplifies. All kinetic energy terms cancel, and the head loss is the pressure drop:

$$h_L = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply a force balance on the fluid element in the pipe:**

Consider a cylindrical fluid column of length $L$ and diameter $D$. The pressure force pushing forward minus the pressure force pushing backward, minus the wall shear force, equals zero (steady flow):

$$(P_1 - P_2) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

This equation states that the net pressure force on the cylindrical fluid slug is balanced by the wall friction (shear stress) acting on the pipe's inner surface area.

**Step 4 — Solve for the pressure drop term:**

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $\rho g$ to convert to head:**

$$h_f = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{\rho g D}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the dimensionless Fanning friction factor:**

The Fanning friction factor $f_{Fanning}$ relates wall shear stress to dynamic pressure:

$$\tau_w = f_{Fanning} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

Substitute this into the head expression:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f_{Fanning} \cdot \rho v^2 \cdot L}{2 \cdot \rho g D}$$

Cancel $\rho$ from numerator and denominator:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f_{Fanning} L v^2}{2 g D} = \frac{2 f_{Fanning} L v^2}{g D}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Darcy (Moody) friction factor:**

In engineering practice, the Darcy friction factor $f$ is **4 times** the Fanning friction factor:

$$f_{Darcy} = 4 \cdot f_{Fanning}$$

Substitute $f = 4 f_{Fanning}$, which means $f_{Fanning} = f/4$:

$$h_f = \frac{2 \cdot (f/4) \cdot L \cdot v^2}{g D} = \frac{f L v^2}{2 g D}$$

**Final Darcy-Weisbach Equation:**

$$\boxed{h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}}$$

This equation is the **engineering standard** for computing frictional head loss in ship piping.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Bilge Pipe Sizing for an Inland Barge

**Given Data:**
- Vessel: Inland cargo barge operating on NW-1 (Ganga).
- Bilge pump capacity: $Q = 25 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$.
- Pipe length (suction + discharge): $L = 30 \, \text{m}$.
- Pipe material: Galvanized steel, roughness $\varepsilon = 0.15 \, \text{mm}$.
- Water temperature: 30 °C → $\rho = 995 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.
- Minor loss coefficients (strainer, elbows, valves): $\Sigma K = 5.0$.
- Allowable head loss: $h_{max} = 4 \, \text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Assume a trial diameter $D = 80 \, \text{mm} = 0.08 \, \text{m}$.**

**Step 2 — Convert flow to SI units:**

$$Q = \frac{25}{3600} = 6.944 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.08^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute flow velocity:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{6.944 \times 10^{-3}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.381 \, \text{m/s}$$

This is within the recommended velocity range (1.5–3.0 m/s), so the diameter is acceptable from a velocity standpoint.

**Step 5 — Compute Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{995 \times 1.381 \times 0.08}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.374 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 6 — Determine relative roughness:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.15 \times 10^{-3}}{0.08} = 1.875 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7 — Use Colebrook-White to find $f$ (iterative, or Moody chart):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \frac{1.875 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.374 \times 10^{5} \cdot \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

After iteration: $f \approx 0.026$.

**Step 8 — Compute major (friction) head loss:**

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} = 0.026 \times \frac{30}{0.08} \times \frac{1.381^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.026 \times 375 \times \frac{1.907}{19.62} = 0.026 \times 375 \times 0.0972 = 0.948 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 9 — Compute minor head losses:**

$$h_m = \Sigma K \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} = 5.0 \times \frac{1.381^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 5.0 \times 0.0972 = 0.486 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 10 — Total head loss:**

$$h_{total} = h_f + h_m = 0.948 + 0.486 = 1.434 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 11 — Verification:**

$$h_{total} = 1.434 \, \text{m} < h_{max} = 4 \, \text{m} \quad \checkmark$$

The 80 mm diameter is adequate. If the head loss had exceeded 4 m, the engineer would re-size to 100 mm and re-iterate.

### 3.3 Derivation: Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) Available

To prevent **cavitation** in a centrifugal pump (critical for fuel and bilge pumps on inland vessels due to high water temperature in summer):

**Step 1 — Define NPSH Available (NPSH_a):**

$$NPSH_a = H_{atm} + H_{static} - H_{friction} - H_{vapor}$$

where:
- $H_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure head = **10.33 m of fresh water** (sea level).
- $H_{static}$ = static suction head (positive if liquid above pump, negative if below).
- $H_{friction}$ = friction head loss in suction line.
- $H_{vapor}$ = vapor pressure head of liquid at operating temperature.

**Step 2 — Convert all terms to metres of liquid column using the standard relation $H = P/(\rho g)$:**

For example, at 30 °C, $P_{vapor} \approx 4.24 \, \text{kPa}$:

$$H_{vapor} = \frac{4.24 \times 10^3}{995 \times 9.81} = 0.434 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Cavitation Prevention Criterion:**

$$NPSH_a \geq NPSH_{required} + \text{Safety margin (typically } 0.5 \text{ m)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For inland vessels operating in **hot tropical rivers** (e.g., Godavari in summer, 35 °C), vapor pressure increases. Pump installation depth must be carefully calculated to avoid cavitation-induced erosion of impeller blades.

### 3.4 Derivation: Pipe Wall Thickness (ASME B31.1 — Power Piping)

For straight pipe under internal pressure:

**Step 1 — Hoop stress equation:**

The hoop (circumferential) stress in a thin-walled cylinder is:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t}$$

**Step 2 — Allowable stress design:**

Setting hoop stress equal to allowable stress $S$ divided by a safety factor (incorporated in $S$ for code):

$$S = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for thickness:**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 S}$$

**Step 4 — Add joint efficiency $E$ and corrosion allowance $C$:**

The ASME B31.1 code introduces efficiency (for welded joints, $E = 0.7$ to $1.0$) and corrosion allowance:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 S E - 1.2 P} + C}$$

The factor **1.2** in the denominator accounts for the additional longitudinal stress contribution per code requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** A fuel oil line with $P = 10 \, \text{bar} = 1 \, \text{MPa}$, $D = 100 \, \text{mm} = 0.1 \, \text{m}$, $S = 100 \, \text{MPa}$, $E = 0.85$, $C = 2 \, \text{mm}$:
>
> $$t = \frac{1 \times 100}{2 \times 100 \times 0.85 - 1.2 \times 1} + 2 = \frac{100}{170 - 1.2} + 2 = \frac{100}{168.8} + 2 = 0.592 + 2 = 2.59 \, \text{mm}$$
>
> Therefore, **schedule 40 (3.91 mm)** pipe is selected.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical Pipe Materials vs Service

| Service | Material | Why Selected | Typical Schedule | Max Temp |
|---------|----------|--------------|-----------------|----------|
| Bilge | Galvanized steel | Corrosion resistance | Sch 40 | 60 °C |
| Ballast | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Biofouling resistance | Sch 40 | 80 °C |
| Fuel oil | Carbon steel ASTM A106 | Strength, economy | Sch 80 | 200 °C |
| Lube oil | Stainless steel 304 | Cleanliness | Sch 40 | 120 °C |
| SW cooling | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Salt water resistance | Sch 40 | 50 °C |
| FW cooling | Galvanized steel | Cost-effective | Sch 40 | 80 °C |
| Compressed air | Seamless carbon steel | High pressure | Sch 80 | 150 °C |
| Steam | Carbon steel A106 Gr.B | High temperature | Sch 80 | 250 °C |
| Sanitary | PVC / SS 316 | Hygiene, lightweight | Sch 40 | 50 °C |
| Hydraulic | SS 316 seamless | High pressure (250 bar) | Sch 160 | 80 °C |
| Exhaust | Mild steel (insulated) | Heat resistance | Sch 40 | 500 °C |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Typical Onboard Piping Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sea Chest River Inlet] --> B1[Sea Water Pump]
    B1 --> C1[Self Cleaning Strainer]

    D1 --> F1[Charge Air Cooler]
    E1 --> G1[Overboard Discharge]
    F1 --> G1
    A1 --> H1[Ballast Pump]
    H1 --> I1[Ballast Tank Fore]
    H1 --> J1[Ballast Tank Aft]
    H1 --> K1[Ballast Tank Wing]
    I1 --> L1[Ballast Overboard]
    J1 --> L1
    K1 --> L1
    M1[Bilge Well Lowest Point] --> N1[Bilge Strainer]
    N1 --> O1[Bilge Pump]
    O1 --> P1[Oily Water Separator]
    P1 --> Q1[Overboard Discharge]
    P1 --> R1[Sludge Tank]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**List any six major piping systems found on board an inland cargo barge and state the primary function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Bilge Piping System** – Removes water accumulating in the bilge well due to leakage or condensation.
2. **Ballast Piping System** – Controls vessel draft and trim by pumping water in/out of ballast tanks.
3. **Fuel Oil Piping System** – Transports fuel (HFO/DO) from storage tanks to the main engine and auxiliaries.
4. **Lubricating Oil Piping System** – Supplies lube oil to engine bearings and cylinder liners.
5. **Sea Water Cooling System** – Cools main engine jacket, lube oil, and charge air using river/sea water.
6. **Fire Fighting Piping System** – Delivers water, foam, or CO₂ to suppress fires.
7. **(Optional) Compressed Air System** – Provides starting air for engines and service air.
8. **(Optional) Sanitary/Sewage System** – Collects and treats black & grey water.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Why is Cu-Ni 90/10 alloy commonly preferred for sea water piping on inland vessels operating on silt-laden rivers?**

**Model Answer:**

Cu-Ni 90/10 (90% copper, 10% nickel) is preferred because:
- **Excellent corrosion resistance** in brackish and saline water.
- **Resistance to biofouling** — the copper ions inhibit marine growth.
- **Good erosion resistance** against silt and suspended solids (important for rivers like Ganga and Brahmaputra).
- **High thermal conductivity**, suitable for coolers and heat exchangers.
- **Reasonable cost** compared to titanium or stainless steel.
- It is also the standard material recommended by **classification societies (IRS, BV, DNV)** for sea water systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse Darcy friction factor $f$ with Fanning friction factor ($f_{Fanning} = f_{Darcy}/4$). Marks are specifically deducted for this.
> - **Always include units** in every step of numerical answers.
> - In pipe-sizing problems, **state your assumed velocity range** at the start (typically 1.5–3.0 m/s for liquids).
> - **Show iteration steps** when solving Colebrook-White equation — examiners want to see your reasoning.
> - For inland vessels, **mention river-specific adaptations** (e.g., self-cleaning strainers, GRP lining) — this shows applied knowledge and earns extra credit.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*:

**(a)** Draw a neat block diagram of a typical **fuel oil piping system** for a main engine on an inland cargo vessel. Label all major components and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bilge pump delivers **30 m³/h** of water through a galvanized steel suction-discharge line of total equivalent length **40 m** (including fittings). The pipe diameter is **100 mm**, water temperature is **30 °C**, and the kinematic viscosity is $\nu = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Take the absolute roughness of galvanized steel as **0.15 mm**. Calculate:
  - (i) The flow velocity and Reynolds number.
  - (ii) The Darcy friction factor using the Colebrook-White equation.
  - (iii) The total head loss (major + minor, with $\Sigma K = 4.0$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fuel Oil System Block Diagram:**

```
Fuel Storage Tank (HFO)
        ↓
Fuel Transfer Pump
        ↓
Fuel Purifier (Centrifuge)
        ↓
Fuel Service Tank (Daily Use)
        ↓
Fuel Booster Pump
        ↓
Fuel Heater (Steam or Electric, 120–150 °C)
        ↓
Viscosity Controller (15–20 cSt)
        ↓
Fine Filter (25–60 μm)
        ↓
       ┌────────────┬────────────┐
       ↓            ↓            ↓
Main Engine   Aux Generator   Boiler
       ↓            ↓
       └─── Spill Back Line ───┐
                               ↓
                  Back to Service Tank
```

**Function of each component:**
- **Fuel Storage Tank** – Bulk storage of HFO with heating coils.
- **Fuel Transfer Pump** – Transfers fuel to settling tank.
- **Fuel Purifier** – Removes water and sediment via centrifugal separation.
- **Fuel Service Tank** – Daily use tank, decanted and clean.
- **Fuel Booster Pump** – Pressurises fuel to engine injection pressure.
- **Fuel Heater** – Reduces viscosity of HFO for proper atomisation.
- **Viscosity Controller** – Regulates heater output to maintain constant viscosity.
- **Fine Filter** – Final safeguard for injectors.
- **Spill Back Line** – Returns excess fuel, preventing dead-heading of pump.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $L = 40 \, \text{m}$, $D = 100 \, \text{mm} = 0.1 \, \text{m}$, $\nu = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $\varepsilon = 0.15 \, \text{mm}$, $\Sigma K = 4.0$.

**(i) Velocity and Reynolds Number:**

Convert flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{30}{3600} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.1^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

Velocity:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.061 \, \text{m/s}$$

Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu} = \frac{1.061 \times 0.1}{0.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.326 \times 10^{5}$$

Flow is **turbulent** since $Re > 4000$.

**(ii) Darcy Friction Factor:**

Relative roughness:

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.15 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

Colebrook-White equation:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.326 \times 10^{5} \cdot \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

**Iteration 1:** Assume $f = 0.025$, then $\sqrt{f} = 0.1581$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( 4.054 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{2.51}{1.326 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1581} \right)$$

$$= -2 \log_{10}\left( 4.054 \times 10^{-4} + 1.197 \times 10^{-4} \right) = -2 \log_{10}\left( 5.251 \times 10^{-4} \right)$$

$$= -2 \times (-3.2797) = 6.559 \Rightarrow \sqrt{f} = 0.1524 \Rightarrow f = 0.0232$$

**Iteration 2:** Using $f = 0.0232$, $\sqrt{f} = 0.1524$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( 4.054 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{2.51}{1.326 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1524} \right)$$

$$= -2 \log_{10}\left( 4.054 \times 10^{-4} + 1.242 \times 10^{-4} \right) = -2 \log_{10}\left( 5.296 \times 10^{-4} \right)$$

$$= -2 \times (-3.276) = 6.552 \Rightarrow \sqrt{f} = 0.1526 \Rightarrow f = 0.0233$$

**Converged: $f \approx 0.0233$**

**(iii) Total Head Loss:**

Major (friction) loss:

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} = 0.0233 \times \frac{40}{0.1} \times \frac{1.061^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$= 0.0233 \times 400 \times \frac{1.126}{19.62} = 0.0233 \times 400 \times 0.0574 = 0.535 \, \text{m}$$

Minor losses:

$$h_m = \Sigma K \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} = 4.0 \times 0.0574 = 0.230 \, \text{m}$$

Total head loss:

$$h_{total} = h_f + h_m = 0.535 + 0.230 = 0.765 \, \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** Total head loss = **0.765 m** (approximately **7.5 kPa** pressure drop).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*:

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **ballast piping system** of an inland cargo vessel. What specific design modifications are required for vessels operating on **silt-laden rivers** like the Ganga? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fire fighting pump delivers **50 m³/h** of fresh water through a fire main of length **80 m** and diameter **150 mm**. The pipe is galvanized steel ($\varepsilon = 0.15 \, \text{mm}$). The water temperature is **25 °C** ($\rho = 997 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$). Calculate:
  - (i) The flow velocity and Reynolds number.
  - (ii) The Darcy friction factor using the Moody chart or Colebrook-White.
  - (iii) The pressure at the pump discharge (assume pump head is 30 m, minor losses are 15% of friction losses). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ballast Piping System:**

```
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │         BALLAST PIPING SYSTEM            │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
                         ↓
                Sea Chest (with Grate)
                         ↓
              Sea Water Strainer
                         ↓
                Ballast Pump
                         ↓
              Ballast Manifold
        ┌──────────┬─────┴─────┬───────────┐
        ↓          ↓           ↓           ↓
   Fore Peak    Wing       Double       After Peak
    Tank        Tank       Bottom          Tank
               (Port)       Tank
               (Stbd)
        ↓          ↓           ↓           ↓
        └──────────┴─────┬─────┴───────────┘
                         ↓
              Ballast Overboard Valve
                         ↓
                 Discharge to River
```

**Function of each component:** Sea chest (water inlet with grate to block debris) → Strainer (filters large particles) → Ballast pump (centrifugal, 50–200 m³/h) → Ballast manifold (distributes to multiple tanks) → Ballast tanks (varying from 8% to 25% of vessel's DWT) → Overboard discharge with non-return valve.

**River-Specific Design Modifications:**

1. **Silt Management:** Heavy-duty **self-cleaning strainers (Triton-type)** with backflushing to handle suspended solids up to 500 mg/l.
2. **Larger Sea Chest Grates:** Slower flow velocities (0.3 m/s) prevent silt ingress.
3. **GRP-Lined Ballast Tanks:** Protect against corrosion from brackish water.
4. **Anti-Siltation Valves:** Periodic flushing capability.
5. **Higher Pump Head Margin:** To overcome additional friction from silt deposits.
6. **Cathodic Protection:** Zinc or aluminium anodes to combat corrosion in low-resistivity river water.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 50 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $L = 80 \, \text{m}$, $D = 150 \, \text{mm} = 0.15 \, \text{m}$, $\rho = 997 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\varepsilon = 0.15 \, \text{mm}$, pump head = 30 m, minor losses = 15% of major.

**(i) Velocity and Reynolds Number:**

$$Q = \frac{50}{3600} = 1.389 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 0.15^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.389 \times 10^{-2}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.786 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{997 \times 0.786 \times 0.15}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.321 \times 10^{5}$$

**(ii) Friction Factor:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.15 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

Colebrook-White (iterative solution):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.321 \times 10^{5} \cdot \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

**Iteration:** $f \approx 0.022$ (converges after 2 iterations).

**(iii) Pump Discharge Pressure:**

Major head loss:

$$h_f = 0.022 \times \frac{80}{0.15} \times \frac{0.786^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.022 \times 533.3 \times 0.0315 = 0.369 \, \text{m}$$

Minor losses (15% of major):

$$h_m = 0.15 \times 0.369 = 0.0554 \, \text{m}$$

Total friction loss:

$$h_{total} = 0.369 + 0.0554 = 0.424 \, \text{m}$$

Pressure at pump discharge:

$$P_{discharge} = \rho g (H_{pump} - h_{total}) = 997 \times 9.81 \times (30 - 0.424)$$

$$P_{discharge} = 997 \times 9.81 \times 29.576 = 289{,}300 \, \text{Pa} \approx 2.89 \, \text{bar}$$

**Final Answer:** Pump discharge pressure ≈ **2.89 bar (gauge)**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Onboard Piping Systems**

- [x] **Bilge System** uses **centrifugal pump + oily water separator (OWS)** — discharge must be <15 ppm oil (MARPOL).
- [x] **Ballast System** uses **sea chest with grate** to prevent silt ingress — critical on rivers.
- [x] **Fuel Oil System** uses **purifier + heater + viscosity controller (15–20 cSt)** — HFO heated to 120–150 °C.
- [x] **Lub Oil System** uses **sump tank + cooler + fine filter** — maintains 40–50 °C standby temperature.
- [x] **SW Cooling System** uses **Cu-Ni 90/10 pipes** + self-cleaning strainers for silt-laden rivers.
- [x] **Fire Fighting System** uses **dual pumps (diesel + electric)** at 8–10 bar pressure.
- [x] **Compressed Air System** delivers **30 bar starting air** + 7 bar service air.
- [x] **Steam System** uses **ASTM A106 Gr.B carbon steel** at 200–250 °C.
- [x] **Hydraulic System** uses **SS 316 seamless (Sch 160)** for 250 bar pressures.
- [x] **Sanitary System** uses **PVC/SS316** — discharge BOD <5 ppm for eco-sensitive inland zones.

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**

$$\text{1. } Q = A \cdot v \quad \text{2. } Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} \quad \text{3. } h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g} \quad \text{4. } P_{pump} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta} \quad \text{5. } t = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 1.2 P} + C$$

**Critical Constants:**
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (approx.)
- Recommended velocity: 1.5–3.0 m/s (liquids), 15–25 m/s (air)
- Corrosion allowance: **1.5–3 mm** for inland vessels
- Hazen-Williams $C$ for steel: **120–140**

**Inland Vessel Special Features (Often Asked in KTU):**
- **Self-cleaning strainers** for high-silt rivers.
- **GRP lining** for ballast tanks in brackish water.
- **Tidal window operation** for ballast timing.
- **Stricter OWS** due to eco-sensitive waterways.
- **Lower draft** → smaller bilge well → more frequent bilge pump operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Fighting Appliances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Fighting Appliances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Fire Fighting Appliances* on an inland water transport vessel comprise the entire integrated suite of **fixed installations** (e.g., fixed fire-extinguishing systems using CO₂, foam, or water-spray) and **portable/mobile appliances** (e.g., extinguishers, hoses, breathing apparatus, fireman's outfits) designed to detect, contain, suppress, and extinguish fires arising from machinery, cargo, galley, or electrical compartments, in compliance with the *Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)*, *Classification Societies* (IRS, BV, DNV), and the *Indian Boat Rules / Inland Vessels (IV) Act, 1917*.

In technical maritime parlance, the term **"Fire Appliance"** is broader than just extinguishers — it encapsulates the *complete fire safety ecosystem* including:

- **Detection systems** (smoke/heat detectors, flame sensors)
- **Alarm systems** (manual call points, sounders)
- **Suppression systems** (fixed + portable)
- **Personal protective equipment (PPE)** for the crew
- **Escape arrangements** (EBA – Emergency Breathing Apparatus)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Floating Hospital ER" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine an inland vessel as a small floating town. A fire on a vessel is akin to a medical emergency in a remote hospital. The *fire detectors* are the diagnostic sensors (like a heart monitor), the *alarm system* is the emergency siren, the *extinguishers* are the medicines (each suitable for a different "ailment" or fire class), and the *fireman's outfit* is the doctor's surgical gown. Just as a doctor must choose the right medicine for a fever vs. a fracture, a seafarer must select the right extinguisher for an electrical fire vs. an oil fire.

## 1.3 Key Standards & Statutory Benchmarks

| Governing Body / Standard | Jurisdiction | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **IWAI** – Inland Waterways Authority of India | India | National waterways regulation |
| **IV Act 1917 / Inland Vessels Rules** | India | Statutory fire appliance minima |
| **SOLAS Chapter II-2** | International | Convention for Safety of Life at Sea (coastal vessels) |
| **IMO Resolution A.602(15)** | International | Fire-extinguishing media selection |
| **IRS Rules & Regulations** | Indian Register of Shipping | Classification rules for vessels > 24 m |
| **NFPA 10** | USA | Portable extinguisher standard (often referenced) |
| **ISO 3941** | International | Classification of fires |

## 1.4 The "Fire Triangle" — Foundamental to Appliance Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every fire requires three components simultaneously — **Fuel, Heat, and Oxygen** (the Fire Triangle). Fire fighting appliances are engineered to **remove at least one side** of this triangle. For example:
> - **Water** → cools (removes heat)
> - **CO₂** → smothers (displaces oxygen)
> - **Foam** → separates fuel from oxygen
> - **Dry Powder** → interrupts the chemical chain reaction

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fires (ISO 3941)

Understanding fire classes is **mandatory** before selecting any appliance. The international classification is:

| Class | Fuel Type | Vessel-Specific Examples | Correct Medium |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Solid combustibles (wood, paper, fabric) | Crew cabins, chart room, bedding | **Water, Foam, Dry Powder** |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids (diesel, lube oil, paints) | Engine room bilges, fuel purifiers | **Foam, CO₂, Dry Powder, BCF** |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (LPG, acetylene) | Gas cylinder lockers, welding shops | **Dry Powder ONLY** |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals (Mg, Na, K) | Rare on vessels, mostly shore | **Special dry powder** |
| **Class E** (or **F** in EN) | Electrical / Cooking oil | Switchboards, galley deep fryers | **CO₂, Dry Powder** |

## 2.2 Portable Fire Extinguishers — Detailed Classification

### 2.2.1 Water-Type Extinguisher

- **Working medium:** Fresh water + 0.5 % wetting agent (or anti-freeze).
- **Operating pressure:** Stored at **1.0 MPa (10 bar)** with nitrogen propellant.
- **Mechanism:** Quenching — removes the **heat side** of the fire triangle.
- **Limitation:** ⚠ **NEVER use on Class B/C/E fires** (risk of explosion and fire spread).

### 2.2.2 Foam Extinguisher (AFFF – Aqueous Film-Forming Foam)

- **Working medium:** AFFF concentrate (typically **3 % or 6 %** premix in water) + Nitrogen.
- **Mechanism:** Forms a blanket that **seals fuel from atmospheric oxygen** and cools simultaneously.
- **Application:** Superior on **bunker fuel spills, lubricating oil**, and bilge fires.
- **Capacity on inland barges:** Generally **9 L, 45 L (trolley-mounted)**.

### 2.2.3 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguisher

- **Working medium:** Liquid CO₂ at **5.7 MPa (57 bar)** stored in seamless steel cylinders.
- **Mechanism:** Smothering — reduces ambient O₂ concentration below the **15 % threshold** required for combustion.
- **Vessel-specific note:** ✅ **Preferred for engine rooms, paint stores, electrical compartments** because it leaves **zero residue**.
- **Discharge time:** Standard portable = **> 8 seconds**; typical capacity **2, 4.5, 6.8 kg**.

### 2.2.4 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Extinguisher

- **Working medium:** Monoammonium phosphate (ABC powder) or sodium bicarbonate (BC).
- **Particle size:** **10 – 75 µm**.
- **Mechanism:** **Chemical chain inhibition** — the powder radicals interrupt the combustion reaction at the molecular level.
- **Universal applicability:** Effective on **Class A, B, C, and E** fires — the closest thing to a "universal" extinguisher on a vessel.

### 2.2.5 BCF / Halon Alternatives (Clean Agent)

- Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) such as **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** or **NOVEC 1230** are increasingly replacing **Halon 1301 (CBrClF₂)** under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** due to Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP).
- **Mechanism:** Chemical chain interruption + heat absorption.
- **Application:** Engine room fixed flooding systems.

## 2.3 Fixed Fire-Extinguishing Systems (FFES)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Inland vessels above a certain tonnage (typically vessels with engine power > 750 kW or carrying > 100 passengers) require **fixed fire-extinguishing installations** in machinery spaces.

### 2.3.1 Fixed CO₂ System

- **Cylinders:** Seamless steel at **5.7 MPa**, manifolded.
- **Quantity calculation:** based on the **gross volume** of the protected space.

### 2.3.2 High-Expansion Foam System

- Foam expansion ratio of **400:1 to 1000:1**.
- Fills the entire machinery space within **3 – 5 minutes** for class B fires.

### 2.3.3 Water-Spray / Mist System

- Fine droplets (typically **< 1 mm** diameter) cool the fire and inert the local atmosphere.
- Superior over conventional sprinklers due to lower water consumption.

## 2.4 Fire Hoses, Nozzles, Hydrants & Hydrant System

- **Hose diameter (vessel standard):** **38 mm (1.5 in)** or **65 mm (2.5 in)**.
- **Material:** Rubber-lined canvas or synthetic polymer (PVC/NBR), burst pressure **≥ 4 MPa**.
- **Nozzle types:** *Jet nozzle* (reach up to **10 – 12 m**), *Spray nozzle*, *Fog/spray dual nozzle* (preferred for hydrocarbon fires).
- **Hydrant pressure:** Minimum **0.25 MPa** at the most remote hydrant (IRS requirement).

## 2.5 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) — Fireman's Outfit

As per **SOLAS Reg. II-2/17** and equivalent IWAI rules, each vessel must maintain a complete fireman's outfit comprising:

1. **Bunker suit** (proximity suit) — aluminised, heat-resistant to **800 °C** for ≥ 30 seconds.
2. **Long boots** — rubber, electrical-insulation rated to **1 kV**.
3. **Helmet** with face visor.
4. **Gloves** — heat and cut resistant.
5. **Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)** — air cylinder **30 MPa**, duration **≥ 30 min**.
6. **Fire axe** — non-sparking (beryllium-copper or bronze).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the consolidated engineering formula sheet for fire-appliance sizing, CO₂ quantity, foam proportioning, and discharge time calculations. All variables are dimensionally consistent.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume of CO₂ required (fixed system) | $V_{CO_2} = 0.56 \cdot V_{gross}$ (machinery space cargo equivalent) | $V$ in m³, result in kg |
| 2 | Free-air equivalent of liquid CO₂ | $V_{air} = 0.56 \cdot m_{CO_2}$ | kg → m³ of gas at NTP |
| 3 | Foam solution volume | $V_{sol} = A_{spill} \cdot d_{app} \cdot t_{dis}$ | $d_{app}$ = application rate (L/min·m²) |
| 4 | Foam concentrate volume | $V_{conc} = V_{sol} \cdot C_{perc}$ | $C_{perc}$ = 0.03 or 0.06 |
| 5 | Extinguisher discharge time (portable) | $t_{dis} = \dfrac{m_{agent}}{\dot{m}_{agent}}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Nozzle discharge velocity (jet) | $v_j = C_v \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho_w}$ | $C_v$ = velocity coefficient ≈ 0.97 |
| 7 | Hydrant flow continuity | $Q = A \cdot v$ | m³/s |
| 8 | SCBA air duration | $t_{air} = \dfrac{P_{cyl} \cdot V_{cyl}}{\dot{V}_{breath}}$ | min, $\dot{V}_{breath}$ ≈ 40 L/min |
| 9 | Effective range of jet | $R \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_w \cdot g}} \cdot \sqrt{H}$ | m |
| 10 | Heat absorption (water mist) | $Q_{abs} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{sat} - T_{0})$ | kW, $c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/kg·K |

**Boundary conditions used in KTU exam problems:**
- Free-air CO₂ concentration needed to inert a machinery space: **≥ 34 %** (IMO standard).
- Discharge of CO₂ fixed system: **must complete within ≤ 2 min** in machinery spaces.
- Foam application rate for Class B: **≥ 6.5 L/min·m²** (high-expansion foam on hydrocarbon pool).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards** use these principles to design and audit fire-safety plans for new inland vessels and barges.
- **Port authorities (IWAI, Cochin Port Trust, V.O. Chidambaranar Port)** require fire-plan approval before issuing operational licences.
- **Classification surveyors (IRS)** physically inspect extinguishers, hoses, SCBA cylinders annually — a **hydrostatic test at 1.5 × working pressure every 5 years** is mandatory for portable extinguishers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Fixed CO₂ Quantity for Machinery Space

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IMO/IRS standard method for sizing the **fixed CO₂ flooding system** is one of the most frequently asked derivations in KTU ESE papers. The complete derivation is presented below.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> Calculate the mass of liquid CO₂ required to inert a vessel machinery space of **length L = 18 m**, **breadth B = 8 m**, **height H = 5 m**. Assume **30 % of the space contains machinery** (volumetric steelwork deduction included).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Gross volume of the protected space:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{gross} &= L \cdot B \cdot H \\
&= 18 \,\text{m} \times 8 \,\text{m} \times 5 \,\text{m} \\
&= 720 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Net volume available for CO₂ infiltration:**

For machinery spaces, only **40 % of the gross volume** is typically filled with gas due to engine blocks, funnels, and structural steelwork. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{net} &= V_{gross} \cdot \eta_{eff} \\
&= 720 \times 0.40 \\
&= 288 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **efficiency factor $\eta_{eff}$** accounts for machinery blockages.

**Step 3 — Required free-air equivalent of CO₂:**

A minimum CO₂ concentration of **34 %** by volume is required to inert a hydrocarbon fire atmosphere. Hence the required gas volume at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{air,\,CO_2} &= V_{net} \cdot f_{conc} \\
&= 288 \,\text{m}^3 \times 0.34 \\
&= 97.92 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion of free-air gas volume to liquid CO₂ mass:**

At NTP, **1 kg of liquid CO₂** liberates **0.56 m³** of free gas (this is the standard thermodynamic factor used by classification societies).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2} &= \frac{V_{air,\,CO_2}}{0.56 \,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}} \\
&= \frac{97.92}{0.56} \\
&= 174.86 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering safety margin:**

Apply a **20 % reserve margin** to account for leakage past bulkheads and door seals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2,\,final} &= m_{CO_2} \cdot (1 + \xi) \\
&= 174.86 \times 1.20 \\
&= 209.83 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\xi$ = 0.20.

**Step 6 — Cylinder selection:**

Standard marine CO₂ cylinders have a water capacity of **67 L each**, holding **45 kg** of liquid CO₂ at 5.7 MPa. The number of cylinders required is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{cyl} &= \left\lceil \frac{m_{CO_2,\,final}}{45 \,\text{kg}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{209.83}{45} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 4.66 \rceil \\
&= 5 \,\text{cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Verification by Dimensional Consistency

$$
[m_{CO_2}] = \frac{[\text{m}^3]}{[\text{m}^3 / \text{kg}]} = \text{kg} \;\; \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Foam Concentrate Volume for Bilge Fire

**Given:** A bilge area of **A = 4 m²** with a diesel pool fire. Standard application rate for high-expansion foam on Class B is **d_app = 6.5 L/min·m²** and discharge time **t_dis = 5 min**. Foam concentrate ratio **C_perc = 3 %**.

**Step 1 — Foam solution volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sol} &= A \cdot d_{app} \cdot t_{dis} \\
&= 4 \times 6.5 \times 5 \\
&= 130 \,\text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Foam concentrate volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{conc} &= V_{sol} \cdot C_{perc} \\
&= 130 \times 0.03 \\
&= 3.9 \,\text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total foam volume (after expansion):**

For high-expansion foam at expansion ratio **E = 800:1**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{foam} &= V_{sol} \cdot E \\
&= 130 \times 800 \\
&= 104{,}000 \,\text{L} = 104 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is enough foam to blanket the **18 m × 8 m × 5 m = 720 m³** machinery space to a depth of **≈ 14 cm** — adequate for inertion.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Appliance Selection Decision Table

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is what KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in the exam. Memorise the placement rules.

| Protected Space | Required Appliance(s) | Quantity (typical inland vessel) |
|---|---|---|
| Wheelhouse | CO₂ 5 kg + Dry powder 4 kg | 1 each |
| Machinery space (main) | Fixed CO₂ + foam applicator + portable CO₂ 6.8 kg | 1 system + 2 portable |
| Engine room bilge | Foam (9 L) | 1 |
| Galley / Pantry | Wet chemical / CO₂ 2 kg | 1 |
| Accommodation | Water 9 L | 1 per 30 m of corridor |
| Paint locker | CO₂ (large portable or fixed) | 1 |
| Battery room | CO₂ (NOT dry powder — avoid corrosion) | 1 |
| Wheelhouse electrical panel | CO₂ 2 kg | 1 |
| Cargo deck (oil barges) | Foam trolley 45 L + dry powder | 1 + 1 |
| Lifeboat / rescue craft | CO₂ 2 kg | 1 |

## 3.4 Analytical Question — Heat Transfer Through PPE

A fireman's bunker suit has **aluminised outer fabric with reflectivity ρ = 0.85** and is exposed to **T_rad = 1200 °C** flames. Approximate the net radiative heat flux received by the body, given ambient **T_skin = 36 °C**.

The Stefan-Boltzmann radiation exchange:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \rho \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{rad}^{\,4} - T_{skin}^{\,4}) \\
&= 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[(1473)^4 - (309)^4\right] \\
&= 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (4.708 \times 10^{12} - 9.11 \times 10^{9}) \\
&= 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.699 \times 10^{12} \\
&\approx 22.65 \,\text{kW/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W}/\text{m}^2\,\text{K}^4$ is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.

The reflection reduces the absorbed flux to **≈ 15 %** of the incident flux, which a properly rated bunker suit can withstand for **30 – 60 seconds** (acceptable for rescue missions).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between stored-pressure and cartridge-operated fire extinguishers. State one application area for each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Stored-pressure type:** Both the extinguishing agent and the propellant gas (nitrogen, 1.0 MPa) are stored in the **same cylinder**. The extinguisher is pressurised at all times, and a pressure gauge indicates readiness. ✅ *Application:* small portable extinguishers on accommodation decks (e.g., 9 L foam, 5 kg CO₂).
- **Cartridge-operated type:** The extinguishing agent is stored in the cylinder **without pressure**, and the propellant gas is contained in a **separate small cartridge** that is punctured only on activation. ✅ *Application:* large trolley-mounted DCP or foam units in engine rooms.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List the components of a "Fireman's Outfit" as required on board an inland vessel.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Bunker suit / proximity suit** (aluminised, fire-resistant).
2. **Long rubber boots** (anti-slip, dielectrically rated).
3. **Helmet** with visor and neck flap.
4. **Heat-resistant gloves.**
5. **Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)** with spare cylinder.
6. **Fire axe** (non-sparking, bronze/beryllium-copper).
7. **Electric safety torch** (intrinsically safe).
8. **Lifeline** (fire-resistant, length ≥ 30 m).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a)** With the aid of a clearly labelled schematic, explain the working of a **fixed CO₂ fire-extinguishing system** installed in the machinery space of an inland cargo vessel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A machinery space of dimensions **20 m × 10 m × 6 m** has **35 % effective free volume**. Calculate (i) the volume of CO₂ gas required for inertion, (ii) the mass of liquid CO₂ to be stored, and (iii) the number of standard 45 kg cylinders needed. Apply a **20 % safety margin**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A(a)

A fixed CO₂ system comprises the following sub-systems:

1. **Storage bank:** A bank of seamless steel cylinders (each 45 kg of liquid CO₂ at 5.7 MPa) manifolded together.
2. **Actuating station:** Located *outside* the protected space for safety. Includes a **pilot cylinder** that triggers the discharge lever.
3. **Distribution piping:** High-pressure steel piping with valves that open sequentially.
4. **Nozzles:** Located at the *top* of the protected space to ensure **uniform downward flooding**.
5. **Time-delay alarm:** A 30-second pre-discharge bell to evacuate personnel.
6. **Ventilation shut-off & fire dampers:** Automatically close to seal the space.

**Working principle:** When the system is activated, the pilot cylinder opens the main cylinder valves. Liquid CO₂ flows through the piping, vaporises at the nozzles, and **fills the space with gaseous CO₂** at a concentration of **≥ 34 %**, lowering O₂ to **≤ 14 %**, which smothers the fire.

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A(b)

**(i) Effective free volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{free} &= 20 \times 10 \times 6 \times 0.35 \\
&= 420 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Volume of CO₂ gas required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CO_2,\,gas} &= V_{free} \times 0.34 \\
&= 420 \times 0.34 \\
&= 142.8 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mass of liquid CO₂:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2} &= \frac{142.8}{0.56} \\
&= 255 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) With 20 % safety margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{final} &= 255 \times 1.20 = 306 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of 45 kg cylinders:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lceil \frac{306}{45} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.8 \rceil = 7 \,\text{cylinders}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **7 cylinders** of 45 kg each are required.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]` *(Alternative)*

**(a)** Explain the **fire triangle** and classify the **five classes of fire** recognised by ISO 3941. For each class, indicate the **most appropriate fire-fighting medium** on a vessel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **selection criteria, application method, and limitations** of **foam (AFFF)** and **dry chemical powder (DCP)** extinguishers on inland vessels. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B(a)

The **fire triangle** consists of three elements — *Fuel*, *Heat*, and *Oxygen*. Removal of any one element extinguishes the fire.

**Five Classes (ISO 3941):**

| Class | Fuel Type | Best Medium (Vessel) |
|---|---|---|
| A | Solid (wood, fabric) | Water or AFFF |
| B | Flammable liquids | AFFF, CO₂, DCP |
| C | Flammable gases | DCP only |
| D | Combustible metals | Special dry powder |
| E | Electrical | CO₂ or Clean Agent |

The diagram below illustrates the placement decision:

```mermaid
graph TD
    NA1[Class A Solid]
    NA2[Class B Liquid]
    NA3[Class C Gas]
    NA4[Class D Metal]
    NA5[Class E Electrical]

    NA1 --> NA1a[Water Foam DCP]
    NA2 --> NA2a[AFFF CO2 DCP]
    NA3 --> NA3a[DCP Only]
    NA4 --> NA4a[Special Powder]
    NA5 --> NA5a[CO2 Clean Agent]
```

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B(b)

**Selection Criteria for Foam (AFFF):**

- **Best for:** Class A and Class B fires (liquid hydrocarbon spills — e.g., diesel, lube oil).
- **Mechanism:** Forms an aqueous film over the fuel surface, sealing it from oxygen.
- **Application method:** Gently apply foam to the *near edge* of the spill, letting it flow across the surface. **Do not direct jet into the liquid** (avoids splashing and fire spread).
- **Limitations:** ⚠ Cannot be used on electrical fires; ⚠ Poor on 3-D fires like running fuel from a broken pipe; ⚠ AFFF concentrates are now subject to **PFAS / fluorochemical environmental bans**, and vessels are shifting to **F3 fluorine-free foams**.

**Selection Criteria for DCP:**

- **Best for:** Truly *versatile* — Class A, B, C and E fires. Hence the standard extinguisher on inland barges.
- **Mechanism:** Chain-breaking chemical reaction interruption by phosphate and ammonium radicals.
- **Application method:** Aim at the **base of the flame**, sweep side to side, attack from upwind.
- **Limitations:** ⚠ Leaves **corrosive residue** — electronics must be cleaned immediately; ⚠ Fine powder reduces visibility in confined spaces; ⚠ Short discharge time (~ 9 s for a 4 kg unit).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in fire-appliance questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing classes** — many students incorrectly identify **electrical fires** as Class C instead of Class E (European) or remember only 4 classes. **Always mention ISO 3941's 5 classes.**
> 2. **Forgetting the safety margin** — examiners will **deduct 1 mark** if the 20 % margin is missing in CO₂ sizing problems.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — *kg* of liquid CO₂ vs. *m³* of free gas at NTP. Always show the **0.56 m³/kg** conversion step explicitly.
> 4. **Suggesting water for fuel-oil fires** — automatic **zero mark** scenario. **Never use water on Class B fires.**
> 5. **Omitting the pre-discharge alarm** in fixed CO₂ systems — this is a statutory IMO requirement; omitting it loses **2 marks** in part-(a).
> 6. **Listing PPE without mentioning SCBA duration** — examiners want **30 minutes minimum** explicitly cited.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Fire triangle**: Fuel + Heat + Oxygen. Every appliance attacks at least one side.
- ✅ **Five fire classes (ISO 3941):** A (solid), B (liquid), C (gas), D (metal), E (electrical).
- ✅ **Portable extinguisher types:** Water, Foam (AFFF), CO₂, Dry Powder (DCP/ABC), Clean Agent (FM-200/NOVEC).
- ✅ **Fixed systems:** CO₂ flooding, high-expansion foam, water mist — mandatory in machinery spaces of powered vessels.
- ✅ **Key sizing constants:** 0.56 m³ of free gas per kg of liquid CO₂; **≥ 34 % CO₂ concentration** for inertion; **≥ 0.25 MPa hydrant pressure**.
- ✅ **Fireman's outfit** includes bunker suit, helmet, gloves, boots, **SCBA (≥ 30 min)**, axe, lifeline, intrinsically-safe torch.
- ✅ **Inspection intervals:** Extinguishers — annual visual, **5-yearly hydrostatic test** at 1.5 × working pressure. SCBA cylinders — **hydrostatic test every 3 years**.
- ✅ **Statutes:** *IV Act 1917*, *IWAI regulations*, *SOLAS Ch. II-2*, *IRS Rules*, *IMO Res. A.602(15)*.
- ✅ **Environmental shift:** Halons replaced by HFCs and NOVEC 1230; PFAS-based AFFF being replaced by **F3 fluorine-free foams**.
- ✅ **Engines and battery compartments use CO₂ or clean agent only** — never dry powder in battery rooms due to **corrosion risk**.
- ✅ **Discharge-time law:** Fixed CO₂ in machinery spaces must discharge in **≤ 2 minutes** to be effective.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Life Saving Appliances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Life Saving Appliances # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Life Saving Appliances (LSA)** are specialized equipment, devices, and personal survival gear carried on board vessels and inland waterway craft, designed to preserve human life during emergencies such as man-overboard situations, vessel abandonment, fire, flooding, or collision. Under international maritime conventions (SOLAS) and inland waterway regulations, LSAs are **mandatory fittings** classified by their function: **personal (lifejackets, immersion suits), collective (lifeboats, life rafts, rescue boats), and signaling (EPIRB, SART, line-throwing appliances, pyrotechnics).** The statutory framework is principally governed by: - **SOLAS Chapter III** (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea) - **LSA Code** (Life-Saving Appliances Code, IMO Resolution MSC.48(66)) - **Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)** safety directives - **Indian Ports Act & Inland Vessels Act** for national compliance ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of Life Saving Appliances as the **"Airbag and Seatbelt System of a Ship."** Just as a car manufacturer equips every vehicle with seatbelts, airbags, and a first-aid kit — not because accidents *will* happen, but because when they do, **seconds decide survival** — every vessel is legally mandated to carry a tiered system of survival equipment. A lifejacket is like a personal seatbelt; a liferaft is the car's deployable survival capsule; an EPIRB is the **emergency beacon** that tells rescuers "I'm here." Each device works at a different stage of an emergency: **prevention → survival → location → rescue.** ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Inland Water Transportation Inland waterways — rivers, canals, backwaters, and estuaries — present **unique risk profiles**: - **Proximity to shore** (false sense of security) - **Shallow waters** with sudden depth changes - **Snag hazards** (submerged debris, weeds) - **Remote stretches** with delayed rescue response - **Crowded ferries** carrying mass passengers Life Saving Appliances mitigate these risks by ensuring that even if the vessel sinks or capsizes, **every individual on board has a fighting chance of survival and retrieval.** ## 1.4 Regulatory Mandate Snapshot > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT — Module 3 Core Outcome (CO3):** *Students must be able to identify, classify, and specify appropriate Life Saving Appliances for inland vessels in compliance with statutory regulations.* The statutory requirement number and type depend on: - **Vessel length** (e.g., vessels < 24 m have reduced requirements) - **Number of persons on board (POB)** - **Operating zone** (smooth/partially smooth/sheltered waters) - **Type of voyage** (day vs. extended) For example, on a Class A inland vessel, **100% lifejacket coverage of POB** is mandatory, plus life buoys spaced along the deck. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Life Saving Appliances Life Saving Appliances are classified into **four functional tiers**, each serving a distinct phase of maritime emergency response: ### Tier 1: Personal Survival Equipment - **Lifejackets (LJ)** — wearable buoyancy garment - **Life Buoys (LB)** — ring-shaped throwable buoyancy aid - **Immersion Suits** — full-body thermal protection - **Thermal Protective Aids (TPA)** — blanket/floater suit ### Tier 2: Collective Survival Equipment - **Liferafts (inflatable / rigid)** — group abandonment craft - **Lifeboats (open / enclosed / free-fall)** — vessel abandonment craft - **Rescue Boats** — fast response for MOB recovery - **Dinghies / Buoyant Apparatus** — basic abandonment ### Tier 3: Visual & Acoustic Signaling Devices - **Pyrotechnic Flares** (handheld, parachute, smoke) - **Line-Throwing Appliances** (rocket or pistol) - **Whistles, Bells, Horns** - **Radar Reflectors** ### Tier 4: Electronic Distress & Location Devices - **EPIRB** — Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon - **SART** — Search and Rescue Transponder - **PLB** — Personal Locator Beacon - **VHF DSC** — Digital Selective Calling ## 2.2 Technical Specifications of Major LSAs ### 2.2.1 Lifejackets A lifejacket provides **buoyancy** to keep an unconscious person's airway (nose and mouth) clear of water. Key specifications: - **Buoyancy:** Minimum **150 N (Newtons)** for adult SOLAS-compliant jacket; 100 N for inshore/journey use - **Freeboard requirement:** The wearer, when unconscious, must float with at least **120 mm** of freeboard (mouth-to-water clearance) - **Turning time:** Must turn an unconscious wearer face-up in **≤ 5 seconds** - **Test drop height:** Must support the wearer from a drop of **4.5 m** > [!NOTE] > **Buoyancy Formula:** The buoyant force equals the weight of displaced water. > $$F_b = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$ > where $\rho_{water}$ ≈ 1025 kg/m³ (seawater) or 1000 kg/m³ (freshwater for inland). ### 2.2.2 Life Buoys A life buoy is a **ring-shaped flotation device** thrown to a person in the water. - **Buoyancy:** Minimum **14.5 kg** of buoyant material (SOLAS requirement) - **Diameter:** **Outer 760 mm, Inner 440 mm** - **Mass:** Not exceeding **2.5 kg** (so it can be thrown accurately) - **Color:** International orange or red, with **retro-reflective tape** (4 stripes, 50 mm each) - **Grab lines:** 4 equally spaced lifeline grips around the periphery ### 2.2.3 Liferafts Liferafts are the **primary abandonment craft** for vessels that sink slowly. They deploy either manually (throw-overboard) or automatically (hydrostatic release). - **Capacity:** 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25 person units - **Inflation system:** CO₂/N₂ cylinder, automatic on painter pull - **Inflation time:** **≤ 60 seconds** at ambient temperature - **Canopy:** Must withstand 24-hour exposure with rain collector - **Equipment pack (SOLAS Pack):** - Bailer, sponges, sea anchor, paddles - First-aid kit, repair kit - **Fishing kit, drinking water rations (1.5 L/person), food rations** - **Pyrotechnic flares (6 hand-held + 2 parachute)** - **EPIRB, SART** (depending on vessel) ### 2.2.4 Lifeboats - **Construction:** Steel, aluminum, or GRP (fiberglass) - **Propulsion:** Oars + sail, inboard/outboard engine - **Capacity:** 6 to 150 persons - **Free-fall lifeboats** are designed to launch from a **ramp at 30° to 45° angle** - **Engine:** Diesel outboard, must run for **5 minutes after 60 minutes submerged** ### 2.2.5 EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon) The EPIRB is a **satellite-linked distress beacon** that transmits vessel position and identity. - **Frequency:** **406 MHz** (transmitted to COSPAS-SARSAT satellites) - **Battery life:** **48 hours continuous** transmission at -20°C - **Hydrostatic Release:** Automatically deploys if vessel sinks to **1.5–4.0 m depth** - **GNSS Accuracy:** ±50 m with GPS lock - **Protocol:** Transmits ID, vessel MMSI, position every 50 seconds ### 2.2.6 SART (Search and Rescue Transponder) The SART operates on **9 GHz (X-band radar frequency** and is interrogated by rescue vessel radar. - **Activation:** Manual or automatic (water switch) - **Range:** Up to **5 nautical miles** when interrogated by rescue radar - **Battery standby:** **96 hours**; operating: **8 hours continuous** - **Display:** Shows 12 concentric blips on rescue radar screen ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Typical Value | Unit | |-----------|-------------------|---------------|------| | Buoyant force | $F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$ | Depends on $V$ | N | | Required buoyancy (lifejacket) | SOLAS minimum | 150 | N | | Required buoyancy (life buoy) | SOLAS minimum | 14.5 | kg | | Liferaft inflation time | SOLAS maximum | 60 | s | | EPIRB transmission frequency | International standard | 406 | MHz | | EPIRB battery endurance | SOLAS minimum | 48 | hours | | SART frequency band | International standard | 9 | GHz (X-band) | | SART standby duration | SOLAS minimum | 96 | hours | | SART active duration | SOLAS minimum | 8 | hours | | Liferaft canopy endurance | SOLAS minimum | 24 | hours | | Drop test (lifejacket) | SOLAS minimum | 4.5 | m | | Unconscious turn time | SOLAS maximum | 5 | seconds | | Life buoy outer diameter | SOLAS standard | 760 | mm | | Life buoy inner diameter | SOLAS standard | 440 | mm | | Life buoy weight max | SOLAS maximum | 2.5 | kg | | EPIRB hydrostatic depth | Typical activation | 1.5 to 4.0 | m | | Freeboard (unconscious wearer) | SOLAS minimum | 120 | mm | | Flare burn time (handheld) | Typical performance | 60 | s | | Parachute flare altitude | Typical performance | 300 to 450 | m | | Parachute flare burn time | Typical performance | 40 | s | | Lifejacket per person allowance | IWAI standard | 100 percent of POB | percent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM TIP:** Always state the **value AND the unit** AND the **regulation** (e.g., "150 N as per SOLAS Chapter III, LSA Code Chapter II").
