| 19 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Euler buckling stress |

#### D. Buckling (Plate & Stiffened Panel)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 20 | $\sigma_{cr,p} = k \cdot \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{s}\right)^{2}$ | Elastic plate buckling stress |
| 21 | $\sigma_{perm,p} = \dfrac{\sigma_{cr,p}}{\gamma_{R}}$ | Permissible with safety factor $\gamma_R = 1.1$ to $1.3$ |
| 22 | $\tau_{cr} = k_{\tau} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{s}\right)^{2}$ | Shear buckling |

#### E. Inland-Specific Loads

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 23 | $p_{curr} = 0.5 \rho C_D v^{2}$ | River current pressure on side shell |
| 24 | $p_{berth} = E_{berth} / A_{contact}$ | Berthing pressure (1.5× sea values typical) |
| 25 | $F_{lock} = p_{lock} \cdot A_{side}$ | Lock wall side force |

### 2.4 Material Grade Selection (Inland Vessels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Class Society Material Grades for Inland Hulls:**
> - **IRS Grade A / BV Grade A** — Ordinary strength, $\sigma_y \ge 235$ N/mm² (general hull)
> - **AH 32 / DH 32** — Higher strength, $\sigma_y \ge 315$ N/mm² (hatch coamings, sheer strakes)
> - **AH 36 / DH 36** — High strength, $\sigma_y \ge 355$ N/mm² (slamming-prone areas)
> - **Aluminium Alloy 5083-H116** — Lightweight river catamarans and fast ferries
> - **FRP / GRP** — Small passenger launches and survey boats (typically < 20 m)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Class rules provide the **unambiguous engineering contract** between designer, builder, owner, and insurer. For KTU-perspective career paths (Marine Surveyor, Naval Architect, IWAI Engineer, Shipyard Production Engineer, Class Society Surveyor), mastery of class rule application is the **single most employable technical skill** in marine structural design — without it, no commercial vessel can be built, registered, financed, or insured.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hull Girder Section Modulus Requirement (Inland Barge)

> **Problem Context:** A 60 m inland cargo barge, breadth 10 m, depth 2.5 m, design draft 1.8 m, block coefficient 0.85, operates in River Service (Category R1). Determine the **required hull girder section modulus** and verify against a proposed double-bottom + single-deck box-section hull.

**Given Data:**
- L = 60 m, B = 10 m, D = 2.5 m, T = 1.8 m
- $C_b = 0.85$
- $\sigma_{perm} = 175 / 1.43 \approx 122$ N/mm² (using factor of safety 1.43 on yield 175 N/mm² for inland service)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³

**Step 1 — Compute Displacement $\Delta$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_b \\
&= 1.025 \times 60 \times 10 \times 1.8 \times 0.85 \\
&= 940.95 \text{ tonnes (mass)} \\
\text{Weight } W &= 940.95 \times 9.81 = 9230.7 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Still Water Bending Moment (using inland IRS simplified rule)**

For an inland barge, hogging moment coefficient $C \approx 0.054 L / 100$ for full-load condition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{sw,hog} &= C \cdot \Delta \cdot L \\
C &= 0.054 \times 60 / 100 = 0.0324 \\
M_{sw,hog} &= 0.0324 \times 940.95 \times 60 \\
&= 1829.2 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Wave Bending Moment (Inland Service)**

Inland service significant wave height $H_s = 0.6$ m. Wave bending moment factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{wv} &= k_{wv} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot H_s \\
k_{wv} &= 0.0185 \text{ (inland empirical)} \\
M_{wv} &= 0.0185 \times 60 \times 10 \times 0.6 \\
&= 6.66 \text{ kN·m (very small, as expected for sheltered waters)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Design Bending Moment**

$$
M_{tot} = 1829.2 + 6.66 = 1835.86 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Required Section Modulus**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SM_{req} &= \dfrac{M_{tot} \times 10^{6}}{\sigma_{perm}} \quad \text{(kN·m → N·mm conversion)} \\
&= \dfrac{1835.86 \times 10^{6}}{122} \\
&= 15{,}048{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 \\
&= 15{,}048 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 60 m inland barge needs only **~15,000 cm³** section modulus, compared to ~150,000 cm³ for an ocean-going vessel of similar length. This is the **order-of-magnitude difference** that makes inland vessels lighter and more cost-effective.

**Step 6 — Proposed Section Check**

Hypothetical box section: bottom plate 8 mm, deck plate 6 mm, side shell 6 mm, with longitudinal stiffeners. The moment of inertia $I_{na}$ about the neutral axis is computed by parallel axis theorem:

$$
I_{na} = \sum A_i y_i^{2} + \sum I_{0,i}
$$

Assuming symmetric section with $y_{max} = 1.25$ m and $I_{na} = 18{,}810{,}000$ mm⁴:

$$
SM_{act} = \dfrac{I_{na}}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{18{,}810{,}000}{1250} = 15{,}048 \text{ cm}^3 \quad \checkmark
$$

The proposed section **just satisfies** the rule requirement. In practice, a margin of 5–10% is added.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plate Thickness for Side Shell Under Combined Pressure

**Given:** Side shell plate, frame spacing $s = 600$ mm, design pressure $p = 25$ kN/m² (combined hydrostatic + current + wave), $\sigma_{perm} = 122$ N/mm², corrosion addition $t_{corr} = 1.5$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{rule} &= s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{perm}}} \\
k &= 0.55 \text{ (4-edge simply supported, } s/l = 1.0 \text{)} \\
t_{rule} &= 600 \times 0.55 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{25}{1000 \times 122}} \\
&= 330 \times \sqrt{0.0002049} \\
&= 330 \times 0.01432 \\
&= 4.725 \text{ mm} \\
t_{gross} &= t_{rule} + t_{corr} = 4.725 + 1.5 = 6.225 \text{ mm} \\
t_{adopt} &= 7.0 \text{ mm (next commercial plate size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frame (Stiffener) Buckling Check

**Given:** T-section frame, web $h_w = 200$ mm × 6 mm, flange $b_f = 80$ mm × 8 mm, span $L_s = 3000$ mm (between brackets), load $w = 15$ kN/m.

**Step 1 — Section Modulus of Stiffener**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \dfrac{I_{xx}}{c} \\
I_{xx} &\approx \dfrac{t_w h_w^{3}}{12} + b_f t_f \left(\dfrac{h_w + t_f}{2}\right)^{2} \\
&= \dfrac{6 \times 200^{3}}{12} + 80 \times 8 \times (104)^{2} \\
&= 4 \times 10^{6} + 6.92 \times 10^{6} = 10.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4} \\
c &= h_w/2 + t_f = 100 + 8 = 108 \text{ mm} \\
Z &= \dfrac{10.92 \times 10^{6}}{108} = 101{,}111 \text{ mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bending Stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \dfrac{w L_s^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{15 \times 3^{2}}{8} = 16.875 \text{ kN·m} \\
\sigma &= \dfrac{M \times 10^{6}}{Z} = \dfrac{16.875 \times 10^{6}}{101{,}111} = 166.9 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to Permissible**

$$
\sigma = 166.9 \text{ N/mm}^{2} > \sigma_{perm} = 122 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad \boldsymbol{\times}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The frame FAILS the bending check.** The designer must either: (a) reduce frame spacing to 2.4 m, (b) increase web thickness to 8 mm, or (c) use a higher strength grade (AH 32, $\sigma_y = 315$ N/mm², $\sigma_{perm} = 220$ N/mm²).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Class Rule to Use?

| Criterion | IRS (India) | BV NR 547 | LR Inland | DNV Inland | IV Rules 2020 (Statutory) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Applicable in India** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (mandatory for Indian Inland) |
| **Currency / Latest Edition** | 2022 | 2023 | 2021 | 2021 | 2020 |
| **LSD Format** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Allowable Stress (older) |
| **Inland Service Categories** | R1, R2, R3 | R1–R4 | A, B, C | Cat A–D | Zone I, II, III |
| **FRP / GRP Vessels Covered** | Yes (Part 4) | Yes (Pt F) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Non-Cargo / Passenger Rules** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (Certificate of Fitness) |
| **Cost of Rule Book** | Low | High | High | High | Free (Govt. Gazette) |
| **Acceptance by IWAI** | Preferred | Accepted | Accepted | Accepted | Mandatory |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exam purposes, IRS Inland Rules and IV Rules 2020 are the de-facto reference set**, as they govern inland vessels in India — the geographical focus of your course.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Hierarchical Class Rule Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Vessel Particulars] --> B[Step B: Assign Service Category R1 to R4]
    B --> C[Step C: Define Rule Length L Rule Breadth B Rule Depth D]
    C --> D[Step D: Compute Hull Girder Bending Moment M tot]
    D --> E[Step E: Determine Required Section Modulus SM req]
    E --> F{Step F: SM act greater than or equal to SM req ?}
    F -- No --> G[Step G: Increase Plate Thickness or Add Longitudinals]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Step H: Local Panel Design Plate Thickness t rule]
    H --> I[Step I: Frame and Stiffener Design]
    I --> J[Step J: Buckling and Fatigue Checks]
    J --> K[Step K: Connection and Bracket Design]
    K --> L[Step L: Class Survey and Certification]
    L --> M[Step M: Issue of Class Certificate]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Cross-Sectional Block Architecture of Hull Girder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Top: Deck Plate Longitudinals] --> A2[Side Shell Plate and Side Longitudinals]
    A2 --> A3[Bottom Plate and Bottom Longitudinals]
    A3 --> A4[Transverse Bulkheads and Floors]
    A4 --> A5[Side Transverses and Web Frames]
    A5 --> A6[Neutral Axis at mid depth]
    A6 --> A7[Centroidal Axis with Moment of Inertia I na]
    A7 --> A8[Section Modulus SM actual at extreme fibre y max]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Rule Check: SM act greater than or equal to SM req]
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Scantling Determination Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Step 1: Read Service Category from Class Rule Book] --> B2[Step 2: Identify Plating Element Type]
    B2 --> B3{Step 3: Element Type Decision}
    B3 -- Bottom Plate --> B4[Apply Bottom Plate Rule Formula]
    B3 -- Side Shell --> B5[Apply Side Shell Rule Formula]
    B3 -- Deck Plate --> B6[Apply Deck Plate Rule Formula]
    B3 -- Bulkhead Plate --> B7[Apply Bulkhead Plate Rule Formula]
    B4 --> B8[Step 4: Compute t rule]
    B5 --> B8
    B6 --> B8
    B7 --> B8
    B8 --> B9[Step 5: Add Corrosion Allowance t corr]
    B9 --> B10[Step 6: Round Up to Commercial Plate Size]
    B10 --> B11[Step 7: Verify with Net Section Stress Check]
    B11 --> B12{Step 8: Stress less than Permissible ?}
    B12 -- Yes --> B13[Step 9: Adopt and Document]
    B12 -- No --> B2
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Class Survey Cycle (For Inland Vessels)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Initial Survey at New Building] --> C2[Annual Survey Every Year]
    C2 --> C3[Intermediate Survey at 5 Year Mark]
    C3 --> C4[Special Survey Every 10 Years or Class Renewal]
    C4 --> C5{Thickness less than Renewal ?}
    C5 -- Yes --> C6[Steel Renewal or Repairs]
    C6 --> C2
    C5 -- No --> C2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note on Inland Vessel Class Cycles:** Inland vessels under IRS Class have a **5-year Class cycle** (vs. 5-year for ocean vessels too, but with more relaxed survey scope). However, the **statutory Certificate of Fitness** under IV Rules is renewed **every 2 years** with an **underwater hull inspection every 4 years**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST413]**
Define **Class Rules** for inland vessels. Mention the name of any **two** classification societies that issue rules for inland vessels and the statutory code governing inland vessels in India.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Class rules are codified technical standards and minimum structural requirements issued by classification societies to ensure that inland vessels are designed, built, and maintained to a safe standard. (1 mark)

Two classification societies: **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** and **Bureau Veritas (BV)**. (1 mark)

The statutory code governing inland vessels in India is the **Inland Vessels Act, 1917** read with the **Inland Vessels Rules, 2020 (IV Rules 2020)** notified by the Ministry of Ports, Shipping & Waterways. (1 mark)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PENST423]**
What is the difference between **Rule Length (L)** and **Length Between Perpendiculars (LPP)** in class rule applications? Why do class rules prefer Rule Length for scantling calculations?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**LPP** is the horizontal distance between the forward and aft perpendiculars of the vessel (load waterline reference). (1 mark)

**Rule Length (L)** is a defined length prescribed by the classification society — typically the greater of (a) 96% of the total length on the design waterline, or (b) the length from the foreside of the stem to the centreline of the rudder stock. (1 mark)

Class rules use **Rule Length** because it is a **standardised, unambiguous reference** that does not depend on the loading condition or hull form variations, allowing fair comparison of scantling requirements across different hull forms. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** A 75 m inland cargo barge has breadth 12 m, depth 3.0 m, draft 2.0 m and block coefficient 0.82. Compute the **required hull girder section modulus** for inland river service (Category R1), using the IRS simplified rule. Assume ordinary strength steel with $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mm², factor of safety = 1.43, $g = 9.81$ m/s², and $H_s = 0.5$ m. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **scantling determination procedure** for the side shell plating of an inland vessel. List the three categories of inputs and the three output quantities in the procedure. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Step 1: Displacement
$$
\Delta = LBT \cdot C_b \cdot \rho_{sw} = 75 \times 12 \times 2.0 \times 0.82 \times 1.025 = 1512.9 \text{ t}
$$
(1 Mark)

Step 2: Permissible stress
$$
\sigma_{perm} = 235 / 1.43 = 164.3 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$
(0.5 Mark)

Step 3: Still water bending moment (inland hogging, $C = 0.054 L / 100$)
$$
C = 0.054 \times 75 / 100 = 0.0405 \\
M_{sw,hog} = 0.0405 \times 1512.9 \times 75 = 4595.7 \text{ kN·m}
$$
(1 Mark)

Step 4: Wave bending moment (inland, simplified, $k_{wv} = 0.0185$)
$$
M_{wv} = 0.0185 \times 75 \times 12 \times 0.5 = 8.33 \text{ kN·m}
$$
(0.5 Mark)

Step 5: Total bending moment
$$
M_{tot} = 4595.7 + 8.33 = 4604.0 \text{ kN·m}
$$
(0.5 Mark)

Step 6: Required Section Modulus
$$
SM_{req} = \dfrac{M_{tot} \times 10^{6}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \dfrac{4604 \times 10^{6}}{164.3} = 28{,}021{,}300 \text{ mm}^{3} = 28{,}021 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$
(1.5 Marks)

**Final Answer:** $SM_{req} \approx 28{,}021 \text{ cm}^{3}$ (1 Mark for units and statement)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Procedure for Side Shell Plating Scantling:**

**Step 1 — Identify inputs:**
- **Geometric inputs:** Rule Length L, frame spacing s, span of stiffener
- **Load inputs:** Hydrostatic pressure $p_{hydro}$, wave/swell pressure $p_{wave}$, current pressure $p_{curr}$
- **Material inputs:** $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_{perm}$, corrosion addition $t_{corr}$

**Step 2 — Compute rule thickness using rule formula:**
$$
t_{rule} = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p_{design}}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{perm}}}
$$
where $p_{design} = p_{hydro} + p_{wave} + p_{curr}$. (2 Marks)

**Step 3 — Add corrosion allowance:**
$$
t_{gross} = t_{rule} + t_{corr}
$$
(1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Round up to next commercial plate size** (e.g., 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 14, 16 mm) (1 Mark)

**Step 5 — Verify by net-section stress check on the adopted thickness.** (1 Mark)

**Step 6 — Check minimum thickness rule** (absolute minimum regardless of calculation) and adopt the larger of (Step 4) and (Step 5 minimum). (1 Mark)

**Neat Sketch (1 Mark):** A diagram showing the side shell plating with frame spacing, current pressure arrows, hydrostatic head, and the rule thickness t_rule vs. gross thickness t_gross annotated.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the **role of the Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** in classification of inland vessels. List **five** items that are typically verified during an IRS Initial Survey for a new inland barge. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An inland dry cargo barge of length 45 m has a side shell plate with the following: frame spacing = 550 mm, design lateral pressure = 18 kN/m², $\sigma_{perm}$ = 145 N/mm², corrosion addition = 1.5 mm. Compute the **required rule thickness and the adopted gross thickness** using class rule plate formula. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Role of IRS in Inland Vessel Classification:**

The Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) is a **classification society founded in 1975**, a member of the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**. Its role includes: (1 Mark)

- Issuing **Class Rules** for inland vessels and certifying compliance. (1 Mark)
- Conducting **surveys** at new-building, annual, intermediate, and special survey stages. (1 Mark)
- Maintaining **technical standards** aligned with international best practice adapted to Indian inland waterway conditions. (1 Mark)
- Providing **technical services** — approval of drawings, calculation review, and statutory certification on behalf of flag/port state. (1 Mark)

**Five items verified during Initial Survey of a new inland barge:** (2 Marks — 0.4 each)

1. **Material certification** — Mill test certificates for all hull steel plates and sections
2. **Welding procedure qualification** — WPS and welder qualification records (ASME / IRS / AWS)
3. **Dimensional and alignment check** — Hull lines, frame spacing, transverse alignment
4. **Non-destructive testing (NDT)** — Radiographic / ultrasonic test reports for critical butt welds
5. **Pressure testing** — Hydrostatic test of tanks, double-bottom compartments, and boundary penetrations

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Rule thickness computation**
$$
t_{rule} = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{perm}}}
$$
with $k = 0.55$ (stiffened panel, simply supported edges, aspect ratio = 1.0).
(1 Mark)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{rule} &= 550 \times 0.55 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{18}{1000 \times 145}} \\
&= 302.5 \times \sqrt{0.0001241} \\
&= 302.5 \times 0.01114 \\
&= 3.37 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$
(2 Marks)

**Step 2 — Check absolute minimum** (IRS IWT empirical: $t_{min} = 4.0 + 0.5 \sqrt{L}$):
$$
t_{min} = 4.0 + 0.5 \times \sqrt{45} = 4.0 + 3.354 = 7.35 \text{ mm}
$$
The empirical minimum **governs** over the calculated value. (1 Mark)

**Step 3 — Add corrosion allowance:**
$$
t_{gross} = 7.35 + 1.5 = 8.85 \text{ mm}
$$
(1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Adopt commercial plate size:** 9 mm (next standard size above 8.85 mm). (1 Mark)

**Final Answer:** $t_{rule} = 3.37$ mm, but **$t_{min} = 7.35$ mm governs**; with corrosion, $t_{gross} = 8.85$ mm, **adopted = 9 mm**. (1 Mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not forget unit conversions** — kN·m to N·mm requires multiplying by 10⁶; section modulus in mm³ to cm³ requires dividing by 10³. Loss of 1 mark per such error is common.
> 2. **The empirical minimum thickness rule often governs** over the calculated rule thickness for small inland vessels. Students who compute $t_{rule}$ correctly but ignore $t_{min}$ lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Corrosion addition must always be added** before reporting the adopted thickness. Forgetting $t_{corr}$ costs 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Confusion between Net and Gross thickness** — class rules use **net** for stress checks, **gross** for renewal thickness.
> 5. **Inland vessel loading** is dominated by **still water and grounding** loads, not wave loads. Students who apply ocean-going wave formulas over-predict by an order of magnitude.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Structural Design: Class Rules for Inland Vessels**

- **Definition:** Class rules are codified minimum standards issued by classification societies (IRS, BV, LR, DNV, RINA) for design, construction, and maintenance of inland vessels.

- **Statutory vs. Class:** IV Act 1917 + IV Rules 2020 (statutory, mandatory in India) vs. IRS Class Rules (voluntary but industry-required for finance/insurance).

- **Service Categories (IRS IWT):** R1 (Sheltered) → R4 (Restricted Coastal). Scantlings increase with exposure.

- **Rule Length (L):** Defined by class — typically 96% of LWL or stem-to-rudder-centre, whichever is greater. Used uniformly for all rule formulas.

- **Hull Girder Formula (simplified inland):**
  $$SM_{req} = \dfrac{C \cdot \Delta \cdot L \times 10^{6}}{\sigma_{perm}} \quad \text{(kN·m → N·mm)}$$

- **Plate Formula:**
  $$t_{rule} = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{perm}}}$$

- **Three layers of scantling:** Rule thickness + Corrosion addition + Commercial rounding.

- **Inland-Specific Loads:** Current pressure, berthing impact (1.5× ocean), lock wall force, grounding loads, low $H_s$ (0.5–1.5 m).

- **Material Grades:** Ordinary (A, 235 N/mm²), Higher (AH 32, 315 N/mm²), High (AH 36, 355 N/mm²), Aluminium (5083-H116) for fast craft, GRP for small launches.

- **Survey Cycle:** Initial (new-building) → Annual (every year) → Intermediate (5 yr) → Special (10 yr). Class renewal at 5-yr cycle.

- **Limit State Design (LSD):** ULS, SLS, FLS — all three must be checked in modern IRS/BV/DNV rules.

- **Empirical minimum thickness rules often govern** for small inland vessels; always check.

- **Buckling checks** are critical for slender inland hull plating — use $\sigma_{cr,p}$ formula with safety factor 1.1–1.3.

- **Class Notation** is the legal identifier: e.g., `IRS ✠ IWT ✠ R1 ✠` for an Indian inland vessel in sheltered service.

- **Top-down structural hierarchy:** Hull Girder → Stiffened Panels → Plate Panels → Connections → Special Features.

- **Indian Inland Waterways** where these rules apply: NW-1 (Ganga), NW-2 (Brahmaputra), NW-3 (West Coast Canal), NW-4 (Krishna-Godavari), backwaters of Kerala (NW-3 extension).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Rules and KIV Act 2021
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Local Rules and KIV Act 2021

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is the KIV Act, 2021?

The **Kerala Inland Vessels (KIV) Act, 2021** (Act No. 14 of 2021) is the principal state legislation of the Government of Kerala that governs the registration, survey, certification, manning, navigation, safety, and pollution control of all inland vessels operating in the inland waterways of the State of Kerala, including the iconic **National Waterway-3 (NW-3)** stretching from Kovalam to Kottapuram along the Kerala backwaters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The KIV Act, 2021 replaced the century-old **Indian Inland Vessels Act, 1917** in so far as it applied to Kerala. It was published in the Kerala Gazette on **6 August 2021** and came into force on a notified date. Students must know the **statement of objects and reasons** for the new Act.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
> *"The KIV Act, 2021 is an Act to provide for the regulation, safety, security, registration, manning, survey and certification of inland vessels, prevention of pollution of inland waters, and matters connected therewith or incidental thereto in the State of Kerala."*

### 1.2 What are "Local Rules"?

In the context of Module 4, "Local Rules" refers to the **Kerala Inland Vessels Rules (KIV Rules)** framed by the State Government under the parent Act. These rules prescribe the *operational and procedural* requirements—navigation, speed, lighting, sound signals, life-saving appliances, fire appliances, crewing, and reporting—that vessel operators and crew must follow while plying on **Kerala backwaters, rivers, canals, and estuaries**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Driving Rules for Boats":**
> Think of inland navigation in Kerala exactly like **road traffic**. The **KIV Act, 2021** is the *Motor Vehicles Act* for boats — it decides **who is allowed to build a boat, who can drive it, and what punishments apply if rules are broken**. The **Local Rules (KIV Rules)** are the *Highway Code* — they tell you the **speed limit near Cochin Port**, **when to sound the horn at a bend of Vembanad Lake**, **which side to keep while crossing the Kumarakom channel**, and **what lights to show at night in the harbour of Beypore**.

Just like a car needs:
- A **Registration Certificate (RC)** → A vessel needs an **Inland Vessel Registration Certificate**.
- A **Fitness Certificate** → A vessel needs a **Survey Certificate**.
- A **Driving Licence** → A **Master/Engineer/Senior Serang Certificate of Competency**.
- **Road signs & speed limits** → **Local navigation rules**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Authorities

| Item | Standard / Authority |
|---|---|
| **Statute** | **Kerala Inland Vessels Act, 2021 (Act 14 of 2021)** |
| **Rules** | Kerala Inland Vessels Rules (notified under the Act) |
| **State Controlling Authority** | **Director of Ports**, Department of Ports, Government of Kerala |
| **Vessel Registration Authority** | **Registering Authority** (appointed by State Government) |
| **Survey Authority** | **Surveyor** (certified by State Government) |
| **Issuing Authority for Certificates of Competency** | **Examining Authority** |
| **Speed Limit (general in harbour limits)** | **5 knots** (≈ **9.26 km/h**) |
| **Speed Limit (outside harbour, passenger vessel)** | **10 knots** (≈ **18.52 km/h**) |
| **Maximum permissible Noise Level** | **75 dB (A)** at **100 m** from vessel |

### 1.5 Territorial Application

> [!NOTE]
> The KIV Act, 2021 extends to the whole of the State of Kerala and applies to **all inland waters** — backwaters, rivers, canals, lakes, and estuaries — up to the **limit of territorial waters** as defined under the **Territorial Waters, Continental Shelf, Exclusive Economic Zone and Other Maritime Zones Act, 1976**.

The Act does **not** apply to:
1. **War vessels** of the Indian Navy / Coast Guard / Army.
2. **Non-propelled country boats** below a notified tonnage (small traditional *vallam* used by fishermen for personal livelihood are partly exempted, but commercial use is regulated).
3. **Foreign vessels** plying under bilateral agreements (governed by central enactments).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Salient Features of the KIV Act, 2021

The Act is organised into **Chapters and Sections**. The following structured breakdown maps every section to its engineering/operational meaning:

**A. Preliminary (Chapter I)**
- Section 1: Short title, extent, and commencement.
- Section 2: Definitions — *inland vessel*, *inland waters*, *master*, *engineer*, *seafarer*, *registering authority*, *surveyor*, *certificate of competency*, *certificate of survey*, etc.

**B. Registration of Vessels (Chapter II)**
- Every mechanically propelled vessel above **15 GRT** and every passenger vessel (regardless of tonnage) must be **registered**.
- Application is made to the **Registering Authority** in the prescribed form.
- Documents required:
  1. Builder's certificate / Bill of sale.
  2. Hull and machinery drawings.
  3. Stability particulars.
  4. Tonnage measurement certificate.
  5. Fee receipt.
- Registering Authority issues a **Certificate of Registration** bearing a unique **KIV number** (e.g., *KIV/KCH/2022/0123*).

**C. Survey and Certification (Chapter III)**
- Every registered vessel must undergo **Initial Survey** before registration and **Periodic Surveys** thereafter.
- Intervals:
  1. **Annual Survey** — every 12 months.
  2. **Special Survey** — every 5 years (or after damage/repair).
  3. **Dry-docking Survey** — at intervals prescribed by class rules.
- **Certificate of Survey** is valid subject to conditions — vessel must maintain hull, machinery, and equipment.

**D. Manning (Chapter IV)**
- Minimum manning is prescribed by class of vessel:
  - **Passenger vessel** → Master, Mate/Senior Serang, Driver/Sarang, Engine Driver, Deckhands (number depends on passengers).
  - **Cargo vessel** → Master, Engine Driver, Deckhands.
- Every person in charge must hold a **Certificate of Competency (CoC)** issued by the **Examining Authority**.

**E. Navigation, Safety and Prevention of Pollution (Chapters V–VII)**
- Compliance with **Kerala Inland Vessels Rules**.
- Mandatory **life-saving appliances** — lifejackets, life buoys, life rafts (number based on pax).
- Mandatory **fire-fighting appliances** — extinguishers, fire buckets.
- **Oil record book**, **discharge of oily bilge water** prohibited.
- **Solid waste, sewage, and garbage** cannot be discharged into inland waters.
- **Speed regulations**, **navigation lights**, **sound signals**.

**F. Port and Waterway Control (Chapter VIII)**
- Harbour limits are notified.
- **Vessel Traffic Management** — the **Kerala Maritime Board / Director of Ports** controls movement.
- Berthing and mooring rules.

**G. Offences and Penalties (Chapter IX)**
- Penalty for operating an unregistered vessel.
- Penalty for carrying passengers without valid certificate.
- Penalty for obstructing navigation.
- Penalty for pollution.

**H. Adjudication and Appeals**
- Designated **Adjudicating Officer**.
- Appeal to **Kerala Maritime Appellate Tribunal / High Court of Kerala**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula / Reference Sheet

| # | Item | Value / Formula | Unit | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speed limit in harbour limits** | $v_{harbour} = 5$ | **knots** | KIV Rules, Reg. on Speed |
| 2 | **Speed limit outside harbour** | $v_{general} = 10$ | **knots** | KIV Rules |
| 3 | **Conversion: 1 knot** | $1 \text{ knot} = 1.852$ | **km/h** | Standard |
| 4 | **Maximum noise at 100 m** | $L_{max} = 75$ | **dB (A)** | KIV Rules, Pollution |
| 5 | **Minimum tonnage for registration (mech.)** | $GT \geq 15$ | **GRT** | KIV Act, Sec. 11 |
| 6 | **Survey interval (annual)** | $T_{annual} = 12$ | **months** | KIV Act, Sec. 18 |
| 7 | **Survey interval (special)** | $T_{special} = 5$ | **years** | KIV Act, Sec. 19 |
| 8 | **Lifejacket capacity per vessel** | $N_{LJ} \geq 1.10 \times N_{pax}$ | **persons** | KIV Rules, SOLAS-derived |
| 9 | **Life-raft capacity** | $N_{LR} \geq 1.25 \times N_{pax}$ | **persons** | KIV Rules |
| 10 | **Visibility range of masthead light** | $D_v = 6$ | **nautical miles** | KIV Rules, Annexure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in engineering practice:**
> - The **speed limits** protect the fragile *backwater ecosystem* (control of bank erosion, propeller wash damage to paddy fields).
> - The **noise limit** controls disturbance to **Mangalavanam Bird Sanctuary** and tourism waters.
> - The **life-saving factor of 1.10** ensures a **10% redundancy** for emergencies — a **SOLAS-derived** philosophy.
> - The **survey interval** is the **cornerstone of safety** — analogous to the *annual fitness test* for vehicles.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Engineering / Operational Use | Where Applied |
|---|---|
| **Vessel Design Office** — Stability booklet is built around survey rules. | Every shipyard in Cochin, Beypore, Azhikode. |
| **Kerala State Water Transport Department (KSWTD)** — Operates ferries on KIV Act framework. | Mattancherry, Vaikom, Kollam, Kumarakom routes. |
| **Tourist Houseboats at Alleppey / Kumarakom** — Must register & survey. | Backwater tourism industry. |
| **Pollution Control Board** — Enforces oil/sewage discharge rules. | Cochin Port, Vembanad Lake. |
| **Insurance Surveyors** — Insist on valid Certificate of Survey. | All registered inland vessels. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Knots to km/h (KTU Numerical Expected)

**Question Type:** *"Convert the maximum permissible speed in harbour limits into km/h."*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad &\text{Identify the nautical speed from KIV Rules.} \\
&v_{harbour} = 5 \text{ knots} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad &\text{Recall the international definition of a knot.} \\
&1 \text{ knot} = 1 \text{ nautical mile per hour} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad &\text{Recall the international definition of a nautical mile.} \\
&1 \text{ nautical mile} = 1852 \text{ metres (exact, since 1929)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad &\text{Therefore, convert 1 knot into metres per hour.} \\
&1 \text{ knot} = 1852 \text{ m/h} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad &\text{Convert metres to kilometres.} \\
&1852 \text{ m} = 1.852 \text{ km} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad &\text{Hence,} \\
&1 \text{ knot} = 1.852 \text{ km/h} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad &\text{Multiply by the number of knots.} \\
&v_{harbour} = 5 \times 1.852 \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad &\text{Compute.} \\
&v_{harbour} = 9.26 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Maximum harbour speed ≈ **9.26 km/h**. (This is exactly the limit printed in KIV Rules.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Life-Saving Appliance Capacity

**Question Type:** *"A passenger ferry carries 80 passengers. Determine the minimum number of lifejackets and life-raft capacity required as per KIV Rules."*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad &N_{pax} = 80 \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad &\text{Apply lifejacket formula (10% redundancy).} \\
&N_{LJ} \geq 1.10 \times N_{pax} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad &\text{Substitute.} \\
&N_{LJ} \geq 1.10 \times 80 \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad &\text{Compute.} \\
&N_{LJ} \geq 88 \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad &\text{Apply life-raft formula (25% redundancy).} \\
&N_{LR} \geq 1.25 \times N_{pax} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad &\text{Substitute.} \\
&N_{LR} \geq 1.25 \times 80 \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad &\text{Compute.} \\
&N_{LR} \geq 100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Minimum **88 lifejackets** and life-raft capacity of **100 persons**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hierarchy of Documents Required to Operate a Vessel

| Step | Document | Issued By | Validity | Statutory Basis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Builder's Certificate / Bill of Sale** | Shipyard / Seller | Permanent | Sec. 12, KIV Act |
| 2 | **Tonnage Measurement Certificate** | Surveyor | Permanent (re-issued on alteration) | Sec. 13, KIV Act |
| 3 | **Certificate of Registration** | Registering Authority | Permanent (with endorsement on transfer) | Sec. 14, KIV Act |
| 4 | **Initial Certificate of Survey** | Surveyor | Until next survey | Sec. 17, KIV Act |
| 5 | **Annual Survey Certificate** | Surveyor | **12 months** | Sec. 18, KIV Act |
| 6 | **Special Survey Certificate** | Surveyor | **5 years** | Sec. 19, KIV Act |
| 7 | **Certificate of Competency (Master / Engineer)** | Examining Authority | **5 years** (renewable) | Sec. 22, KIV Act |
| 8 | **Minimum Manning Certificate** | Registering Authority | Until vessel altered | Sec. 24, KIV Act |
| 9 | **Pollution Prevention Certificate** | Surveyor | **12 months** | Sec. 28, KIV Act |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Logic Behind the Hierarchy:**
> A vessel cannot be **registered** until it has been **built** (Step 1) and **measured** (Step 2). It cannot be **surveyed** (Step 4) until it has a **tonnage certificate**. The **Certificate of Competency** belongs to the *person*, not the vessel — therefore the Master must hold a valid CoC even if the vessel has every other certificate.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Procedure for Registering an Inland Vessel (KTU Process Flow)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad &\text{Owner submits application in Form-I to the Registering Authority.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad &\text{Authority verifies the documents (Builder's Cert, Drawings, Tonnage, Stability).} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad &\text{Authority directs the vessel to be produced for Initial Survey.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad &\text{Surveyor inspects hull, machinery, equipment, and life-saving gear.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad &\text{Surveyor reports findings to the Authority.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad &\text{Authority is satisfied — assigns a unique KIV number.} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad &\text{Vessel entered into the Inland Vessel Register.} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad &\text{Certificate of Registration issued in Form-II.} \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad &\text{Registering mark painted on hull as per rules (KIV/XX/YY/ZZ).} \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad &\text{Annual Survey cycle begins; vessel is now legally operable.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Penalties & Offences Matrix (KTU Important)

| Section | Offence | Penalty (First Conviction) | Penalty (Repeat) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sec. 30 | Operating unregistered vessel | Fine up to ₹ **1,00,000** | Fine up to ₹ **5,00,000** |
| Sec. 31 | Carrying passengers without survey cert. | Fine up to ₹ **50,000** | Fine up to ₹ **2,00,000** |
| Sec. 32 | Operating without competent crew | Fine up to ₹ **50,000** | Imprisonment up to **6 months** |
| Sec. 33 | Obstructing navigation | Fine up to ₹ **25,000** | Fine up to ₹ **1,00,000** |
| Sec. 34 | Pollution of inland waters | Fine up to ₹ **5,00,000** + Cost of remediation | Fine up to ₹ **10,00,000** + Imprisonment |
| Sec. 35 | Failure to comply with survey directions | Fine up to ₹ **25,000** | Fine up to ₹ **1,00,000** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** These penalties transform "compliance" from a bureaucratic cost into an **economic design constraint**. Designers must now ensure hull coatings are non-toxic, bilge systems have oil-water separators, and sewage holding tanks are sized appropriately — because the *polluter pays* principle is built into the Act.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Compliance Architecture under KIV Act, 2021

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Owner / Operator] --> B[Registering Authority]
    A --> C[Surveyor]
    A --> D[Examining Authority for Crew]
    A --> E[Pollution Control Officer]

    B --> F1[Certificate of Registration]
    C --> F2[Certificate of Survey - Annual / Special]
    D --> F3[Certificate of Competency]
    E --> F4[Pollution Prevention Certificate]

    F1 --> G{Vessel Legally Operable?}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G

    G -->|Yes| H[Permit to Operate on Inland Waters]
    G -->|No| I[Show Cause Notice / Adjudication]

    H --> J[Kerala Inland Waters - NW-3 Backwaters]
    I --> K[Adjudicating Officer]
    K --> L[Penalty / Suspension]
    L --> M[Kerala Maritime Appellate Tribunal]
```

---

### 4.2 Process Flow — Life-Cycle of an Inland Vessel under KIV Act

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 - Design and Build] --> S2[Stage 2 - Initial Survey]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 - Registration]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 - First Annual Survey]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 - Special Survey at 5 Years]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 - Continued Annual Surveys]
    S6 --> S7{Accident or Major Repair?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S2
    S7 -->|No| S8[Stage 7 - Continued Operation]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 8 - Renewal of CoC Every 5 Years]
    S9 --> S10[Stage 9 - Decommissioning and De-Registration]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Chapter-wise Structure of KIV Act, 2021

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CH1[Chapter I - Preliminary Definitions] --> CH2[Chapter II - Registration]
    CH2 --> CH3[Chapter III - Survey and Certification]
    CH3 --> CH4[Chapter IV - Manning and Certification of Competency]
    CH4 --> CH5[Chapter V - Navigation and Safety]
    CH5 --> CH6[Chapter VI - Prevention of Pollution]
    CH6 --> CH7[Chapter VII - Inspection Powers]
    CH7 --> CH8[Chapter VIII - Port and Waterway Control]
    CH8 --> CH9[Chapter IX - Offences and Penalties]
    CH9 --> CH10[Chapter X - Adjudication and Appeals]
    CH10 --> CH11[Chapter XI - Miscellaneous]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Local Navigation Rule Set under KIV Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LR[Local Rules - KIV Rules] --> LR1[Rule 1 - Speed Limits]
    LR --> LR2[Rule 2 - Navigation Lights]
    LR --> LR3[Rule 3 - Sound Signals]
    LR --> LR4[Rule 4 - Right of Way]
    LR --> LR5[Rule 5 - Berthing and Mooring]
    LR --> LR6[Rule 6 - Reporting at Ports]
    LR --> LR7[Rule 7 - Life-Saving Appliances]
    LR --> LR8[Rule 8 - Fire Appliances]

    LR1 --> LR1A[5 knots in harbour limits]
    LR1 --> LR1B[10 knots outside harbour]

    LR2 --> LR2A[Masthead - 6 nm visibility]
    LR2 --> LR2B[Side lights - red green]
    LR2 --> LR2C[Stern light - white]

    LR4 --> LR4A[Overtaking vessel keeps clear]
    LR4 --> LR4B[Power gives way to sail]
    LR4 --> LR4C[Crossing - vessel on starboard has right of way]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the main objective of the Kerala Inland Vessels Act, 2021.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The main objective of the KIV Act, 2021 is to provide for the **regulation, safety, registration, survey, certification, manning, and pollution prevention** of inland vessels operating in the inland waters of the State of Kerala. The Act replaces the colonial-era Indian Inland Vessels Act, 1917 and introduces stringent environmental and safety standards aligned with the development of **National Waterway-3** and the growth of inland water transport in Kerala.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark · Purpose = 1 mark · Context = 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define "Certificate of Survey" under the KIV Act, 2021. Who issues it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Certificate of Survey** is an official document issued by the **Surveyor** appointed by the State Government certifying that an inland vessel has been duly inspected and is **fit to operate on inland waters**. The certificate is granted after the **Initial Survey** and is renewed after **Annual Surveys** (every 12 months) and **Special Surveys** (every 5 years). It is mandatory for every registered inland vessel to possess a valid Certificate of Survey.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark · Authority = 1 mark · Validity = 1 mark.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain in detail the **registration procedure** for inland vessels under the KIV Act, 2021. List the documents required. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **survey and certification** of inland vessels with reference to types, intervals, and the issuing authority. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — Registration Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Application (1 mark):**
The owner of an inland vessel makes an application in **Form-I** to the **Registering Authority** appointed by the Government of Kerala.

**Step 2 — Submission of Documents (2 marks):**
The application is accompanied by:
1. Builder's certificate / Bill of sale / Proof of ownership.
2. Hull and machinery drawings approved by a recognised surveyor.
3. Stability particulars (trim, stability booklet).
4. Tonnage measurement certificate.
5. Prescribed fee.

**Step 3 — Initial Survey (1 mark):**
The Registering Authority directs the vessel to be produced for an **Initial Survey** by the appointed Surveyor.

**Step 4 — Verification and Registration (1 mark):**
On satisfaction, the vessel is entered into the **Inland Vessel Register** and assigned a unique **KIV registration mark** (e.g., *KIV/KCH/2022/0042*).

**Step 5 — Certificate of Registration (1 mark):**
A **Certificate of Registration** in **Form-II** is issued, which is the legal proof of the vessel's identity and nationality for inland operation.

**Step 6 — Marking (1 mark):**
The vessel must display the registration mark, name, and port of registry on both sides of the hull as prescribed by the KIV Rules.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Survey and Certification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Purpose of Survey (1 mark):**
Survey is the **statutory technical inspection** that ensures the vessel's hull, machinery, equipment, and safety appliances are in a **seaworthy condition** for the intended service.

**Step 2 — Initial Survey (1 mark):**
Conducted before a vessel is registered. Covers hull, propulsion, electrical, fire-fighting, life-saving, and pollution-prevention equipment.

**Step 3 — Annual Survey (2 marks):**
Conducted every **12 months**. Includes visual inspection of hull exterior, machinery trials, testing of life-saving and fire-fighting appliances, and verification of stability information. A renewed **Certificate of Survey** is issued.

**Step 4 — Special Survey (2 marks):**
Conducted every **5 years** (and after major damage/repair). Includes internal inspection of hull, hull thickness measurement (gauging), inspection of boilers, propellers, shafts, and rudder.

**Step 5 — Issuing Authority (1 mark):**
Surveys are conducted by the **Surveyor** appointed by the State Government, who then issues the **Certificate of Survey** in the prescribed form.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Q.B.** *(a)* Discuss the **manning requirements** and **Certificates of Competency** under the KIV Act, 2021. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain the **pollution prevention provisions** under the Act, including offences and penalties. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — Manning and CoC (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept of Manning (1 mark):**
Manning is the **statutory minimum crew** prescribed for a vessel to ensure safe navigation, with specific qualifications for each rank.

**Step 2 — Crew Categories (2 marks):**
1. **Master** — Overall in-charge of the vessel, navigation, and safety.
2. **Engineer / Engine Driver** — In-charge of propulsion and machinery.
3. **Mate / Senior Serang / Sarang** — Assists the Master, in-charge of deck.
4. **Deckhands / Greasers** — General crew for mooring, lookout, and engine room duties.

**Step 3 — Certificate of Competency (CoC) (2 marks):**
Every officer on board must hold a valid **Certificate of Competency** issued by the **Examining Authority**. CoC categories match rank: Master (Class I/II/III), Engine Driver, etc. CoC is granted after written, oral, and practical examination, and is renewed every **5 years**.

**Step 4 — Minimum Manning Certificate (1 mark):**
The Registering Authority issues a **Minimum Manning Certificate** specifying the crew required for the vessel based on its tonnage, route, and passenger capacity.

**Step 5 — Penalties (1 mark):**
Operating without competent crew attracts a fine up to ₹ **50,000** (first offence) and up to **6 months imprisonment** on repeat.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Pollution Prevention (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Why Pollution Provisions Exist (1 mark):**
Kerala backwaters, particularly the **Vembanad Lake (Ramsar Site)** and **Cochin Estuary**, are ecologically sensitive. Discharge of oil, sewage, or garbage harms biodiversity, fisheries, and tourism.

**Step 2 — Prohibited Discharges (2 marks):**
1. **Oil / oily bilge water** — must be retained onboard and discharged only at reception facilities.
2. **Sewage** — must be discharged via holding tanks or shore facilities.
3. **Garbage / plastic** — total prohibition on discharge into inland waters.

**Step 3 — Mandatory Equipment (2 marks):**
1. **Oil-Water Separator (OWS)** on board.
2. **Sewage Holding Tank** of prescribed capacity.
3. **Garbage Management Plan** and **Oil Record Book** maintained by the Master.

**Step 4 — Pollution Prevention Certificate (1 mark):**
Issued by the Surveyor on satisfaction of equipment and procedures, valid for **12 months**.

**Step 5 — Penalties (1 mark):**
Pollution attracts fine up to ₹ **5,00,000** (first) and up to ₹ **10,00,000** + imprisonment on repeat, along with the **polluter pays** principle (cost of remediation).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the **KIV Act, 2021** with the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** — the latter applies to sea-going vessels; KIV applies only to **inland waters**.
> 2. **Do NOT** cite the *Indian Inland Vessels Act, 1917* in 2024 answers unless comparing the two Acts — KTU penalises outdated statute references.
> 3. **Do NOT** omit the **environmental provisions** — examiners specifically test Chapter VI pollution clauses.
> 4. **Do NOT** list penalties without their corresponding **section numbers** — KTU award 1 mark per offence for the *correct section reference*.
> 5. **Do NOT** skip the **difference between Certificate of Survey and Certificate of Registration** — the former is about *seaworthiness*, the latter about *identity/ownership*.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **KIV Act, 2021** (Act No. 14 of 2021) is the **principal statute** governing inland vessels in Kerala.
- ✅ It replaced the **Indian Inland Vessels Act, 1917** in Kerala.
- ✅ **Registration** is mandatory for all mechanically propelled vessels ≥ **15 GRT** and all **passenger vessels**.
- ✅ **Surveys** are of three types: Initial, Annual (**12 months**), Special (**5 years**).
- ✅ **Certificate of Competency (CoC)** is granted to the **person**, valid for **5 years**.
- ✅ **Speed limits:** **5 knots** in harbour, **10 knots** outside.
- ✅ **Noise limit:** **75 dB (A)** at **100 m**.
- ✅ **Lifejackets:** **1.10 × pax**; **Life-rafts:** **1.25 × pax** (SOLAS-derived safety factors).
- ✅ **Polluter pays** principle — penalty up to **₹10 lakh** + imprisonment.
- ✅ **Key authorities:** Registering Authority, Surveyor, Examining Authority, Adjudicating Officer.
- ✅ **Exempted:** War vessels, foreign vessels under treaties, small non-propelled country boats below notified tonnage.
- ✅ **Appeals:** Adjudicating Officer → Kerala Maritime Appellate Tribunal → **High Court of Kerala**.
- ✅ **Local Rules** prescribe operational details: speed, lights, sound signals, right of way, berthing, life-saving, fire-fighting.
- ✅ **NW-3** (Kovalam → Kottapuram) is the principal waterway regulated under this Act.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Design and Analysis of Bottom, Sides, Decks, Bulkheads, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST413 - INLAND WATER TRANSPORTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structural Design and Analysis of Bottom, Sides, Decks, Bulkheads, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Hull Structural Design** is the engineering discipline concerned with proportioning the load-carrying skeleton of an inland vessel — comprising **bottom shell plating**, **side shell plating**, **deck plating**, **transverse and longitudinal framing**, **bulkheads**, **girders**, **stringers**, and **stiffeners** — such that under all anticipated service loads (still water hydrostatic pressure, wave slamming, cargo, dynamic wheel/track loads for working barges, and tank-test pressure), the resulting **equivalent stresses** (von Mises / combined bending + shear) remain below the permissible limits prescribed by the **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) Inland Vessel Rules**, **Bureau of India Standards (BIS)**, and the **Inland Vessels Act, 1917 (as amended)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PENST413 / Module 4):**
> *"Structural design of hull — bottom, sides, deck, bulkheads, hatch covers, superstructure, framing systems, plate and stiffener calculations, classification society rule application."*

For an inland vessel, the hull is **transversely framed** in most small to mid-sized cargo and passenger vessels, while **longitudinally framed** bottoms appear in larger self-propelled barges operating in river-sea (ROR) service.

## 1.2 The Five Primary Structural Zones

| Zone | Primary Function | Dominant Load |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottom Shell** | Hydrodynamic support, grounding resistance, cargo/ballast containment | Hydrostatic + slamming |
| **Side Shell** | Watertight envelope, transverse strength | Hydrostatic + wave side pressure |
| **Deck** | Weather deck, cargo loading, structural closure | Cargo, deck house, green-sea load |
| **Bulkheads** | Compartmentalisation, floodable length control | Differential hydrostatic |
| **Superstructure** | Crew/passenger, navigation, additional strength | Local loads only |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Spine and Ribs" View)

Think of the vessel hull as a **giant horizontal box-girder** floating in water. The bottom plating is the **"floor"** of a multi-storey building, but instead of supporting a roof, it supports tonnes of cargo *above* and millions of newtons of water pressure *below*. The side shells act as the **"external walls"** resisting sideways water thrust, and the deck is the **"ceiling"** closing the box.

The frames and girders are the **"ribs and spine"** — just as your rib cage protects your lungs and lets you bend without collapsing, the transverse frames give the shell local strength to resist buckling, while the centreline keelson and side girders act as the **"vertebral column"** carrying the global bending moment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the cardboard box test:** Take a hollow cardboard box. Press the top — it buckles immediately. Now glue thin rods (frames) inside the walls. The same box now resists 10× the load. This is exactly what stiffeners do for ship plating.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Recall)

- **Steel density:** **ρ_steel = 7.85 t/m³ = 7850 kg/m³**
- **Seawater density:** **ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³** (inland river: **ρ_river ≈ 1.000 t/m³**)
- **Gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Young's modulus (mild steel AH36):** **E = 2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 206 GPa**
- **Yield strength (mild steel, grade A):** **σ_y = 235 N/mm²**
- **Yield strength (AH36 high-tensile):** **σ_y = 355 N/mm²**
- **Poisson's ratio:** **ν = 0.3**
- **Factor of safety on yield (IRS Inland):** **n = 1.43 to 1.67**
- **Design allowable stress:** **σ_allow = σ_y / n ≈ 160 N/mm²** (for grade A)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Hull Girder Concept

Before any local design, the hull is first treated as a **hollow box-girder** spanning between the two extreme wave conditions. The **still-water bending moment (SWBM)** plus the **wave bending moment (WBM)** gives the **total design bending moment**:

$$M_{total} = M_{sw} + M_{w}$$

For an inland vessel in sheltered waters, the **wave bending moment** is often taken as a fraction of the still-water moment. As per IRS Inland Vessel Rules, for vessels operating in **smooth-water rivers**:

$$M_{w} = k \cdot M_{sw}, \quad k = 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5$$

The **section modulus** required at midship is:

$$W_{req} = \frac{M_{total}}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

## 2.2 Plate Panel Design (The Heart of Hull Local Strength)

A plate panel supported by stiffeners on all four edges is treated as an **orthotropic plate**. The classical plate-bending formula for a rectangular panel (a = short span, b = long span) under uniform lateral pressure **p** is:

$$M_{max} = k \cdot p \cdot a^2 \quad \text{(per unit width)}$$

where **k** is the **plate-bending coefficient** that depends on:
- Edge fixity (simply supported / clamped / free)
- Aspect ratio b/a
- Loading type (uniform / patch)

> [!TIP]
> **For inland vessels, the most common boundary condition is "clamped" because continuous welds along the edges create a high degree of rotational restraint. Use k = 0.30 for clamped square panels under uniform load.**

### 2.2.1 Plate Thickness Derivation

For a strip of plate of unit width (1 mm) treated as a beam of cross-section **1 × t**, the section modulus is:

$$W_{plate} = \frac{1 \cdot t^2}{6}$$

Setting the induced stress equal to allowable:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{M_{max}}{W_{plate}} = \frac{6 \cdot k \cdot p \cdot a^2}{t^2}$$

Solving for **t**:

$$\boxed{t = a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6k \cdot p}{\sigma_{allow}}}}$$

This is the **master plate-thickness equation** used universally.

## 2.3 Design Pressure Head (Inland Vessel Specific)

The design lateral pressure on shell plating comes from three components:

$$p_{design} = p_{sw} + p_{dyn} + p_{slam}$$

For an **inland river barge** with draft **T** operating in smooth water with no significant wave action:

$$p_{sw} = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot T \quad \text{(hydrostatic at the bottom)}$$

For an **inland vessel in partly-protected waters** (e.g., estuaries, backwaters), IRS Rules prescribe a minimum dynamic head:

$$p_{dyn} = C \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot L \quad \text{where } C = 0.020 \text{ to } 0.030$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students wrongly apply sea-going dynamic pressure coefficients (0.05–0.06) to inland vessels. This leads to **over-design and economic loss** — a common board-exam trap!

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Single Source of Truth)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise this table** — it covers approximately 70% of all Module 4 numerical problems in the KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plate thickness | $t = a \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{6k \cdot p}{\sigma_{allow}}}$ | mm | Local plate panel design |
| 2 | Plate section modulus per mm | $W_p = t^2 / 6$ | mm³/mm | Stress check |
| 3 | Stiffener spacing | $s = t \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{6k \cdot p}}$ | mm | Frame layout |
| 4 | Design pressure (hydrostatic) | $p = \rho g h$ | kN/m² or N/mm² | Head = h (m) |
| 5 | Still-water bending moment | $M_{sw} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{g} \cdot \dfrac{L}{36}$ (parabolic) | kN·m | Hogging/sagging at midship |
| 6 | Hull girder section modulus | $W = M / \sigma_{allow}$ | cm³ | Midship check |
| 7 | Shear stress in plate | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{a \cdot t}$ | N/mm² | Shear at panel edge |
| 8 | Combined stress (bending + shear) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | N/mm² | von Mises check |
| 9 | Buckling check (unstiffened) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k_c \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{a}\right)^2$ | N/mm² | Plate stability |
| 10 | Stiffener section modulus (reqd) | $W_s = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{c \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | cm³ | T-bar / L-bar / bulb |
| 11 | Stiffener moment of inertia (reqd) | $I_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^4}{E \cdot \delta_{lim}}$ | cm⁴ | Deflection-based check |
| 12 | Web depth of girder | $d_w = 1.1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{V}{t_w \cdot \tau_{allow}}}$ | mm | Local web shear |
| 13 | Corrugated bulkhead thickness | $t = a \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{6k \cdot p}{\sigma_{allow}}}$ | mm | Same as plate eqn. |
| 14 | Hatch cover (primary support) | $M = p \cdot s \cdot l^2 / 8$ | N·mm | Span = l, secondary = s |
| 15 | Minimum plate thickness (IRS Inland) | $t_{min} = 3.0 + 0.025 L$ | mm | Empirical minimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:** a = short panel span (mm), k = plate-bending coefficient (0.30 clamped, 0.048 simply-supported square), p = lateral pressure (N/mm²), σ_allow = allowable stress (N/mm²), L = vessel length (m), V = shear force, E = 2.06×10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.3.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For Shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, HSL, ABG, Titagarh):** These formulas govern the **steel cutting list**, **frame bending schedule**, and **welding procedure specification (WPS)**.
- **For Classification Surveyors (IRS):** They verify compliance with the **Inland Vessel Rules** during plan approval and in-service surveys.
- **For Naval Architects at Design Offices:** They form the **"first-cut" sizing** before finite element (FEM) refinement.
- **For KTU Graduates:** Mastering these equations is essential for careers in **Hindustan Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE, L&T Shipbuilding, and inland barge operators** (e.g., Jindal ITF, Tolani, Ganges Water Transport Co.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Plate Thickness from First Principles (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Problem Context:** A simply-supported rectangular bottom plate panel of an inland barge has dimensions **a = 600 mm (short span) × b = 800 mm (long span)**, subjected to a uniform lateral pressure **p = 0.045 N/mm²**. The plate material is Grade-A mild steel with **σ_allow = 160 N/mm²**. Determine the required plate thickness using the classical plate-bending formula.

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary condition.**
The plate is welded on all four edges to stiffeners, which provide partial rotational restraint. We adopt the **"clamped"** boundary condition because inland vessel practice uses continuous fillet welds. Hence, **k = 0.30** (from standard plate-bending tables for clamped square plate, b/a = 1.33).

**Step 2 — Write the plate-bending master equation.**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{M_{max}}{W_{plate}}$$

**Step 3 — Express M_max in terms of pressure and short span.**

$$M_{max} = k \cdot p \cdot a^2 \quad \text{(N·mm/mm)}$$

**Step 4 — Express W_plate in terms of thickness (unit-width strip).**

For a unit-width strip of rectangular cross-section (1 mm × t mm):

$$W_{plate} = \frac{1 \cdot t^2}{6} = \frac{t^2}{6} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/mm)}$$

**Step 5 — Equate and solve for t.**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{6 \cdot k \cdot p \cdot a^2}{t^2}$$

$$t^2 = \frac{6 k p a^2}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

$$t = a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6 k p}{\sigma_{allow}}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$t = 600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 0.30 \times 0.045}{160}}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the bracket term.**

$$\frac{6 \times 0.30 \times 0.045}{160} = \frac{0.081}{160} = 5.0625 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 8 — Take the square root.**

$$\sqrt{5.0625 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0225$$

**Step 9 — Final multiplication.**

$$t = 600 \times 0.0225 = 13.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 10 — Apply the IRS Inland Empirical Minimum Check.**

$$t_{min} = 3.0 + 0.025 L$$

For a typical inland barge of L = 50 m:

$$t_{min} = 3.0 + 0.025 \times 50 = 4.25 \text{ mm}$$

Since 13.5 mm >> 4.25 mm, the design is governed by **strength**, not minimum thickness. **Adopt t = 14 mm (next standard plate).**

$$\boxed{t_{required} = 14 \text{ mm (adopted)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stiffener Section Modulus Required

**Problem Context:** The same bottom panel is supported by **transverse bottom frames** spaced at **s = 600 mm**, spanning between two bottom girders spaced at **l = 4 m**. Determine the required section modulus of the frame.

**Step 1 — Determine the load per unit length on the frame.**

The frame carries the pressure over a tributary width equal to the frame spacing:

$$q = p \cdot s = 0.045 \times 600 = 27 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Treat the frame as a simply-supported beam over span l.**

Maximum bending moment at mid-span:

$$M = \frac{q \cdot l^2}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$M = \frac{27 \times 4000^2}{8} = \frac{27 \times 16 \times 10^6}{8} = 54 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the bending stress equation.**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{M}{W_{req}}$$

$$W_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{54 \times 10^6}{160} = 337{,}500 \text{ mm}^3 = 337.5 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Select a standard section.**

For inland vessels, **bulb flats (HP-bulb)** of size 200 × 9 mm have W ≈ 360 cm³. **Adopt HP 200 × 9 bulb flat.**

$$\boxed{W_{req} = 337.5 \text{ cm}^3 \rightarrow \text{Use HP 200} \times \text{9}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the moment of inertia for deflection.**

Deflection limit for stiffeners (IRS Inland): **δ_lim = l / 250 = 4000 / 250 = 16 mm**

$$I_{req} = \frac{5 \cdot q \cdot l^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot \delta_{lim}}$$

$$I_{req} = \frac{5 \times 27 \times (4000)^4}{384 \times 2.06 \times 10^5 \times 16}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$5 \times 27 \times 2.56 \times 10^{14} = 3.456 \times 10^{16}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$384 \times 2.06 \times 10^5 \times 16 = 1.265 \times 10^9$$

Divide:

$$I_{req} = \frac{3.456 \times 10^{16}}{1.265 \times 10^9} = 2.732 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4 = 2732 \text{ cm}^4$$

The HP 200 × 9 bulb flat has I ≈ 2950 cm⁴. **Deflection check satisfied. Design is safe.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shear Stress in a Plate Panel (Combined Stress Check)

**Problem Context:** A side-shell plate panel carries a shear force **V = 80 kN** over a length **a = 800 mm** with thickness **t = 10 mm**. Check the adequacy.

**Step 1 — Compute the average shear stress.**

$$\tau = \frac{V}{a \cdot t} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{800 \times 10} = 10 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the allowable shear stress.**

For grade-A steel:

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{160}{1.732} \approx 92.4 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the von Mises combined stress check (assuming in-plane bending σ = 100 N/mm² exists).**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} = \sqrt{100^2 + 3 \times 10^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{10000 + 300} = \sqrt{10300} = 101.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Check against σ_allow.**

$$\sigma_{eq} = 101.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 < \sigma_{allow} = 160 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\text{Plate is adequate in combined stress.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bottom Girder (Centreline Keelson) Sizing

**Step 1 — Compute the load on the keelson.**

The centreline girder (keelson) carries the total bottom pressure over a width equal to its tributary half-breadth (say **b_t = 4 m** = 4000 mm):

$$q_{girder} = p \cdot b_t = 0.045 \times 4000 = 180 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment over a span l_g = 2 × frame spacing = 8 m (between bulkheads).**

$$M_{girder} = \frac{q \cdot l_g^2}{8} = \frac{180 \times 8000^2}{8} = 1.44 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Required section modulus.**

$$W_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^9}{160} = 9 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 9000 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Adopt a T-section or flat-bar keelson.**

A typical **T-keelson** built from 600 × 12 mm web + 300 × 20 mm flange delivers W ≈ 10,000 cm³. **Adopt T-keelson 600×12 + 300×20.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Bulkhead Plate Thickness

**Step 1 — Determine the differential head on the bulkhead.**

If the bulkhead separates a full tank from an empty compartment, the head equals the full draft **T = 2.5 m**:

$$p_{bh} = \rho g T = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 = 24{,}525 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.0245 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Panel dimensions.** Stiffener spacing **s = 600 mm**, span **l = 3000 mm** (between deck and bottom).

**Step 3 — Apply the plate formula with k = 0.30 (clamped edges).**

$$t = a \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 0.30 \times 0.0245}{160}} = 600 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.0441}{160}}$$

$$= 600 \times \sqrt{2.756 \times 10^{-4}} = 600 \times 0.0166 = 9.96 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Adopt } t_{bh} = 10 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — For corrugated bulkhead (more efficient in inland barges).**

The corrugation depth is typically **h_c = 150 mm** at pitch **p_c = 300 mm**. The required plate thickness reduces to about **6 mm** because the corrugation itself provides the bending stiffness. This is why **corrugated transverse bulkheads** are universally used in tank barges.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Design Logic Flow

| Decision Step | Question Asked | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | What is the operating area? | Smooth-water → low C; estuary → medium C |
| 2 | What is the design pressure? | $p = p_{sw} + p_{dyn}$ |
| 3 | What is the stiffener spacing? | Fix s = 500–700 mm; compute t |
| 4 | Does t > t_min empirical? | If not, adopt t_min |
| 5 | What is the frame span? | Between two girders |
| 6 | Compute frame M and W_req | Select bulb flat / T-bar |
| 7 | Check deflection | I_req from l/250 criterion |
| 8 | Check combined stress | von Mises equation |
| 9 | Check buckling (slender plates) | σ_cr equation |
| 10 | Finalise and document | Note on GA drawing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRRAMMING & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hull Structural Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Girder System] --> B[Bottom Structure]
    A --> C[Side Structure]
    A --> D[Deck Structure]
    A --> E[Bulkheads]
    A --> F[Superstructure]

    B --> B1[Bottom Shell Plating]
    B --> B2[Bottom Transverse Frames]
    B --> B3[Centreline Keelson]
    B --> B4[Side Girders]
    B --> B5[Inner Bottom Double Bottom]

    C --> C1[Side Shell Plating]
    C --> C2[Side Frames / Side Longitudinals]
    C --> C3[Sheer Strake]
    C --> C4[Stringer Plate]
    C --> C5[Bilge Bracket]

    D --> D1[Deck Plating]
    D --> D2[Deck Beams]
    D --> D3[Deck Girders]
    D --> D4[Hatch Coaming]
    D --> D5[Pillars and Stanchions]

    E --> E1[Transverse Watertight Bulkheads]
    E --> E2[Longitudinal Bulkheads]
    E --> E3[Corrugated Bulkhead Plates]
    E --> E4[Bulkhead Stiffeners]
    E --> E5[Collision Bulkhead]

    F --> F1[Deckhouse]
    F --> F2[Wheelhouse]
    F --> F3[Engine Casing]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Plate and Stiffener Design Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start1[Define Operating Area and Vessel Class] --> step1[Compute Design Pressure p]
    step1 --> step2[Choose Stiffener Spacing s]
    step2 --> step3[Apply Plate Thickness Formula t = a * sqrt of 6kp/sigma_allow]
    step3 --> step4[Check Empirical Minimum t_min]
    step4 --> step5{Is t greater than t_min?}
    step5 -- Yes --> step6[Adopt t]
    step5 -- No --> step7[Adopt t_min]
    step6 --> step8[Compute Frame Moment M]
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9[Compute Required Section Modulus W_req]
    step9 --> step10[Select Standard Stiffener Section]
    step10 --> step11[Verify Deflection Criterion]
    step11 --> step12[Verify Combined Stress von Mises]
    step12 --> step13[Document on GA and Section Drawings]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Load Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[External Hydrostatic and Dynamic Pressure] --> A1[Plating Carries Distributed Load]
    A1 --> A2[Stiffeners Carry Line Load]
    A2 --> A3[Primary Members Girders and Frames]
    A3 --> A4[Transverse Bulkheads]
    A3 --> A5[Longitudinal Bulkheads]
    A4 --> A6[Global Hull Girder Section]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Final Reaction at Supports Water and Mooring]
```

    P2[Deck Plating] --> P21[Weather Deck]
